100_Marsh_Wren.jpg.title: 100 Marsh Wren
100_Marsh_Wren.jpg.description: Plate 100 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Marsh Wren.
100_Marsh_Wren.jpg.story: The Marsh Wren is a pugnacious little sprite of the cattails, weaving globular nests among reeds and belting a rattling song far out of proportion to its size. Audubon shows the bird clinging vertically to stems in its natural element, a composition typical of his habit of placing small passerines inside the tangled geometry of their own habitat.

101_Raven.jpg.title: 101 Raven
101_Raven.jpg.description: Plate 101 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Raven.
101_Raven.jpg.story: Ravens were common across eastern North America in Audubon's day, though they've since retreated to wilder country. He paints a single bird at life size, its heavy bill and shaggy throat hackles rendered in the glossy blue-black that Havell's aquatint process could suggest through layered ink washes before the colorists went to work.

102_Blue_Jay.jpg.title: 102 Blue Jay
102_Blue_Jay.jpg.description: Plate 102 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue Jay.
102_Blue_Jay.jpg.story: Three Blue Jays raid a nest for eggs, yolk running down one bird's bill — a small domestic horror typical of Audubon's willingness to show birds doing what birds actually do. Jays are notorious nest robbers, and the plate pairs their dazzling cobalt plumage with behavior most sentimental bird books of the period preferred to leave out.

103_Canada_Warbler.jpg.title: 103 Canada Warbler
103_Canada_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 103 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Canada Warbler.
103_Canada_Warbler.jpg.story: A necklace of black spots across a lemon breast makes the Canada Warbler unmistakable in any thicket. Audubon places the bird among foliage in the restless posture of a foraging wood-warbler, tail cocked, head tilted to spot insects on the undersides of leaves.

104_Chipping_Sparrow.jpg.title: 104 Chipping Sparrow
104_Chipping_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 104 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Chipping Sparrow.
104_Chipping_Sparrow.jpg.story: The Chipping Sparrow, with its rusty cap and dry insect-trill of a song, is a dooryard bird that nests in hedgerows and young conifers. Audubon gives it a modest, accurate portrait — a reminder that Birds of America devotes as much attention to the familiar as to the exotic.

105_Red-breasted_Nuthatch.jpg.title: 105 Red-breasted Nuthatch
105_Red-breasted_Nuthatch.jpg.description: Plate 105 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-breasted Nuthatch.
105_Red-breasted_Nuthatch.jpg.story: Red-breasted Nuthatches move along trunks head-downward, probing bark crevices with that slightly upturned bill. Audubon catches the species in this signature inverted posture, pairing the bird's black eyestripe and cinnamon underparts against a conifer branch — the northern spruce-fir forests they favor.

106_Black_Vulture_or_Carrion_Crow.jpg.title: 106 Black Vulture or Carrion Crow
106_Black_Vulture_or_Carrion_Crow.jpg.description: Plate 106 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black Vulture or Carrion Crow.

source.canonical_title: File:106 Black Vulture or Carrion Crow.jpg

106_Black_Vulture_or_Carrion_Crow.jpg.story: Black Vultures gather at a deer carcass, bald heads and hooked bills at the grim work of disarticulation. Audubon, who wrote a combative paper disputing whether vultures find food by sight or smell, had strong views about these birds and painted them with the unsparing attention of someone who'd spent hours watching them feed.

107_Canada_Jay.jpg.title: 107 Canada Jay
107_Canada_Jay.jpg.description: Plate 107 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Canada Jay.
107_Canada_Jay.jpg.story: Now called the Gray Jay or Canada Jay, this fluffy boreal corvid is famous for tameness around northern camps — it will steal a sandwich off your knee. Audubon shows a pair among conifer branches, their soft pale plumage well-suited to the snowbound forests they inhabit year-round.

108_Fox-coloured_Sparrow.jpg.title: 108 Fox-coloured Sparrow
108_Fox-coloured_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 108 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Fox-coloured Sparrow.
108_Fox-coloured_Sparrow.jpg.story: The Fox Sparrow is a big, russet-streaked ground-scratcher that kicks backwards through leaf litter with both feet at once. Audubon's birds are rendered in the rich foxy reds that give the species its name — the eastern population he would have known from winter thickets.

109_Savannah_Finch.jpg.title: 109 Savannah Finch
109_Savannah_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 109 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Savannah Finch.
109_Savannah_Finch.jpg.story: Now the Savannah Sparrow, this streaky grassland bird was named for Savannah, Georgia, where Alexander Wilson first described it. Audubon places the species among low vegetation, capturing the yellow lores that are the best field mark when the bird isn't singing its thin, insect-like buzz.

10_Brown_Titlark.jpg.title: 10 Brown Titlark
10_Brown_Titlark.jpg.description: Plate 10 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Brown Titlark (now American Pipit).
10_Brown_Titlark.jpg.story: Audubon's Brown Titlark is the American Pipit, a slim, tail-bobbing ground bird that breeds on alpine and arctic tundra and winters in open country down south. The plate shows the species in its understated streaky brown plumage, a bird most people overlook until it flushes underfoot.

110_Hooded_Warbler.jpg.title: 110 Hooded Warbler
110_Hooded_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 110 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hooded Warbler.
110_Hooded_Warbler.jpg.story: Male Hooded Warblers sport a brilliant yellow face set into a jet-black hood — one of the most striking patterns in the wood-warbler family. Audubon's plate captures that graphic contrast and the species' habit of flicking its white outer tail feathers while foraging low in southern hardwood understory.

111_Pileated_Woodpecker.jpg.title: 111 Pileated Woodpecker
111_Pileated_Woodpecker.jpg.description: Plate 111 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pileated Woodpecker.
111_Pileated_Woodpecker.jpg.story: Four Pileated Woodpeckers crowd a single plate in one of Audubon's more crowded family tableaus, males and females chiseling at a wild grape vine. It's a large, theatrical composition built to show off the species' crow-sized body and flaming red crest at true life size — the kind of bird that forced the double-elephant folio to exist in the first place.

112_Downy_Woodpecker.jpg.title: 112 Downy Woodpecker
112_Downy_Woodpecker.jpg.description: Plate 112 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Downy Woodpecker.
112_Downy_Woodpecker.jpg.story: The Downy is the smallest North American woodpecker, a friendly black-and-white bird happy to visit suet feeders. Audubon pairs several individuals on a branch, showing the male's red nape spot and the subtle size difference that separates Downy from its near-twin, the Hairy Woodpecker.

113_Blue-bird.jpg.title: 113 Blue-bird
113_Blue-bird.jpg.description: Plate 113 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Eastern Bluebird Sialia sialis .
113_Blue-bird.jpg.story: Three Eastern Bluebirds perch on a mullein stalk, the male's lapis back and rusty breast almost glowing against the plate's pale ground. The species was a familiar fence-post bird across Audubon's America — cavity-nesting, insect-eating, and beloved long before it became a conservation cause.

114_White-crowned_Sparrow.jpg.title: 114 White-crowned Sparrow
114_White-crowned_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 114 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-crowned Sparrow.
114_White-crowned_Sparrow.jpg.story: The White-crowned Sparrow wears crisp black-and-white head stripes that make it one of the more dapper members of its genus. Audubon's plate shows the bird in winter habitat — it breeds in the subarctic and passes through much of the country on migration, singing a clear whistled song that travels far on cold mornings.

115_Wood_Pewee.jpg.title: 115 Wood Pewee
115_Wood_Pewee.jpg.description: Plate 115 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wood Pewee. The flower is identified as Azalea biscosa , maybe what's now called Rhododendron viscosum .
115_Wood_Pewee.jpg.story: The Eastern Wood-Pewee sits upright on a bare twig, sallying out for insects and returning to the same perch — Audubon catches it among the pale blossoms of a swamp azalea (Rhododendron viscosum). The bird's plaintive PEE-a-wee whistle is one of the signature sounds of an eastern summer woodlot.

116_Ferruginous_Thrush.jpg.title: 116 Ferruginous Thrush
116_Ferruginous_Thrush.jpg.description: Plate 116 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ferruginous Thrush.
116_Ferruginous_Thrush.jpg.story: Now called the Brown Thrasher, this long-tailed mimic pours out a twice-repeated medley from exposed perches in spring. Audubon's plate is one of his dramatic tableaus — four thrashers defending a nest from a black snake coiled among the branches, a scene of pure nineteenth-century naturalist theater.

117_Mississippi_Kite.jpg.title: 117 Mississippi Kite
117_Mississippi_Kite.jpg.description: Plate 117 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Mississippi Kite.
117_Mississippi_Kite.jpg.story: Mississippi Kites are elegant aerial hunters, slate-gray birds that snatch dragonflies and cicadas on the wing over southern woodlands. Audubon's plate catches the predator-prey dynamic he loved to depict, showing the kite in possession of a small snake — a classic piece of Birds of America drama.

118_Warbling_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 118 Warbling Flycatcher
118_Warbling_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 118 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Warbling Flycatcher.
118_Warbling_Flycatcher.jpg.story: What Audubon called the Warbling Flycatcher is now the Warbling Vireo, a plain olive bird with a burbling, run-on song delivered from the tops of streamside cottonwoods. The plate is modest, fitting a species whose virtues are vocal rather than visual.

119_Yellow-throated_Vireo.jpg.title: 119 Yellow-throated Vireo
119_Yellow-throated_Vireo.jpg.description: Plate 119 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-throated Vireo.
119_Yellow-throated_Vireo.jpg.story: The Yellow-throated Vireo wears bright yellow spectacles and throat, a splash of color in the leafy canopy where it sings its slow, burry phrases. Audubon's plate places the bird among foliage in the deliberate, methodical posture typical of vireos hunting caterpillars.

11_Bird_of_Washington.jpg.title: 11 Bird of Washington
11_Bird_of_Washington.jpg.description: Plate 11 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bird of Washington. Originally claimed by Audubon to be a different, significantly bigger, already very rare species, most subsequent ornithologists generally considered it was a misidentified juvenile Bald Eagle Haliaeetus leucocephalus . However, recent detailed investigation (Halley, M. R. 2020, Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club 140 (2): 110-141 ) has shown it to be a case of scientific fraud, based not on a specimen as Audubon claimed, but by copying older drawings of other smaller eagles. His motive is thought to be to raise funds for and interest in his forthcoming Birds of America book.
11_Bird_of_Washington.jpg.story: Audubon's 'Bird of Washington' was long considered a misidentified immature Bald Eagle, but a 2020 study by Matthew Halley in the Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club argued the plate was outright fabrication — assembled from older drawings of smaller eagles to drum up interest in the forthcoming book. Whatever its origin, it's the most notorious plate in the folio.

120_Pewit_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 120 Pewit Flycatcher
120_Pewit_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 120 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pewit Flycatcher.
120_Pewit_Flycatcher.jpg.story: The Pewit Flycatcher is the Eastern Phoebe, the tail-wagging little tyrant who builds mud nests under bridges and barn eaves. Audubon has a personal claim here: in 1804, as a young man at Mill Grove, he tied silver threads to phoebe nestlings and confirmed they returned the next year — the first known bird-banding experiment in North America.

121_Snowy_Owl.jpg.title: 121 Snowy Owl
121_Snowy_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 121 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Snowy Owl.
121_Snowy_Owl.jpg.story: Two Snowy Owls face opposite directions on a rocky ledge — one white male, one heavily barred female — making the plate a study in sexual dimorphism as much as portraiture. The birds are irruptive visitors to the northern U.S. in winters when arctic lemming populations crash, and Audubon would have seen them most often along the Great Lakes.

122_Blue_Grosbeak.jpg.title: 122 Blue Grosbeak
122_Blue_Grosbeak.jpg.description: Plate 122 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue Grosbeak.
122_Blue_Grosbeak.jpg.story: The male Blue Grosbeak is a deep ultramarine with chestnut wingbars and a disproportionately massive bill built for cracking seeds and beetles. Audubon shows the species in a southern brushy setting, where the bird's slow, musical warble carries across old fields on hot afternoons.

123_Black_&_Yellow_Warblers.jpg.title: 123 Black & Yellow Warblers
123_Black_&_Yellow_Warblers.jpg.description: Plate 123 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black & Yellow Warblers.
123_Black_&_Yellow_Warblers.jpg.story: The 'Black & Yellow Warbler' is the Magnolia Warbler, a gaudy boreal songbird with a black mask, yellow throat, and bold black streaks down its flanks. Audubon's composition pairs a couple of these birds among foliage, a straightforward foliage-gleaner study.

124_Green_Black-capt_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 124 Green Black-capt Flycatcher
124_Green_Black-capt_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 124 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Green Black-capt Flycatcher.
124_Green_Black-capt_Flycatcher.jpg.story: This is the Wilson's Warbler, a tiny olive bird wearing a neat black yarmulke atop a lemon-yellow face. Alexander Wilson got the common name and Audubon painted it here under an older flycatcher label — taxonomy was still sorting itself out in the 1820s.

125_Brown-headed_Nuthatch.jpg.title: 125 Brown-headed Nuthatch
125_Brown-headed_Nuthatch.jpg.description: Plate 125 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Brown-headed Nuthatch.
125_Brown-headed_Nuthatch.jpg.story: Brown-headed Nuthatches are pine-forest specialists of the southeastern U.S., famous for using bark flakes as tools to pry up other bark — one of the few tool-using songbirds. Audubon's plate shows them working a pine trunk in their characteristic acrobatic fashion, smaller and squeakier than their northern cousins.

126_White-headed_Eagle.jpg.title: 126 White-headed Eagle
126_White-headed_Eagle.jpg.description: Plate 126 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-headed Eagle.
126_White-headed_Eagle.jpg.story: The Bald Eagle stands over a freshly killed catfish, wings mantled, the great yellow bill half open. It's one of the folio's marquee plates — a national symbol rendered at full double-elephant scale, which for an eagle means truly enormous, and Havell's aquatint brings out every separate feather of the shaggy leg tarsus.

127_Rose-breasted_Grosbeak.jpg.title: 127 Rose-breasted Grosbeak
127_Rose-breasted_Grosbeak.jpg.description: Plate 127 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Rose-breasted Grosbeak.
127_Rose-breasted_Grosbeak.jpg.story: A male Rose-breasted Grosbeak in full breeding plumage is almost theatrical — black and white with a deep pink triangle spilled down the breast as if the bird had cut itself. Audubon pairs male and female, and the female's streaky sparrow-like plumage shows just how different the sexes can be in this family.

128_Cat_Bird.jpg.title: 128 Cat Bird
128_Cat_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 128 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Cat Bird.
128_Cat_Bird.jpg.story: The Gray Catbird is a slate-gray mimid with a rusty undertail and a mewing call that gives it its name. Audubon shows the bird in thick cover, the habitat where it actually lives — catbirds skulk through hedgerows pouring out long, improvised songs full of borrowed phrases and harsh notes.

129_Great_Crested_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 129 Great Crested Flycatcher
129_Great_Crested_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 129 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Crested Flycatcher.
129_Great_Crested_Flycatcher.jpg.story: Great Crested Flycatchers are cavity nesters with an odd habit of weaving shed snakeskin into their nest linings. Audubon catches one on a branch showing off its cinnamon tail and gray-and-lemon underparts, a large tyrant flycatcher whose loud WHEEP call cuts through any eastern hardwood forest in summer.

12_Baltimore_Oriole.jpg.title: 12 Baltimore Oriole
12_Baltimore_Oriole.jpg.description: Plate 12 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Baltimore Oriole.
12_Baltimore_Oriole.jpg.story: Baltimore Orioles hang pendulous woven nests from the tips of elm and sycamore branches, and Audubon's plate shows the full architecture — a pouch suspended among leaves, with adults tending it. The male's burnt-orange and black plumage was named for the livery of Lord Baltimore, a piece of colonial heraldry preserved in a warbler's common name.

130_Yellow-winged_Sparrow.jpg.title: 130 Yellow-winged Sparrow
130_Yellow-winged_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 130 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-winged Sparrow.
130_Yellow-winged_Sparrow.jpg.story: This is the Grasshopper Sparrow, a shy, buzzy-voiced grassland bird easier to hear than see. Audubon's plate captures the flat-headed, short-tailed silhouette and the subtle yellow wing edge that gives the species its older name — a good field mark if you can flush one onto an exposed perch.

131_American_Robin.jpg.title: 131 American Robin
131_American_Robin.jpg.description: Plate 131 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Robin.
131_American_Robin.jpg.story: American Robins are so ubiquitous they become invisible, and Audubon plates them generously — multiple birds on a fruiting branch, the composition built around the red breast and the yellow bill. Havell's aquatint keeps the breast color from going crude, which was always a danger when hand-colorists reached for the carmine.

132_Three-toed_Woodpecker.jpg.title: 132 Three-toed Woodpecker
132_Three-toed_Woodpecker.jpg.description: Plate 132 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Three-toed Woodpecker.
132_Three-toed_Woodpecker.jpg.story: The Three-toed Woodpecker is a boreal specialist that peels bark from beetle-infested spruces, often concentrating in recent burns. Audubon's plate shows the male's yellow forehead patch — the field mark separating it from the similar Black-backed Woodpecker, another northern pyrophile.

133_Black-poll_Warbler.jpg.title: 133 Black-poll Warbler
133_Black-poll_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 133 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-poll Warbler.
133_Black-poll_Warbler.jpg.story: Blackpoll Warblers make one of the most astonishing migrations of any songbird, flying nonstop from the northeastern coast to South America over open Atlantic. Audubon's plate shows a breeding male with its black cap and white cheek — a bird most birders now know from its thin, high-frequency song at the edge of hearing.

134_Hemlock_Warbler.jpg.title: 134 Hemlock Warbler
134_Hemlock_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 134 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hemlock Warbler.
134_Hemlock_Warbler.jpg.story: The 'Hemlock Warbler' is now recognized as an immature plumage of the Yellow-rumped Warbler — one of several Audubon and Wilson-era names that taxonomy later folded together. The plate is still a fine study of a small wood-warbler in conifer branches.

135_Blackburnian_Warbler.jpg.title: 135 Blackburnian Warbler
135_Blackburnian_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 135 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blackburnian Warbler.
135_Blackburnian_Warbler.jpg.story: The male Blackburnian Warbler has a throat like a lit match — a searing orange that no other North American songbird can match. Audubon's plate does its best with the color, though the species is one of those cases where the printed pigment can only gesture at the real thing glowing in a hemlock top at dawn.

136_Meadow_Lark.jpg.title: 136 Meadow Lark
136_Meadow_Lark.jpg.description: Plate 136 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Meadow Lark.
136_Meadow_Lark.jpg.story: Eastern Meadowlarks are open-country birds with a bright yellow breast crossed by a black V, more closely related to blackbirds than to true larks. Audubon shows the species in a prairie-edge setting, singing its clear slurred whistle from a fencepost — the sound of American grassland before the plow.

137_Yellow-breasted_Chat.jpg.title: 137 Yellow-breasted Chat
137_Yellow-breasted_Chat.jpg.description: Plate 137 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-breasted Chat.
137_Yellow-breasted_Chat.jpg.story: The Yellow-breasted Chat is the misfit of the warbler family — larger, skulkier, and given to a bizarre vocal repertoire of whistles, cackles, and chuckles delivered from deep within brambles. Audubon's plate shows the chat in dense thicket cover, where it actually lives and is almost impossible to see.

138_Connecticut_Warbler.jpg.title: 138 Connecticut Warbler
138_Connecticut_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 138 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Connecticut Warbler.
138_Connecticut_Warbler.jpg.story: The Connecticut Warbler is a notoriously shy boreal bog-nester that walks rather than hops through undergrowth — an oddity among wood-warblers. Audubon knew it mainly as a migrant, and the plate shows the gray hood and complete white eye-ring that separate it from the similar Mourning Warbler.

139_Field_Sparrow.jpg.title: 139 Field Sparrow
139_Field_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 139 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Field Sparrow.
139_Field_Sparrow.jpg.story: Field Sparrows sing a series of sweet clear whistles that accelerate into a bouncing-ball trill — one of the most distinctive songs of old-field country. Audubon's plate shows the pink bill and plain face of this small, gentle sparrow among the briars it nests in.

13_Snow_Bird.jpg.title: 13 Snow Bird
13_Snow_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 13 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Snow Bird.
13_Snow_Bird.jpg.story: The 'Snow Bird' is the Dark-eyed Junco, the slate-gray winter feeder bird that flashes white outer tail feathers as it flushes. Audubon shows the species on the ground, which is where juncos spend most of their time — scratching through snow for weed seeds in noisy little flocks.

140_Pine_Creeping_Warbler.jpg.title: 140 Pine Creeping Warbler
140_Pine_Creeping_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 140 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pine Creeping Warbler.
140_Pine_Creeping_Warbler.jpg.story: The Pine Warbler lives up to its name, creeping along pine boughs to glean insects and even taking seeds from cones in winter — unusual for a wood-warbler. Audubon's plate places the bird in its native conifer, one of the more habitat-faithful warblers in the group.

141_I._Goshawk_-_2._Stanley_Hawk.jpg.title: 141 I. Goshawk - 2. Stanley Hawk
141_I._Goshawk_-_2._Stanley_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 141 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Goshawk - . Stanley Hawk.
141_I._Goshawk_-_2._Stanley_Hawk.jpg.story: Two accipiters share this plate — a Northern Goshawk and what Audubon called the Stanley Hawk, now recognized as a Cooper's Hawk. Both are bird-hunting forest specialists with short wings and long rudder-like tails, and pairing them on a single sheet invites the comparison Audubon's readers would have wanted to make.

142_American_Sparrow_Hawk.jpg.title: 142 American Sparrow Hawk
142_American_Sparrow_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 142 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Sparrow Hawk.
142_American_Sparrow_Hawk.jpg.story: The American Kestrel — Audubon's 'Sparrow Hawk' — is the smallest and most colorful falcon in North America, the male slate-blue-winged with a rusty back and mustached face. The plate shows the birds with prey, a characteristic Audubon touch that doubles as a guide to diet.

143_Golden-crowned_Thrush.jpg.title: 143 Golden-crowned Thrush
143_Golden-crowned_Thrush.jpg.description: Plate 143 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Golden-crowned Thrush.
143_Golden-crowned_Thrush.jpg.story: This is the Ovenbird, a ground-walking wood-warbler that builds a domed Dutch-oven nest on the forest floor. Audubon's plate catches the orange-crowned head stripe and streaky white breast of a bird most people know only from its ringing teacher-TEACHER-TEACHER song in deciduous woods.

144_Small_Green_Crested_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 144 Small Green Crested Flycatcher
144_Small_Green_Crested_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 144 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Small Green Crested Flycatcher.
144_Small_Green_Crested_Flycatcher.jpg.story: The Acadian Flycatcher is an Empidonax — one of those olive-green tyrants separated from its cousins chiefly by voice and habitat. Audubon shows the species in the shaded understory of a southern bottomland hardwood forest, which is exactly where the Acadian's explosive pit-SEE call still rings today.

145_Yellow_Red-poll_Warbler.jpg.title: 145 Yellow Red-poll Warbler
145_Yellow_Red-poll_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 145 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow Red-poll Warbler.
145_Yellow_Red-poll_Warbler.jpg.story: The 'Yellow Red-poll' is the Palm Warbler, a tail-wagging wood-warbler that breeds in northern bogs and winters across the southeastern U.S. Audubon's bird shows the rusty cap and yellow underparts of the breeding plumage, a little warbler almost always found near the ground.

146_Fish_Crow.jpg.title: 146 Fish Crow
146_Fish_Crow.jpg.description: Plate 146 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Fish Crow.
146_Fish_Crow.jpg.story: Fish Crows are smaller than American Crows and give a nasal, two-note uh-uh call — the best way to tell the two species apart. Audubon's plate keeps it simple: a black corvid on a branch, with the subtler proportions that a careful eye can pick out from its more abundant cousin.

147_Night_Hawk.jpg.title: 147 Night Hawk
147_Night_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 147 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Night Hawk.
147_Night_Hawk.jpg.story: The Common Nighthawk isn't a hawk at all but a nightjar, cruising high over open country at dusk on sharply pointed wings with white wrist patches. Audubon's plate shows the species in the spread-wing pose that displays those flashing patches — the best field mark when the birds are silhouettes against a summer sky.

148_Pine_Swamp_Warbler.jpg.title: 148 Pine Swamp Warbler
148_Pine_Swamp_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 148 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pine Swamp Warbler.
148_Pine_Swamp_Warbler.jpg.story: The Pine Swamp Warbler is the Black-throated Blue Warbler in female or immature plumage — another of those early-nineteenth-century names later folded into a single species. The plate reflects the confusing variety of plumages that made warbler taxonomy such a puzzle before field guides existed.

149_Sharp-tailed_Finch.jpg.title: 149 Sharp-tailed Finch
149_Sharp-tailed_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 149 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Sharp-tailed Finch.
149_Sharp-tailed_Finch.jpg.story: Nelson's and Saltmarsh Sparrows — both once called Sharp-tailed Finches — are secretive salt-marsh birds with streaky buff-orange faces. Audubon's plate shows the species in its low grassy habitat, where the birds nest within inches of the highest tides and the females raise young alone.

14_Prairie_Warbler.jpg.title: 14 Prairie Warbler
14_Prairie_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 14 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Prairie Warbler.
14_Prairie_Warbler.jpg.story: Prairie Warblers wag their tails constantly and sing a rising chromatic buzz that climbs the scale. Despite the name they don't live on prairies but in scrubby old fields and pine barrens, and Audubon's plate catches the yellow face with its distinctive black streaks below the eye.

150_Red-eyed_Vireo.jpg.title: 150 Red-eyed Vireo
150_Red-eyed_Vireo.jpg.description: Plate 150 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-eyed Vireo.
150_Red-eyed_Vireo.jpg.story: Red-eyed Vireos sing all day, every day, through the eastern hardwood summer — short burry phrases repeated thousands of times. Audubon's plate shows the white eyebrow and dark eye-line that, along with the olive back, mark this most abundant of northeastern forest songbirds.

151_Turkey_Buzzard.jpg.title: 151 Turkey Buzzard
151_Turkey_Buzzard.jpg.description: Plate 151 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Turkey Buzzard.
151_Turkey_Buzzard.jpg.story: The Turkey Vulture is the master thermal-rider of open country, wings held in a characteristic dihedral V as it teeters on the updrafts. Audubon was opinionated about vultures — he argued publicly that they hunt by sight, not smell, and lost that argument, though not for lack of observation.

152_White-breasted_Black-capped_Nuthatch.title: 152 White-breasted Black-capped Nuthatch
152_White-breasted_Black-capped_Nuthatch.description: Plate 152 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-breasted Black-capped Nuthatch.
152_White-breasted_Black-capped_Nuthatch.story: The White-breasted Nuthatch is the chunky, nasal-voiced bark-creeper of eastern deciduous forests, a familiar feeder visitor year-round. Audubon's plate shows the species in its typical head-down posture on a trunk, sooty cap meeting clean white face in a sharp horizontal line — a bird whose yank-yank call is one of the steadiest winter sounds in any hardwood woodlot.

153_Yellow-crown_Warbler.jpg.title: 153 Yellow-crown Warbler
153_Yellow-crown_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 153 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-crown Warbler.
153_Yellow-crown_Warbler.jpg.story: The 'Yellow-crown Warbler' is the breeding-plumage Yellow-rumped Warbler of the eastern Myrtle subspecies — yet another casualty of taxonomic lumping. Audubon's plate shows the crown patch and yellow flanks that made this seem like a distinct species when warbler identification was still largely a matter of collecting specimens.

154_Tennessee_Warbler.jpg.title: 154 Tennessee Warbler
154_Tennessee_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 154 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tennessee Warbler.
154_Tennessee_Warbler.jpg.story: Tennessee Warblers pass through Tennessee on migration but breed in the boreal forest far to the north, where they specialize on spruce budworm outbreaks — their populations boom and crash with the caterpillars. Audubon's plate shows the plain gray-and-olive plumage of a bird mostly identified by its staccato three-part song.

155_Black-throated_Blue_Warbler.jpg.title: 155 Black-throated Blue Warbler
155_Black-throated_Blue_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 155 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-throated Blue Warbler.
155_Black-throated_Blue_Warbler.jpg.story: Male Black-throated Blue Warblers are simply and perfectly drawn: deep blue above, black throat and sides, white belly, with a small white square on the wing. Females look so different they were once thought a separate species, and Audubon probably painted both under different names across his folio.

156_American_Crow.jpg.title: 156 American Crow
156_American_Crow.jpg.description: Plate 156 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Crow.
156_American_Crow.jpg.story: American Crows are so familiar that making a life-sized portrait of one is an artistic challenge — there's nowhere for exoticism to hide. Audubon gives the bird a clean profile on a branch, relying on Havell's aquatint gradations to keep the black plumage from becoming a flat silhouette.

157_Rusty_Grakle.jpg.title: 157 Rusty Grakle
157_Rusty_Grakle.jpg.description: Plate 157 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Rusty Grakle.
157_Rusty_Grakle.jpg.story: Rusty Blackbirds are boreal wetland specialists that winter in southern swamps, and their populations have crashed dramatically since Audubon's time. The plate shows the subtle rusty feather edges of the non-breeding plumage that gives the bird its name — a harbinger of fall in the eastern hardwoods.

158_American_Swift.jpg.title: 158 American Swift
158_American_Swift.jpg.description: Plate 158 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Swift.
158_American_Swift.jpg.story: The Chimney Swift is a cigar with wings, spending nearly its entire life airborne and roosting by clinging vertically inside hollow trees or, since European settlement, masonry chimneys. Audubon's plate is unusual in showing the species inside a hollow sycamore — a glimpse of pre-chimney roosting habitat now mostly vanished.

159_Cardinal_Grosbeak.jpg.title: 159 Cardinal Grosbeak
159_Cardinal_Grosbeak.jpg.description: Plate 159 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Cardinal Grosbeak.
159_Cardinal_Grosbeak.jpg.story: Northern Cardinals need no introduction: the crested red male and warm buff female are fixtures of eastern yards and feeders. Audubon pairs them on a plate with a flowering branch, one of the more frankly decorative compositions in the folio because the bird itself is decoration.

15_Blue_Yellow-backed_Warbler.jpg.title: 15 Blue Yellow-backed Warbler
15_Blue_Yellow-backed_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 15 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue Yellow-backed Warbler.
15_Blue_Yellow-backed_Warbler.jpg.story: The Northern Parula is a tiny wood-warbler with a blue-gray hood, yellow throat, and chestnut breast-band, breeding in forests draped with Spanish moss in the south and Usnea lichen in the north — it nests in both. Audubon's plate places the birds in a flowering branch, true to the species' habit of foraging in the canopy.

160_Black-capped_Titmouse.jpg.title: 160 Black-capped Titmouse
160_Black-capped_Titmouse.jpg.description: Plate 160 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-capped Titmouse.
160_Black-capped_Titmouse.jpg.story: The 'Black-capped Titmouse' is the Black-capped Chickadee, the cheerful little bandit of northern woodlots with its hoarse chicka-dee-dee-dee. Audubon's plate shows the black cap and bib against the white cheek — a bird small enough to perch on a human finger when baited with sunflower seeds.

161_Brasilian_Caracara_Eagle.jpg.title: 161 Brasilian Caracara Eagle
161_Brasilian_Caracara_Eagle.jpg.description: Plate 161 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Brasilian Caracara Eagle.
161_Brasilian_Caracara_Eagle.jpg.story: The Crested Caracara is a long-legged, ground-walking falcon of open country from Florida and Texas south through the tropics — an oddball that eats carrion and hunts on foot. Audubon painted the species from specimens obtained in Florida, and the plate shows the orange face and capped crest that give the bird a distinctly reptilian intensity.

162_Zenaida_Dove.jpg.title: 162 Zenaida Dove
162_Zenaida_Dove.jpg.description: Plate 162 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Zenaida Dove.
162_Zenaida_Dove.jpg.story: Zenaida Doves are Caribbean cousins of the Mourning Dove, and in Audubon's time they reached southern Florida — they're now rare visitors there at best. Audubon named the genus after his wife, Lucy (née Zenaide Labatut, in one version of the story), making this plate a quiet domestic tribute tucked into the folio.

163_Palm_Warbler.jpg.title: 163 Palm Warbler
163_Palm_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 163 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Palm Warbler.
163_Palm_Warbler.jpg.story: Palm Warblers pump their tails constantly, a useful field mark since their plumage is otherwise a modest wash of yellow and rust. Audubon shows the species among palmetto and other southern vegetation, reflecting the bird's winter range in the southeastern U.S.

164_Tawny_Thrush.jpg.title: 164 Tawny Thrush
164_Tawny_Thrush.jpg.description: Plate 164 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tawny Thrush.
164_Tawny_Thrush.jpg.story: The Tawny Thrush is the Veery, a softly patterned woodland thrush with a downward-spiraling flute-song that seems to echo inside the bird itself. Audubon's plate shows the uniformly warm brown upperparts and only faintly spotted breast that separate Veery from its more heavily marked thrush cousins.

165_Bachmans_Finch.jpg.title: 165 Bachmans Finch
165_Bachmans_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 165 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bachmans Finch.
165_Bachmans_Finch.jpg.story: Bachman's Sparrow is a secretive pine-barrens specialist named for Audubon's close friend and collaborator John Bachman of Charleston. The bird sings a long clear whistle followed by a trill, delivered from low perches in fire-maintained longleaf pine — a habitat, and a species, now much rarer than in Audubon's time.

166_Rough-legged_Falcon.jpg.title: 166 Rough-legged Falcon
166_Rough-legged_Falcon.jpg.description: Plate 166 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Rough-legged Falcon.
166_Rough-legged_Falcon.jpg.story: The Rough-legged Hawk is a large buteo of the arctic tundra that winters down into the northern U.S., where it hunts voles from exposed perches and low hovering flight. Audubon's plate shows the feathered tarsi that give the species its name — an adaptation for cold that few other North American raptors share.

167_Key-west_Dove.jpg.title: 167 Key-west Dove
167_Key-west_Dove.jpg.description: Plate 167 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Key-west Dove.
167_Key-west_Dove.jpg.story: The Key West Quail-Dove is a Caribbean ground dove that Audubon encountered on his Florida Keys expedition in 1832, one of the few times the species has been recorded in the U.S. The plate is a record of a genuine rarity, painted from a bird shot during that trip.

168_Fork-tailed_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 168 Fork-tailed Flycatcher
168_Fork-tailed_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 168 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Fork-tailed Flycatcher ( Muscicapa savana ) = Fork-tailed Flycatcher ( Tyrannus savana )
168_Fork-tailed_Flycatcher.jpg.story: The Fork-tailed Flycatcher is a tropical tyrant with ribbon-like tail streamers twice its body length, showing up in North America only as a rare vagrant. Audubon's plate depicts one of these stray birds — the kind of ornithological oddity that turns up a few times a century on the Atlantic coast.

169_Mangrove_Cuckoo.jpg.title: 169 Mangrove Cuckoo
169_Mangrove_Cuckoo.jpg.description: Plate 169 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Mangrove Cuckoo ( Coccyzus seniculus ) = Mangrove Cuckoo ( Coccyzus minor ).
169_Mangrove_Cuckoo.jpg.story: Mangrove Cuckoos are specialists of Caribbean mangrove and hardwood hammock, reaching the U.S. only in south Florida. Audubon's plate places the bird among the foliage of its namesake habitat — a slinking, long-tailed cuckoo with a black ear-patch and buff belly, usually detected by its guttural calls.

16_Great-footed_Hawk.jpg.title: 16 Great-footed Hawk
16_Great-footed_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 16 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great-footed Hawk.
16_Great-footed_Hawk.jpg.story: The Peregrine Falcon — Audubon's 'Great-footed Hawk' — is the fastest animal on Earth in its stoop. The plate shows a pair on a bluff with a freshly killed duck, rendered with Audubon's characteristic eye for the predator's anatomy: the notched tomial tooth on the bill, the massive yellow feet, the black helmet of the head.

170_Gray_Tyrant.jpg.title: 170 Gray Tyrant
170_Gray_Tyrant.jpg.description: Plate 170 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Gray Tyrant.
170_Gray_Tyrant.jpg.story: The Gray Kingbird is a pale, big-headed tyrant flycatcher of the Caribbean and extreme southeastern U.S. coast. Audubon would have known the species from Florida, where it perches prominently on open branches to sally for insects — a tropical counterpart to the Eastern Kingbird.

171_Barn_Owl.jpg.title: 171 Barn Owl
171_Barn_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 171 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Barn Owl.
171_Barn_Owl.jpg.story: Barn Owls have heart-shaped faces that function as acoustic dishes, letting them catch rodents in total darkness by sound alone. Audubon's plate shows a pair with prey, rendering the pale cinnamon-and-white plumage that makes a Barn Owl in flight look like a drifting ghost.

172_Blue-headed_Pigeon.jpg.title: 172 Blue-headed Pigeon
172_Blue-headed_Pigeon.jpg.description: Plate 172 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue-headed Pigeon.
172_Blue-headed_Pigeon.jpg.story: The Blue-headed Quail-Dove is a Cuban endemic Audubon would have known from specimens traded up from the Caribbean. The plate is less a record of an American bird than an aspirational inclusion — a sign of how porous the concept of 'Birds of America' was when the Gulf of Mexico counted as a neighborhood.

173_Barn_Swallow.jpg.title: 173 Barn Swallow
173_Barn_Swallow.jpg.description: Plate 173 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Barn Swallow.
173_Barn_Swallow.jpg.story: Barn Swallows are the deep-forked-tail swallows of open country worldwide, nesting under bridges and inside barns on cup-shaped mud structures. Audubon's plate captures the iridescent steel-blue back and rusty throat — a species whose shift from cave nesting to human structures is one of the great quiet adaptations of the colonial era.

174_Olive_sided_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 174 Olive sided Flycatcher
174_Olive_sided_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 174 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Olive sided Flycatcher.
174_Olive_sided_Flycatcher.jpg.story: Olive-sided Flycatchers perch conspicuously atop dead snags and whistle a three-note quick-THREE-BEERS that carries across boreal burns and bogs. Audubon's plate shows the vested look of dark flanks meeting a white central breast — a big-headed tyrant whose populations are now in steep decline.

175_Nuttall's_lesser-marsh_Wren.jpg.title: 175 Nuttall's lesser-marsh Wren
175_Nuttall's_lesser-marsh_Wren.jpg.description: Plate 175 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Nuttall's lesser-marsh Wren.
175_Nuttall's_lesser-marsh_Wren.jpg.story: This is the Sedge Wren, named in Audubon's plate for Thomas Nuttall, the English-American naturalist whose Manual of Ornithology was the standard field reference of the 1830s. The species skulks in wet meadows and damp sedges, a smaller and more secretive cousin of the Marsh Wren.

176_Spotted_Grous.jpg.title: 176 Spotted Grous
176_Spotted_Grous.jpg.description: Plate 176 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Spotted Grous.
176_Spotted_Grous.jpg.story: The Spruce Grouse is a boreal forest bird famous for its tameness — early travelers called it the 'fool hen' because it refused to flush. Audubon's plate shows the male's red eye-comb and black throat, a species still found across the North American conifer belt and still often willing to be approached.

177_White-crowned_Pigeon.jpg.title: 177 White-crowned Pigeon
177_White-crowned_Pigeon.jpg.description: Plate 177 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-crowned Pigeon.
177_White-crowned_Pigeon.jpg.story: White-crowned Pigeons are fruit-eating Caribbean pigeons that reach the U.S. only in the Florida Keys, where they feed on poisonwood and other hammock fruits. Audubon met them during his 1832 Florida trip, and the plate's heavy slate bird with its clean white cap is a memento of that southern excursion.

178_Orange-crowned_Warbler.jpg.title: 178 Orange-crowned Warbler
178_Orange-crowned_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 178 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Orange-crowned Warbler.
178_Orange-crowned_Warbler.jpg.story: Orange-crowned Warblers are plain olive birds with a crown patch you can almost never actually see — it's only revealed when the bird is excited or handled. Audubon's plate shows the species honestly: drab, with the faint yellow undertail coverts and broken eye-ring that are the real field marks.

179_Wood_Wren.jpg.title: 179 Wood Wren
179_Wood_Wren.jpg.description: Plate 179 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wood Wren.
179_Wood_Wren.jpg.story: The 'Wood Wren' is the Winter Wren, a tiny dark wren of cool forests whose song is an astonishing ten-second cascade of tinkling notes. Audubon's plate shows the stub-tailed, cocked posture of a bird small enough to disappear into a mossy log and usually heard long before it's seen.

17_Carolina_Turtle_Dove.jpg.title: 17 Carolina Turtle Dove
17_Carolina_Turtle_Dove.jpg.description: Plate 17 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Carolina Turtle Dove.
17_Carolina_Turtle_Dove.jpg.story: The Carolina Turtle Dove is the Mourning Dove, and Audubon's plate is one of his tenderer family compositions — a pair perched on a branch, male attending female, with the slightly melancholy soft-gray plumage that suits the species' mournful cooing call.

180_Pine_Finch.jpg.title: 180 Pine Finch
180_Pine_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 180 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pine Finch.
180_Pine_Finch.jpg.story: The Pine Siskin is a streaky little finch with yellow flashes in its wings, nomadic across the northern conifer belt and prone to irruptive invasions south when cone crops fail. Audubon's plate shows the species in its preferred pines, where the birds dangle upside-down to extract seeds.

181_Goldon_Eagle.jpg.title: 181 Goldon Eagle
181_Goldon_Eagle.jpg.description: Plate 181 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Goldon Eagle.
181_Goldon_Eagle.jpg.story: A Golden Eagle carries a limp Northern Hare skyward, talons sunk deep — one of the folio's pure-drama plates. Audubon claimed he bought the eagle live from a trapper and then struggled for days over how to kill and paint it, a backstory recounted in his Ornithological Biography that's become nearly as famous as the image itself.

182_Ground_Dove.jpg.title: 182 Ground Dove
182_Ground_Dove.jpg.description: Plate 182 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ground Dove.
182_Ground_Dove.jpg.story: Common Ground Doves are tiny sparrow-sized doves of the southern U.S., walking the ground in pairs on pink legs with soft scaly breasts. Audubon's plate shows the species in open sandy habitat, the rufous wing patches flashing when the birds flush.

183_Goldon-crested-Wren.jpg.title: 183 Goldon-crested-Wren
183_Goldon-crested-Wren.jpg.description: Plate 183 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Goldon-crested-Wren.
183_Goldon-crested-Wren.jpg.story: The 'Golden-crested Wren' is the Golden-crowned Kinglet, a bird smaller than many warblers and endlessly active among spruce needles. Audubon's plate catches the flame-colored crown stripe that lights up when the male displays — a tiny conflagration in the tops of the northern conifer belt.

184_Mangrove_Humming_Bird.jpg.title: 184 Mangrove Humming Bird
184_Mangrove_Humming_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 184 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Mangrove Humming Bird.
184_Mangrove_Humming_Bird.jpg.story: The 'Mangrove Humming Bird' appears to have been based on a Caribbean specimen and its identity remains debated — possibly a Bahama Woodstar or a confused drawing of another tropical hummer. The plate is one of the folio's stranger entries, a reminder that Audubon sometimes worked from traded skins whose provenance was vague.

185_Bachman's_Warbler.jpg.title: 185 Bachman's Warbler
185_Bachman's_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 185 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bachman's Warbler.
185_Bachman's_Warbler.jpg.story: Bachman's Warbler was discovered by John Bachman in South Carolina canebrakes in 1832 and is now almost certainly extinct, the last confirmed sightings in the 1960s. Audubon's plate is therefore doubly valuable — a record of a species we can no longer see in the flesh, painted from specimens by a friend's hand.

186_Pinnated_Grous.jpg.title: 186 Pinnated Grous
186_Pinnated_Grous.jpg.description: Plate 186 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pinnated Grous.
186_Pinnated_Grous.jpg.story: Greater Prairie-Chickens boom at dawn on traditional leks, males inflating orange throat sacs and stamping their feet. Audubon's plate captures the pinnae — the neck feathers raised into horn-like tufts during display — and the bird's theatrical posture at the height of breeding performance.

187_Boat-tailed_Grackle.jpg.title: 187 Boat-tailed Grackle
187_Boat-tailed_Grackle.jpg.description: Plate 187 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Boat-tailed Grackle.
187_Boat-tailed_Grackle.jpg.story: Boat-tailed Grackles are southern coastal birds with keeled tails males fold into a deep trough in flight. Audubon's plate shows the long-tailed silhouette of the male and the smaller tawny female, a sexual dimorphism much more extreme than in the Common Grackle.

188_Tree_Sparrow.jpg.title: 188 Tree Sparrow
188_Tree_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 188 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tree Sparrow.
188_Tree_Sparrow.jpg.story: American Tree Sparrows are winter visitors to the northern U.S. from arctic breeding grounds, recognized by the rusty cap, dark stickpin breast spot, and two-toned bill. Audubon's plate shows the species in a weedy winter setting — they almost never perch in actual trees despite the name.

189_Snow_Bunting.jpg.title: 189 Snow Bunting
189_Snow_Bunting.jpg.description: Plate 189 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Snow Bunting.
189_Snow_Bunting.jpg.story: Snow Buntings are arctic breeders that winter in flocks across northern fields, flashing black-and-white wings as they wheel over stubble like a snowstorm given agency. Audubon's plate shows the more heavily marked winter plumage, since the clean white-and-black breeding male is rarely seen south of the tundra.

18_Bewick's_Wren.jpg.title: 18 Bewick's Wren
18_Bewick's_Wren.jpg.description: Plate 18 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bewick's Wren.
18_Bewick's_Wren.jpg.story: Bewick's Wren was named for English engraver Thomas Bewick, whose bird illustrations influenced a generation of naturalists. Audubon's plate shows the species' long white eyebrow and long tail edged with white — a wren now largely absent from its former eastern range, though still common in the west.

190_Yellow_bellied_Woodpecker.jpg.title: 190 Yellow bellied Woodpecker
190_Yellow_bellied_Woodpecker.jpg.description: Plate 190 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow bellied Woodpecker.
190_Yellow_bellied_Woodpecker.jpg.story: The Yellow-bellied Sapsucker drills neat horizontal rows of sap wells and licks up both the sap and the insects it attracts. Audubon's plate shows the red forehead and throat of a male and the subtle yellow wash on the belly that most observers have to take on faith — it's easier to see on a plate than in the field.

191_Willow_Grous_or_Large_Ptarmigan.jpg.title: 191 Willow Grous or Large Ptarmigan
191_Willow_Grous_or_Large_Ptarmigan.jpg.description: Plate 191 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Willow Grous or Large Ptarmigan.

source.canonical_title: File:191 Willow Grous or Large Ptarmigan.jpg

191_Willow_Grous_or_Large_Ptarmigan.jpg.story: Willow Ptarmigan turn pure white in winter to match the arctic snow and molt back to cryptic brown for summer — one of the most dramatic plumage transitions of any bird. Audubon's plate shows the species in transitional dress, a hint of how the seasonal change looks mid-molt.

192_Great_American_Shrike_or_Butcher_Bir.title: 192 Great American Shrike or Butcher Bird
192_Great_American_Shrike_or_Butcher_Bir.description: Plate 192 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great American Shrike or Butcher Bird.

source.canonical_title: File:192 Great American Shrike or Butcher Bird.jpg

192_Great_American_Shrike_or_Butcher_Bir.story: Northern Shrikes are songbirds with the hearts of raptors, impaling prey on thorns to store and dismember it — hence the butcher bird name. Audubon's plate shows this arctic-breeding predator with a small bird in its grip, the hooked bill and black mask advertising intent.

193_Lincoln_Finch.jpg.title: 193 Lincoln Finch
193_Lincoln_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 193 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Lincoln Finch.
193_Lincoln_Finch.jpg.story: Lincoln's Sparrow is a shy streaked sparrow of boggy edges, named by Audubon for young Thomas Lincoln of Dennysville, Maine, who collected the type specimen on their 1833 Labrador expedition. The plate commemorates a collaborator-turned-friend on one of Audubon's most ambitious field trips.

194_Canadian_Titmouse.jpg.title: 194 Canadian Titmouse
194_Canadian_Titmouse.jpg.description: Plate 194 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Canadian Titmouse.
194_Canadian_Titmouse.jpg.story: The Boreal Chickadee is the chickadee of the northern conifer belt, replacing its Black-capped cousin in spruce forests across Canada and into the northeastern mountains. Audubon's plate shows the brown cap and cinnamon flanks that distinguish this quieter, wheezier-voiced cousin.

195_Ruby_crowned_Wren.jpg.title: 195 Ruby crowned Wren
195_Ruby_crowned_Wren.jpg.description: Plate 195 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ruby crowned Wren.
195_Ruby_crowned_Wren.jpg.story: The Ruby-crowned Kinglet is a hyperactive tiny bird that flicks its wings constantly and flashes a crimson crown patch only when excited. Audubon's plate catches the bird mid-motion among foliage — a species whose song is astonishingly loud for a bird that weighs less than a nickel.

196_Labrador_Falcon.jpg.title: 196 Labrador Falcon
196_Labrador_Falcon.jpg.description: Plate 196 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Labrador Falcon.
196_Labrador_Falcon.jpg.story: The 'Labrador Falcon' is a dark color morph of the Gyrfalcon, the largest falcon in the world and an arctic specialist that hunts ptarmigan and seabirds across the circumpolar north. Audubon would have seen these birds during his 1833 Labrador expedition, and the plate records one of the dark extremes of the species' variable plumage.

197_American_Crossbill.jpg.title: 197 American Crossbill
197_American_Crossbill.jpg.description: Plate 197 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Crossbill.
197_American_Crossbill.jpg.story: Red Crossbills use their oddly crossed mandibles to pry apart conifer cone scales — a specialization so refined that North American birds are split into multiple call types keyed to different tree species. Audubon's plate shows the brick-red male and olive-yellow female working a cone together.

198_Brown_headed_Worm_eating_Warbler.jpg.title: 198 Brown headed Worm eating Warbler
198_Brown_headed_Worm_eating_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 198 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Brown headed Worm eating Warbler.

source.canonical_title: File:198 Brown headed Worm eating Warbler.jpg

198_Brown_headed_Worm_eating_Warbler.jpg.story: The Swainson's Warbler is a plain brown-headed wood-warbler of southern canebrakes and hardwood swamps, one of the most secretive species in its family. Audubon's plate, under an older name, captures a bird named by John Bachman for the English ornithologist William Swainson.

199_Little_Owl.jpg.title: 199 Little Owl
199_Little_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 199 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Little Owl.
199_Little_Owl.jpg.story: The Burrowing Owl stands long-legged on the ground outside a prairie-dog burrow, its yellow eyes startlingly direct. Audubon's plate — labeled 'Little Owl' — shows an owl unlike any of its tree-dwelling cousins: diurnal, terrestrial, and equally at home in western grasslands and Florida scrub.

19_Louisiana_Water_Thrush.jpg.title: 19 Louisiana Water Thrush
19_Louisiana_Water_Thrush.jpg.description: Plate 19 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Louisiana Water Thrush.
19_Louisiana_Water_Thrush.jpg.story: The Louisiana Waterthrush is a tail-bobbing wood-warbler of clear woodland streams, walking the banks like a miniature sandpiper while singing a ringing downslurred song. Audubon's plate places the bird in the streamside vegetation where it nests, tucked under an overhanging root ball.

1_Wild_Turkey.jpg.title: 1 Wild Turkey
1_Wild_Turkey.jpg.description: Plate 1 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wild Turkey. Notes: Engraved from painting made by John James Audubon at Beechwoods Plantation, Feliciana Parish (now West Feliciana Parish), Louisiana 1825.
1_Wild_Turkey.jpg.story: A male turkey, wary and magnificent, strides through Louisiana cane in the plate Audubon chose to open his great work. He painted the bird at Beechwoods Plantation in 1825, and Robert Havell Jr.'s London workshop reproduced it at full life size, a feat only the double-elephant folio could contain.

200_Shore_Lark.jpg.title: 200 Shore Lark
200_Shore_Lark.jpg.description: Plate 200 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Shore Lark.
200_Shore_Lark.jpg.story: The Shore Lark, now called Horned Lark, is a ground-runner of bare fields and tundra that walks rather than hops and sings its tinkling flight-song high above the frozen barrens. Audubon studied the species during his Labrador expedition of 1833, the voyage that filled out much of his later seabird and shorebird coverage.

201_Canada_Goose.jpg.title: 201 Canada Goose
201_Canada_Goose.jpg.description: Plate 201 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Canada Goose.
201_Canada_Goose.jpg.story: Canada Geese mate for life and guide their broods in those famous V-shaped skeins, bugling through the dusk over every flyway on the continent. To render them at the true four-foot wingspan, Havell's engravers pressed each sheet against a copper plate the size of a tabletop, the hallmark of the double-elephant folio.

202_Red-Throated_Diver.jpg.title: 202 Red-Throated Diver
202_Red-Throated_Diver.jpg.description: Plate 202 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-Throated Diver.
202_Red-Throated_Diver.jpg.story: Red-throated Divers breed on tiny tundra ponds yet fly out to the sea to fish, the only loon able to lift straight off the water without a running start. Audubon drew many northern waterfowl from specimens collected during his 1833 Labrador voyage, working in a cramped cabin aboard the schooner Ripley.

203_Fresh_Water_Marsh_Hen.jpg.title: 203 Fresh Water Marsh Hen
203_Fresh_Water_Marsh_Hen.jpg.description: Plate 203 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Fresh Water Marsh Hen.
203_Fresh_Water_Marsh_Hen.jpg.story: Known today as the King Rail, this skulker of cattail swamps is far more often heard than seen, its grunting duet rolling out at dusk. Audubon's painted birds were translated to copper in London by Robert Havell Jr., whose aquatint ground gave the reed and water their velvety tonal depth.

204_Salt_Water_Marsh_Hen.jpg.title: 204 Salt Water Marsh Hen
204_Salt_Water_Marsh_Hen.jpg.description: Plate 204 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Salt Water Marsh Hen.
204_Salt_Water_Marsh_Hen.jpg.story: The Clapper Rail rattles through tidal cordgrass on the ebb, picking fiddler crabs from the mud as the water falls. Audubon often hunted rails from a skiff poled through the spartina, a method he described in the accompanying Ornithological Biography that was published alongside the prints.

205_Virginia_Rail.jpg.title: 205 Virginia Rail
205_Virginia_Rail.jpg.description: Plate 205 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Virginia Rail.
205_Virginia_Rail.jpg.story: A slim russet rail that runs marshes on long red legs, the Virginia Rail can compress its flexible vertebrae to slip between standing reeds. Each of Havell's aquatints was hand-colored by a team of London colorists working from a master proof that Audubon personally approved.

206_Summer_or_Wood_Duck.jpg.title: 206 Summer or Wood Duck
206_Summer_or_Wood_Duck.jpg.date_created: 1827-1838 (publication; from a painting made in Louisiana in 1821)
206_Summer_or_Wood_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 206 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Summer or Wood Duck.
206_Summer_or_Wood_Duck.jpg.story: Wood Ducks nest in tree cavities, and the downy ducklings leap from heights of fifty feet on their first morning to follow the hen to water. Audubon called them the most beautiful of our ducks, and their iridescent greens taxed the colorists who finished every Havell aquatint by hand in watercolor.

207_Booby_Gannet.jpg.title: 207 Booby Gannet
207_Booby_Gannet.jpg.description: Plate 207 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Booby Gannet.
207_Booby_Gannet.jpg.story: The Brown Booby plunges from the wing at tropical fish shoals, folding into an arrow at the instant of impact. Audubon encountered colonies on the Florida Keys during his 1832 trip aboard the revenue cutter Marion, sketching specimens fresh on deck before decay could dull the colors.

208_Esquimaux_Curlew.jpg.title: 208 Esquimaux Curlew
208_Esquimaux_Curlew.jpg.description: Plate 208 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Esquimaux Curlew.
208_Esquimaux_Curlew.jpg.story: Once one of the most abundant shorebirds of the Americas, the Eskimo Curlew migrated in vast wheeling flocks between the Arctic and the pampas before market gunners drove it to the edge of extinction. Audubon's plate is now a relic of a species almost certainly lost.

209_Wilson's_Plover.jpg.title: 209 Wilson's Plover
209_Wilson's_Plover.jpg.description: Plate 209 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wilson's Plover.
209_Wilson's_Plover.jpg.story: A stocky sand-colored plover of southern beaches, distinguished by a heavy black bill suited to cracking fiddler crabs. Audubon named many of his birds after friends and rivals in ornithology; this one honors Alexander Wilson, the Scottish-American whose earlier American Ornithology Audubon set out to surpass.

20_Blue-winged_Yellow_Warbler.jpg.title: 20 Blue-winged Yellow Warbler
20_Blue-winged_Yellow_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 20 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue-winged Yellow Warbler.
20_Blue-winged_Yellow_Warbler.jpg.story: The Blue-winged Warbler buzzes a two-note bee-buzz from brushy old fields, gleaning insects from leaf undersides with a sharp needle bill. Audubon often arranged his small songbirds on botanical specimens he himself collected, insisting the foliage be drawn from living plants rather than memory.

210_Least_Bittern.jpg.title: 210 Least Bittern
210_Least_Bittern.jpg.description: Plate 210 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Least Bittern.
210_Least_Bittern.jpg.story: No bigger than a robin, the Least Bittern freezes with bill skyward when startled, its streaked underparts blending perfectly with the vertical reeds. Audubon sometimes wired freshly shot specimens into lifelike postures on a gridded board, a field method he had refined since his Kentucky years.

211_Great_blue_Heron.jpg.title: 211 Great blue Heron
211_Great_blue_Heron.jpg.description: Plate 211 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great blue Heron.
211_Great_blue_Heron.jpg.story: The continent's largest heron stalks shallows with almost geological patience, then strikes with a bill like a javelin. At life size this bird required Havell's largest copper plates, and even so Audubon bent the long neck into an S to fit the sheet, a compositional trick he used for many tall waders.

212_Common_Gull.jpg.title: 212 Common Gull
212_Common_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 212 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Common Gull.
212_Common_Gull.jpg.story: The Mew Gull, smaller and gentler than its larger cousins, follows plows and wave-lines alike across the northern hemisphere. Havell's London workshop inherited the Birds of America project in 1827 after the first engraver, William Lizars of Edinburgh, lost his colorists to a strike.

213_Puffin.jpg.title: 213 Puffin
213_Puffin.jpg.description: Plate 213 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Puffin.
213_Puffin.jpg.story: Atlantic Puffins carry a dozen sand eels crosswise in that comical painted bill, their tongues pinning each fish as a new one is added. Audubon watched the colonies on Labrador's rocky stacks in 1833 and described burrow-digging parents hissing at any intruder who came too close.

214_Razor_Bill.jpg.title: 214 Razor Bill
214_Razor_Bill.jpg.description: Plate 214 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Razor Bill.
214_Razor_Bill.jpg.story: Razorbills nest on narrow ledges where a single pear-shaped egg rolls in a circle rather than falling to the sea. They are the closest living relatives of the extinct Great Auk, a kinship Audubon noted in the Ornithological Biography that accompanied his folios.

215_Hyperborean_phalarope.jpg.title: 215 Hyperborean phalarope
215_Hyperborean_phalarope.jpg.description: Plate 215 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hyperborean phalarope.
215_Hyperborean_phalarope.jpg.story: Red-necked Phalaropes spin tight circles on the water to stir up a vortex of plankton, then pluck prey from the upwelling. In this species the female is the brighter bird and leaves the male to incubate the eggs, a reversal Audubon reported with evident surprise.

216_Wood_Ibiss.jpg.title: 216 Wood Ibiss
216_Wood_Ibiss.jpg.description: Plate 216 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wood Ibiss.
216_Wood_Ibiss.jpg.story: The Wood Stork is America's only native stork, feeding by touch with its bill half open in murky water until a fish brushes the mandibles and triggers a snap faster than the human eye can track. Audubon met them in Florida's cypress sloughs, which he described as cathedrals of tangled green.

217_Louisiana_Heron.jpg.title: 217 Louisiana Heron
217_Louisiana_Heron.jpg.description: Plate 217 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Louisiana Heron.
217_Louisiana_Heron.jpg.story: Now called the Tricolored Heron, this slender slate-blue bird runs through tidal shallows with wings half-spread, startling minnows into its bill. Audubon considered it the most graceful of the herons, and Havell's aquatint caught the motion without smudging the delicate plume feathers.

218_Foolish_Guillemot.jpg.title: 218 Foolish Guillemot
218_Foolish_Guillemot.jpg.description: Plate 218 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Foolish Guillemot.
218_Foolish_Guillemot.jpg.story: The Common Murre earned its old name from tame indifference to approaching hunters, a trust that emptied many cliffs in the egging years. Breeding birds stand shoulder-to-shoulder in such density that a single pair may defend no more than a square foot of ledge.

219_Black_Guillemot.jpg.title: 219 Black Guillemot
219_Black_Guillemot.jpg.description: Plate 219 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black Guillemot.
219_Black_Guillemot.jpg.story: Black Guillemots show off by opening their bills to flash a shocking vermilion gape and matching red legs, a display that rings through northern harbors each spring. Audubon sketched them from the Ripley's deck off Labrador, often finishing watercolors below by lantern the same night.

21_Mocking_Bird.jpg.title: 21 Mocking Bird
21_Mocking_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 21 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Mocking Bird.
21_Mocking_Bird.jpg.story: A Northern Mockingbird can master more than two hundred songs in its lifetime, weaving rival birds, frogs, and creaking gates into its endless recitation. Audubon's plate pitches a family's defense against a coiled rattlesnake, a dramatic tableau that drew skeptical fire from European naturalists when the print first appeared.

220_Piping_Plover.jpg.title: 220 Piping Plover
220_Piping_Plover.jpg.description: Plate 220 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Piping Plover.
220_Piping_Plover.jpg.story: Pale as dry sand, Piping Plovers nest in scrapes on open beach and lead intruders away with a broken-wing act so convincing predators follow it every time. The life-sized format of Audubon's folio rendered even such small birds at their true dimensions, aiding field identification for a century.

221_Mallard_Duck.jpg.title: 221 Mallard Duck
221_Mallard_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 221 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Mallard Duck.
221_Mallard_Duck.jpg.story: Mallards are the ancestors of nearly every domestic duck, a lineage begun in ancient China and still unfolding in every city pond. Audubon's pair is pitched among reeds; he usually worked from freshly shot birds, drawing within hours before the green head-sheen began to fade.

222_White_Ibis.jpg.title: 222 White Ibis
222_White_Ibis.jpg.description: Plate 222 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White Ibis.
222_White_Ibis.jpg.story: White Ibis probe with that down-curved red bill for crayfish and crabs in shallow southern marshes, their young chasing parents across the mudflats with comically mottled plumage. Havell Jr. took over engraving all of Audubon's plates after 1827 and produced the bulk of the final 435 at his Oxford Street workshop.

223_Pied_oyster-catcher.jpg.title: 223 Pied oyster-catcher
223_Pied_oyster-catcher.jpg.description: Plate 223 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pied oyster-catcher.
223_Pied_oyster-catcher.jpg.story: American Oystercatchers pry bivalves open with a chisel-shaped red bill, inserting the blade before the mollusk can clamp shut. Audubon prized this bird for its boldness on the strand, and he often posed shorebirds against the surf line they had been collected from.

224_Kittiwake_Gull.jpg.title: 224 Kittiwake Gull
224_Kittiwake_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 224 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Kittiwake Gull.
224_Kittiwake_Gull.jpg.story: Black-legged Kittiwakes are the most oceanic of the true gulls, returning to land only to nest on sheer cliffs where their name-giving ki-ti-wake cry echoes off the rock. Audubon observed enormous colonies during the 1833 Labrador voyage, calling their clamor the voice of the northern cliffs.

225_Kildeer_Plover.jpg.title: 225 Kildeer Plover
225_Kildeer_Plover.jpg.description: Plate 225 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Kildeer Plover.
225_Kildeer_Plover.jpg.story: The Killdeer nests on gravel far from water, distracting intruders with the classic broken-wing display and a ringing kill-deer cry that carries across fields at dawn. Audubon's volumes were sold only by subscription, at roughly one thousand dollars a set, a sum that bought a small farm in his day.

226_Hooping_Crane.jpg.title: 226 Hooping Crane
226_Hooping_Crane.jpg.description: Plate 226 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hooping Crane. Bird painted in New Orleans 1821; baby alligators added 1822; background at later date.
226_Hooping_Crane.jpg.story: Whooping Cranes stand nearly five feet tall and bugle over the prairie in a courtship dance of leaps and flung grass. Audubon's plate is a composite of work from several years: the crane painted in New Orleans in 1821, baby alligators added the next year, and the background finished later still.

227_Pin_tailed_Duck.jpg.title: 227 Pin tailed Duck
227_Pin_tailed_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 227 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pin tailed Duck.
227_Pin_tailed_Duck.jpg.story: Northern Pintails cut the sky with long pointed tails and elegantly slim necks, among the swiftest of all ducks in straight flight. Audubon liked to show drakes alongside hens to make the strong sexual dimorphism legible at a glance, a teaching habit carried over from his Kentucky drawing school days.

228_American_Green_winged_Teal.jpg.title: 228 American Green winged Teal
228_American_Green_winged_Teal.jpg.description: Plate 228 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Green winged Teal.
228_American_Green_winged_Teal.jpg.story: Green-winged Teal are the smallest dabblers on the continent, twisting through timber pools in compact flocks that bank as one. Havell's aquatint technique laid down tonal values by biting powdered resin into the copper with acid, producing the soft watery washes these small ducks required.

229_Scaup_Duck.jpg.title: 229 Scaup Duck
229_Scaup_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 229 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Scaup Duck.
229_Scaup_Duck.jpg.story: Greater Scaup raft in huge winter flocks on open bays and dive for mussels, surfacing with shells still clamped in their bills. The standard double-elephant folio sheet measured about 26 by 39 inches, and a full set filled four massive bound volumes weighing close to two hundred pounds.

22_Purple_Martin.jpg.title: 22 Purple Martin
22_Purple_Martin.jpg.description: Plate 22 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Purple Martin.
22_Purple_Martin.jpg.story: Purple Martins are the largest North American swallows and depend almost entirely on human-provided housing east of the Rockies, a partnership begun by Southeastern tribes who hung hollow gourds for them. Audubon noted the custom admiringly and added his own voice to the gathering case that martins eat prodigious numbers of flying insects around farms.

230_Ruddy_Plover.jpg.title: 230 Ruddy Plover
230_Ruddy_Plover.jpg.description: Plate 230 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ruddy Plover.
230_Ruddy_Plover.jpg.story: Sanderlings chase receding waves and flee the incoming ones in the ceaseless shorebird ballet every beachgoer knows, pattering on black legs as the foam curls. Audubon understood migration poorly by modern standards, but he correctly pegged these little sprinters as long-distance travelers from the high Arctic, wintering on beaches as far south as Patagonia.

231_Long-billed_Curlew.jpg.title: 231 Long-billed Curlew
231_Long-billed_Curlew.jpg.description: Plate 231 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Long-billed Curlew.
231_Long-billed_Curlew.jpg.story: The Long-billed Curlew probes six inches deep into mud and sand for burrowing shrimp, its down-curved bill the longest of any North American shorebird. Audubon set his pair on a Charleston harbor flat, a city he visited repeatedly as a guest of the naturalist John Bachman.

232_Hooded_Merganser.jpg.title: 232 Hooded Merganser
232_Hooded_Merganser.jpg.description: Plate 232 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hooded Merganser.
232_Hooded_Merganser.jpg.story: The male Hooded Merganser raises a fan-shaped white crest in courtship and growls like a distant frog to impress the hen. Audubon often dissected specimens to confirm diet, and his serrated saw-bill studies showed these mergansers were built for gripping slippery minnows.

233_Sora_or_Rail.jpg.title: 233 Sora or Rail
233_Sora_or_Rail.jpg.description: Plate 233 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Sora or Rail.
233_Sora_or_Rail.jpg.story: Soras whinny from cattail marshes in a descending trill that slides into silence, and their slim bodies slip through reeds that wouldn't bend for a songbird. The Ornithological Biography ran to five volumes and was published in Edinburgh without plates, the text priced for readers who could not afford the folio.

234_Tufted_Duck.jpg.title: 234 Tufted Duck
234_Tufted_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 234 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tufted Duck.
234_Tufted_Duck.jpg.story: Tufted Ducks are Old World divers that wander to North America now and then, easily picked out by the drake's drooping ponytail crest. Audubon acquired some Eurasian species through the London specimen trade rather than the field, a compromise he made to keep the folio complete.

235_Sooty_Tern.jpg.title: 235 Sooty Tern
235_Sooty_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 235 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Sooty Tern.
235_Sooty_Tern.jpg.story: Sooty Terns can stay aloft for years at a stretch after fledging, sleeping on the wing and dipping for small fish at the surface without ever landing on water. Audubon visited the great colony in the Dry Tortugas in 1832, pulled ashore by revenue cutter in a cloud of screaming white-bellied birds.

236_Night_Heron_or_Qua_bird.jpg.title: 236 Night Heron or Qua bird
236_Night_Heron_or_Qua_bird.jpg.description: Plate 236 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Night Heron or Qua bird.

source.canonical_title: File:236 Night Heron or Qua bird.jpg

236_Night_Heron_or_Qua_bird.jpg.story: Black-crowned Night-Herons hunt by dusk with hunched shoulders and staring red eyes, delivering a hoarse quawk that gives them their old common name. Audubon was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1830, a prestige that helped him hold onto British subscribers through the long years of publication.

237_Great_Esquimaux_Curlew.jpg.title: 237 Great Esquimaux Curlew
237_Great_Esquimaux_Curlew.jpg.description: Plate 237 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Esquimaux Curlew.
237_Great_Esquimaux_Curlew.jpg.story: The Whimbrel migrates between Arctic tundra and tropical coasts, feeding on the fiddler crabs it extracts from their burrows with its neatly curved bill. Audubon sometimes conflated whimbrels and Eskimo Curlews in the field, a muddle later ornithologists spent decades sorting out.

238_Great_Marbled_Godwit.jpg.title: 238 Great Marbled Godwit
238_Great_Marbled_Godwit.jpg.description: Plate 238 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Marbled Godwit.
238_Great_Marbled_Godwit.jpg.story: Marbled Godwits probe wet prairie and mudflat with a long slightly upturned bill tipped in pink, calling god-WIT as they rise in loose flocks. Each completed folio volume sold for roughly $1,000 in 1830s dollars, delivered to subscribers in groups of five plates at a time over eleven years.

239_American_Coot.jpg.title: 239 American Coot
239_American_Coot.jpg.description: Plate 239 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Coot.
239_American_Coot.jpg.story: American Coots paddle on lobed toes rather than webbed feet, patter across open water to take off, and bicker incessantly at every marsh edge. Audubon filled out his coverage of water birds during his later years on the Atlantic coast after his inland Kentucky and Ohio decades.

23_Yellow-breasted_Warbler.jpg.title: 23 Yellow-breasted Warbler
23_Yellow-breasted_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 23 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-breasted Warbler.
23_Yellow-breasted_Warbler.jpg.story: Audubon's Yellow-breasted Warbler has been debated ever since, some taking it for a pine warbler variant and others for a lost form. He painted scores of small songbirds during his Mississippi River years, riding from Natchez to New Orleans and back while collecting specimens for the folio.

240_Roseate_Tern.jpg.title: 240 Roseate Tern
240_Roseate_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 240 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Roseate Tern.
