240_Roseate_Tern.jpg.story: Roseate Terns fledge with a faint pink bloom on the breast that survives only a few weeks before the plumage turns pure white. Havell Jr. engraved the outlines in line work, then bit the tonal areas in aquatint, a two-stage process that gave the prints both precision and painterly atmosphere.

241_Black_Backed_Gull.jpg.title: 241 Black Backed Gull
241_Black_Backed_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 241 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black Backed Gull.
241_Black_Backed_Gull.jpg.story: Great Black-backed Gulls are the largest gulls in the world and ruthless opportunists, killing puffin chicks and stealing from smaller seabirds with casual ease. Audubon sketched them on Labrador's rocky stacks, where he recorded their harassment of kittiwake colonies at close range.

242_Snowy_Heron_or_White_Egret.jpg.title: Snowy Heron or White Egret
242_Snowy_Heron_or_White_Egret.jpg.description: Plate 242 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Snowy Heron or White Egret Egretta thula .

source.canonical_title: File:242 Snowy Heron or White Egret.jpg

242_Snowy_Heron_or_White_Egret.jpg.story: Snowy Egrets dance on the shallows with golden feet that may startle small fish into the open, and their lacy breeding plumes nearly drove the species extinct in the early twentieth century before protection returned. Audubon set this bird against a low-country plantation backdrop said to be Charleston, a nod to his friend John Bachman who hosted him during southern expeditions.

243_American_Snipe.jpg.title: 243 American Snipe
243_American_Snipe.jpg.description: Plate 243 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Snipe.
243_American_Snipe.jpg.story: Wilson's Snipe probes soft ground with a flexible bill tip that can feel and seize worms while the bird stares straight ahead. The male's courtship winnow is an eerie hollow vibration, produced not by voice but by wind rushing through outer tail feathers in steep diving flight, a detail Audubon tried to describe from field observation in the Ornithological Biography.

244_Common_Gallinule.jpg.title: 244 Common Gallinule
244_Common_Gallinule.jpg.description: Plate 244 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Common Gallinule.
244_Common_Gallinule.jpg.story: Common Gallinules clamber through emergent marshes on long yellow-green toes, their red-and-yellow bills flashing like traffic lights against the reeds. Audubon delivered the folio's plates in sets of five to his subscribers every few months, a delivery rhythm Havell's London workshop maintained for eleven years running.

245_Thick-billed_Murre.jpg.title: 245 Thick-billed Murre
245_Thick-billed_Murre.jpg.description: Plate 245 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Thick-billed Murre.
245_Thick-billed_Murre.jpg.story: Thick-billed Murres are the deepest-diving flighted birds in the world, reaching five hundred feet on a single breath to chase fish in near-darkness. Audubon visited seabird colonies on Labrador in 1833 aboard the schooner Ripley, and he recorded how the birds' single pear-shaped eggs rolled in a tight circle rather than falling off the narrow ledges.

246_Eider_Duck.jpg.title: 246 Eider Duck
246_Eider_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 246 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Eider Duck.
246_Eider_Duck.jpg.story: Common Eiders winter on the North Atlantic and line their nests with down plucked from the hen's own breast, insulation so effective that Icelanders have gathered it sustainably for a thousand years. Audubon met the species on the Labrador coast, where he watched drakes in full dress court the dowdy brown hens along the lee side of stony islets.

247_Velvet_Duck.jpg.title: 247 Velvet Duck
247_Velvet_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 247 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Velvet Duck (= White-winged Scoter Melanitta deglandi ).
247_Velvet_Duck.jpg.story: The bird Audubon called Velvet Duck is now the White-winged Scoter, a heavy sea-diver that feeds on mussels and clams torn from the bottom. Havell's aquatint reproduces the drake's velvet black plumage through tiny pits of acid-bitten copper, a technique that held ink more softly than engraved line work ever could.

248_American_Pied-bill_Dobchick.jpg.title: 248 American Pied-bill Dobchick
248_American_Pied-bill_Dobchick.jpg.description: Plate 248 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Pied-bill Dobchick.
248_American_Pied-bill_Dobchick.jpg.story: The Pied-billed Grebe sinks vertically like a submarine when alarmed, leaving nothing above water but the tip of its banded bill. Audubon's text often ran into digressions on frontier life, canoe travel, and the hospitality of backwoods settlers, giving the Ornithological Biography a memoir quality unusual for its scientific genre.

249_Tufted_Auk.jpg.title: 249 Tufted Auk
249_Tufted_Auk.jpg.description: Plate 249 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tufted Auk.
249_Tufted_Auk.jpg.story: The Tufted Puffin is a Pacific seabird that nests in turf burrows on high sea-cliffs, the breeding adults sporting long golden head plumes that curl back past the nape. Audubon never reached the Pacific and worked from skins collected by British or Russian voyagers who passed through London on their way home.

24_Roscoe's_Yellow-throat.jpg.title: 24 Roscoe's Yellow-throat
24_Roscoe's_Yellow-throat.jpg.description: Plate 24 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Roscoe's Yellow-throat.
24_Roscoe's_Yellow-throat.jpg.story: Roscoe's Yellow-throat is now considered a juvenile or aberrant Common Yellowthroat rather than a species; Audubon, working without modern reference collections, named a number of such ghost taxa. He christened this one for William Roscoe, a Liverpool banker who had subscribed to the folio.

250_Arctic_Tern.jpg.title: 250 Arctic Tern
250_Arctic_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 250 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Arctic Tern.
250_Arctic_Tern.jpg.story: The Arctic Tern flies from pole to pole every year, a round trip of roughly forty thousand miles that gives it more daylight than any other animal on earth. Audubon met them on the 1833 Labrador voyage, where he watched birds drop into the sea for capelin along the Ripley's wake.

251_Brown_Pelican.jpg.title: 251 Brown Pelican
251_Brown_Pelican.jpg.description: Plate 251 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Brown Pelican.
251_Brown_Pelican.jpg.story: Brown Pelicans plunge from forty feet to stun fish, then tip the bill vertical to drain water from the pouch before swallowing. Audubon was said to have painted this pelican from a live bird he kept tied in his Charleston yard, feeding it on mullet until it sat still enough to draw.

252_Florida_Cormorant.jpg.title: 252 Florida Cormorant
252_Florida_Cormorant.jpg.description: Plate 252 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Florida Cormorant.
252_Florida_Cormorant.jpg.story: Once considered a distinct species, the Florida Cormorant is now lumped into the Double-crested Cormorant, a fish-eating diver that hangs its wings out to dry on snags. Audubon's 1832 Florida expedition produced a string of new plates from the mangrove keys.

253_Jager.jpg.title: 253 Jager
253_Jager.jpg.description: Plate 253 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Jager.
253_Jager.jpg.story: Jaegers are seabird pirates, chasing terns and gulls in the air until the victim drops or disgorges its catch, which the jaeger snatches before it hits the water. Audubon used the Scandinavian name Jager long after English ornithologists began calling them skuas.

254_Wilson's_Phalarope.jpg.title: 254 Wilson's Phalarope
254_Wilson's_Phalarope.jpg.description: Plate 254 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wilson's Phalarope.
254_Wilson's_Phalarope.jpg.story: Wilson's Phalaropes are inland birds of western alkaline lakes, where they swim in tight spinning circles to stir the shallow water. Like all phalaropes the hen wears the brighter plumage, courts the male, and abandons him to the eggs; Audubon noted the role reversal with care.

255_Red_Phalarope.jpg.title: 255 Red Phalarope
255_Red_Phalarope.jpg.description: Plate 255 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red Phalarope.
255_Red_Phalarope.jpg.story: Red Phalaropes breed on wet Arctic tundra and then vanish to the open ocean, the only shorebird to spend most of its year at sea. Audubon's coverage of pelagic species was thin until his Labrador voyage opened a window onto the subarctic, adding many plates in the middle numbers.

256_Purple_Heron.jpg.title: 256 Purple Heron
256_Purple_Heron.jpg.description: Plate 256 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Purple Heron.
256_Purple_Heron.jpg.story: The slender Purple Heron is an Old World marsh specialist that Audubon added to the folio from specimens acquired in Europe during his long periods in London and Edinburgh shepherding the folio through publication. The species has rarely been recorded in North America since.

257_Double-crested_Cormorant.jpg.title: 257 Double-crested Cormorant
257_Double-crested_Cormorant.jpg.description: Plate 257 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Double-crested Cormorant.
257_Double-crested_Cormorant.jpg.story: During the breeding season, Double-crested Cormorants sprout the twin wispy crests of their name, one over each eye, and dance them at prospective mates. Havell's workshop issued Audubon's plates in numbered sets of five every couple of months, a pace that kept the subscription income flowing.

258_Hudsonian_Godwit.jpg.title: 258 Hudsonian Godwit
258_Hudsonian_Godwit.jpg.description: Plate 258 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hudsonian Godwit.
258_Hudsonian_Godwit.jpg.story: The Hudsonian Godwit is one of the continent's most mysterious migrants, vanishing from southbound sight in New England and reappearing on the pampas six weeks later after a trans-oceanic leg. Audubon rarely saw them fresh and worked from specimens supplied by trappers around Hudson Bay.

259_Horned_Grebe.jpg.title: 259 Horned Grebe
259_Horned_Grebe.jpg.description: Plate 259 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Horned Grebe.
259_Horned_Grebe.jpg.story: In breeding dress, Horned Grebes sport golden ear-tufts that flare in courtship dances across small northern lakes, the paired birds rushing upright side by side. Audubon noted their habit of eating their own feathers, an oddity later proven to protect the stomach lining from sharp fish bones, and Havell's aquatint caught the soft silken flank plumage that makes the species unmistakable.

25_Song_Sparrow.jpg.title: 25 Song Sparrow
25_Song_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 25 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Song Sparrow.
25_Song_Sparrow.jpg.story: Song Sparrows sing more than twenty variations of their jingling refrain, each male taught by his father and refined against neighbors. Audubon's accompanying essays in the Ornithological Biography are as lively as his paintings, with long passages on nesting, migration, and the birds' individual quirks.

260_Fork-tail_Petrel.jpg.title: 260 Fork-tail Petrel
260_Fork-tail_Petrel.jpg.description: Birds of America - Complete Double Elephant folio

source.permission: Author died more than 70 years ago - public domain

260_Fork-tail_Petrel.jpg.story: Leach's Storm-Petrels nest in underground burrows they visit only at night, finding their own mates by scent in the tangle of vocalizations over the colony. Seabirds like this one pushed Audubon's folio to its monumental length, stretched across eleven years of engraving in London.

261_Hooping_Crane.jpg.title: 261 Hooping Crane
261_Hooping_Crane.jpg.description: Plate 261 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hooping Crane.
261_Hooping_Crane.jpg.story: This is Audubon's second Whooping Crane plate, showing a young bird in its rust-cinnamon juvenile plumage. By the 1940s the species had fallen to about twenty individuals; the slow recovery since has become a signal case in American wildlife conservation.

262_Tropic_Bird.jpg.title: 262 Tropic Bird
262_Tropic_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 262 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tropic Bird.
262_Tropic_Bird.jpg.story: White-tailed Tropicbirds trail two long streamers behind a slender white body as they wheel over blue tropical seas, plunging from height for squid. Audubon probably worked from specimens taken in the Caribbean and prepared in Charleston by his friend John Bachman.

263_Pigmy_Curlew.jpg.title: 263 Pigmy Curlew
263_Pigmy_Curlew.jpg.description: Birds of America - Complete Double Elephant folio

source.permission: Author died more than 70 years ago - public domain

263_Pigmy_Curlew.jpg.story: The Curlew Sandpiper is an Old World shorebird that occasionally strays to North America, recognized by its down-curved bill and in breeding dress a deep brick-red belly. Audubon included such vagrants when he could obtain reliable specimens, broadening the folio's claim to cover the continent's full avifauna.

264_Fulmar_Petrel.jpg.title: 264 Fulmar Petrel
264_Fulmar_Petrel.jpg.description: Plate 264 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Fulmar Petrel.
264_Fulmar_Petrel.jpg.story: Northern Fulmars spit a foul stomach oil at any predator that approaches the nest, a defense that mats a gull's plumage and can even kill it. Audubon described the stench with relish in the Biography, a five-volume companion text published in Edinburgh.

265_Buff_breasted_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 265 Buff breasted Sandpiper
265_Buff_breasted_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 265 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Buff breasted Sandpiper.
265_Buff_breasted_Sandpiper.jpg.story: Buff-breasted Sandpipers gather on short-grass prairie for an aerial-then-ground lek where males flash the silver underwing at passing hens, then freeze in comic ritual postures. Their populations cratered under market hunting in the late nineteenth century and have only partially recovered, and Audubon's plate today reads partly as a record of an older prairie he could still observe firsthand.

266_Common_Cormorant.jpg.title: 266 Common Cormorant
266_Common_Cormorant.jpg.description: Plate 266 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Common Cormorant.
266_Common_Cormorant.jpg.story: The Great Cormorant is a heavy-bodied diver of North Atlantic coasts, trained for centuries in China and Japan to catch fish for their human owners. Audubon met the species on both sides of the Atlantic and included it among his many cormorant plates.

267_Arctic_Yager.jpg.title: 267 Arctic Yager
267_Arctic_Yager.jpg.description: Plate 267 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Arctic Yager.
267_Arctic_Yager.jpg.story: The Parasitic Jaeger harries terns and small gulls in long chases that end when the prey drops its catch in the air. Audubon's folio was bound in four volumes of plates, accompanied by five companion volumes of text, a set that only the wealthiest libraries could afford to keep complete.

268_American_Woodcock.jpg.title: 268 American Woodcock
268_American_Woodcock.jpg.description: Plate 268 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Woodcock.
268_American_Woodcock.jpg.story: Woodcocks probe earthworms from soft ground with a flexible-tipped bill, eyes set high in the skull so they can watch for hawks while feeding head-down. The male's springtime sky-dance spirals two hundred feet up in twittering flight, a ritual Audubon described with delight.

269_Greenshank.jpg.title: 269 Greenshank
269_Greenshank.jpg.description: Plate 269 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Greenshank.
269_Greenshank.jpg.story: The Common Greenshank is a long-legged Eurasian shorebird that Audubon obtained from British dealers while he was in London supervising the engraving. His years abroad, chasing subscribers through English country estates, were as much of the work as any field trip.

26_Carolina_Parrot.jpg.title: 26 Carolina Parrot
26_Carolina_Parrot.jpg.description: Plate 26 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Carolina Parrot.
26_Carolina_Parrot.jpg.story: The Carolina Parakeet was once the only parrot native to the eastern United States, a green and orange flock bird that descended on cocklebur and apple in screeching mobs. It was declared extinct in 1918, and Audubon's plate is among the best surviving likenesses of a species we will not see again.

270_Stormy_Petrel.jpg.title: 270 Stormy Petrel
270_Stormy_Petrel.jpg.description: Plate 270 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Stormy Petrel.
270_Stormy_Petrel.jpg.story: European Storm-Petrels flutter just above heaving waves with feet pattering the surface, as if walking on water; sailors took the name petrel from Peter, the apostle who tried the same feat. Audubon added this species from skins since it breeds only in the eastern Atlantic.

271_Frigate_Pelican.jpg.title: 271 Frigate Pelican
271_Frigate_Pelican.jpg.description: Plate 271 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Frigate Pelican.
271_Frigate_Pelican.jpg.story: The Magnificent Frigatebird has the highest wing area to body weight ratio of any bird and can stay aloft for a week without touching down, stealing fish from the bills of boobies in mid-air. Audubon watched them off the Florida Keys during his 1832 trip on the revenue cutter Marion.

272_Richardson's_Jager.jpg.title: 272 Richardson's Jager
272_Richardson's_Jager.jpg.description: Plate 272 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Richardson's Jager.
272_Richardson's_Jager.jpg.story: The bird Audubon called Richardson's Jager is our Parasitic Jaeger, a piratical seabird that chases terns until they drop their catch in mid-air. The name honors the Scottish naturalist John Richardson, whose Arctic explorations kept supplying North American ornithology with new specimens, part of a web of homages Audubon exchanged with transatlantic colleagues throughout the folio.

273_Cayenne_Tern.jpg.title: 273 Cayenne Tern
273_Cayenne_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 273 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Cayenne Tern.
273_Cayenne_Tern.jpg.story: What Audubon called the Cayenne Tern is now lumped with the Royal Tern, a crested orange-billed fisher of southern coasts that plunges into the surf after menhaden. Havell's colorists used watercolor over the aquatint base, matching Audubon's original paintings plate by plate.

274_Semipalmated_Snipe,_or_Willet.jpg.title: 274 Semipalmated Snipe, or Willet
274_Semipalmated_Snipe,_or_Willet.jpg.description: Plate 274 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Semipalmated Snipe, or Willet.
274_Semipalmated_Snipe,_or_Willet.jpg.story: The Willet is plain brown until it flies, when a striking black-and-white wing pattern unfurls with a pill-will-willet cry. Audubon often painted flying and standing attitudes of the same species in paired plates, a didactic habit useful to ornithologists well into the modern era.

275_Noddy_Tern.jpg.title: 275 Noddy Tern
275_Noddy_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 275 of The Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Noddy Tern.
275_Noddy_Tern.jpg.story: Brown Noddies build flimsy stick nests on low branches of tropical islands and got their name from the bobbing head-display of courting pairs. Audubon found them nesting on the Dry Tortugas in 1832, a boat-accessible colony he described at length in the Ornithological Biography.

276_King_Duck.jpg.title: 276 King Duck
276_King_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 276 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting King Duck.
276_King_Duck.jpg.story: The King Eider drake wears a blue-grey crown, orange forehead shield, and rose-colored breast, a baroque combination that Audubon's colorists struggled to match batch to batch. The species breeds on Arctic coasts and was known to him only through specimens from northern traders.

277_Hutchins's_Barnacle_Goose.jpg.title: 277 Hutchins's Barnacle Goose
277_Hutchins's_Barnacle_Goose.jpg.description: Plate 277 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hutchins's Barnacle Goose.

source.credit: This file comes from the Biodiversity Heritage Library , and is available online at biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40392741 .

277_Hutchins's_Barnacle_Goose.jpg.story: Hutchins's Barnacle Goose is now recognized as a small subspecies of the Cackling Goose, separated from the Canada Goose only in 2004. Audubon named it for Thomas Hutchins, a Hudson's Bay Company factor whose arctic notes were feeding British ornithology in the early 1800s.

278_Schinz's_Sandpiper_restored.jpg.title: 278 Schinz's Sandpiper restored
278_Schinz's_Sandpiper_restored.jpg.artist: Drawn from nature by John James Audubon F R S. F L S. Engraved, Printed & Coloured by Robert Havell 1835
278_Schinz's_Sandpiper_restored.jpg.description: Plate 278 of The Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Schinz's Sandpiper Tringa schinzii Brehm [= Dunlin Calidris alpina schinzii as to Brehm's 1822 original description, but widely misidentified as White-rumped Sandpiper Calidris fuscicollis following Bonaparte 1828 until Coues 1861 discussed the error].
278_Schinz's_Sandpiper_restored.jpg.story: Originally described from Brehm's 1822 specimens, Schinz's Sandpiper bounced between names for decades before Coues settled it as a European Dunlin in 1861. Audubon's plate is a reminder of how tangled shorebird taxonomy was before binoculars and reliable reference collections reached the field.

279_Cabot's_Tern.jpg.title: 279 Cabot's Tern
279_Cabot's_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 279 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Cabot's Tern (formerly a subspecies of Sandwich Tern).
279_Cabot's_Tern.jpg.story: Cabot's Tern, until recently grouped as a subspecies of Sandwich Tern, has a slender black bill tipped bright yellow as if dipped in mustard. The species was named for Samuel Cabot Jr., a Boston physician who helped Audubon with specimens and lived to see the folio completed.

27_Red_headed_Woodpecker.jpg.title: 27 Red headed Woodpecker
27_Red_headed_Woodpecker.jpg.description: Plate 27 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red headed Woodpecker.
27_Red_headed_Woodpecker.jpg.story: Red-headed Woodpeckers wedge acorns and even live grasshoppers into bark crevices for later, a caching habit unusual among American woodpeckers and often covered with bits of wood. Audubon praised their pugnacity, describing skirmishes at nest cavities he had watched along Kentucky creeks during his years running a general store in Henderson before the failure that set him on the long road to the folio.

280_Black_Tern.jpg.title: 280 Black Tern
280_Black_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 280 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black Tern.
280_Black_Tern.jpg.story: Black Terns are the continent's only inland-breeding marsh tern, a dark summer-dressed bird that hawks dragonflies over freshwater sloughs. Audubon's text entries often ran longer than the descriptions of the bird itself, venturing into Mississippi flatboat travel and frontier hospitality.

281_Great_White_Heron.jpg.title: 281 Great White Heron
281_Great_White_Heron.jpg.description: Plate 281 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great White Heron.
281_Great_White_Heron.jpg.story: Once considered a distinct species, the Great White Heron is now treated as a pale morph of the Great Blue Heron, restricted to South Florida and the Keys. Audubon met them on his 1832 Florida expedition and mistook them for a separate kind, as did most ornithologists for more than a century.

282_White-winged_silvery_Gull.jpg.title: 282 White-winged silvery Gull
282_White-winged_silvery_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 282 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-winged silvery Gull.
282_White-winged_silvery_Gull.jpg.story: This plate is considered a juvenile or poorly recognized gull whose identity remained fuzzy in Audubon's day; modern authorities place it with the Iceland Gull complex. The folio contains a handful of such taxonomic ghosts, artifacts of an era before field guides existed to settle identifications.

283_Wandering_Shearwater.jpg.title: 283 Wandering Shearwater
283_Wandering_Shearwater.jpg.description: Plate 283 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wandering Shearwater.
283_Wandering_Shearwater.jpg.story: Great Shearwaters breed on a few islands in the South Atlantic and fly a huge looping migration each year up into North Atlantic waters, where Audubon would have met them from the deck of the Ripley. They rarely flap, banking instead on stiff wings down the face of each swell.

284_Purple_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 284 Purple Sandpiper
284_Purple_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 284 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Purple Sandpiper.
284_Purple_Sandpiper.jpg.story: Purple Sandpipers winter farther north than any other shorebird, hopping among sea-washed rocks where the surf has just broken, picking periwinkles from the kelp. Audubon was one of the first to describe their boldness, letting humans approach within arm's reach in harsh weather.

285_Fork-tailed_Gull.jpg.title: 285 Fork-tailed Gull
285_Fork-tailed_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 285 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Fork-tailed Gull.
285_Fork-tailed_Gull.jpg.story: Sabine's Gull has a forked tail and a striking black-white-grey wing pattern seen in flight, a bird of high Arctic tundra pools that winters at sea off South America and Africa. Audubon obtained specimens from British Arctic expeditions returning through London in the 1830s.

286_White-fronted_Goose.jpg.title: 286 White-fronted Goose
286_White-fronted_Goose.jpg.description: Plate 286 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-fronted Goose.
286_White-fronted_Goose.jpg.story: Greater White-fronted Geese graze prairie and stubble on a shrill musical laughter-call that gave them their old frontier name of Laughing Goose. Audubon sometimes painted geese from market birds sold in New Orleans stalls, working at speed before the birds were plucked for the pot.

287_Ivory_Gull.jpg.title: 287 Ivory Gull
287_Ivory_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 287 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ivory Gull.
287_Ivory_Gull.jpg.story: The Ivory Gull is pure white except for black legs and a pale blue bill, a high-Arctic scavenger that follows polar bears and walrus hunts for scraps. Its extreme range meant Audubon never saw a live one; the plate was drawn from a specimen acquired through the London natural history trade.

288_Yellow_Shank.jpg.title: 288 Yellow Shank
288_Yellow_Shank.jpg.description: Plate 288 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow Shank.
288_Yellow_Shank.jpg.story: Yellowlegs are long-legged slim shorebirds that bob nervously on a wet flat, calling their sharp tew-tew as they spring into flight. Audubon arranged many of his shorebirds standing on a narrow stage of mud at the plate's lower edge, leaving white space above that made the bird seem to glow.

289_Solitary_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 289 Solitary Sandpiper
289_Solitary_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 289 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Solitary Sandpiper.
289_Solitary_Sandpiper.jpg.story: Solitary Sandpipers nest in the old tree-nests of robins and waxwings rather than on the ground, the only North American shorebird to do so. They migrate alone through wooded ponds, a quiet bobbing presence easy to overlook even when close at hand.

28_Solitary_Flycatcher_or_Vireo.jpg.title: 28 Solitary Flycatcher or Vireo
28_Solitary_Flycatcher_or_Vireo.jpg.description: Plate 28 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Solitary Flycatcher or Vireo.
28_Solitary_Flycatcher_or_Vireo.jpg.story: The Blue-headed Vireo works slowly through coniferous and mixed woods, plucking caterpillars from the undersides of leaves in a deliberate hopping pattern. Audubon's workflow had him killing, drawing, skinning, and eating many of his subjects in a single long day of fieldwork.

290_Red_backed_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 290 Red backed Sandpiper
290_Red_backed_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 290 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red backed Sandpiper.
290_Red_backed_Sandpiper.jpg.story: The Dunlin in breeding dress wears a black belly-patch and chestnut back that stand out across miles of tundra. Audubon drew them as fall migrants along the Atlantic coast, their summer markings already molting into the plain winter grey that later confused him into new-species errors.

291_Herring_Gull.jpg.title: 291 Herring Gull
291_Herring_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 291 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Herring Gull.
291_Herring_Gull.jpg.story: Herring Gulls can drink salt water thanks to specialized glands above the eye that excrete the excess salt, letting them forage indefinitely at sea. Audubon called them the commonest gull of the American coast, and Havell's plate shows the heavy-billed adult against a rolling gray sky.

292_Crested_Grebe.jpg.title: 292 Crested Grebe
292_Crested_Grebe.jpg.description: Plate 292 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Crested Grebe.
292_Crested_Grebe.jpg.story: The Great Crested Grebe performs a celebrated weed dance, a mated pair rising chest-to-chest with water plants gripped in their bills. Audubon's version covers both New World and Old World populations, now considered separate species, an error typical of 1830s ornithology.

293_Large_billed_Puffin.jpg.title: 293 Large billed Puffin
293_Large_billed_Puffin.jpg.description: Plate 293 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Large billed Puffin.
293_Large_billed_Puffin.jpg.story: The Horned Puffin is a Pacific species, sporting a small fleshy horn above the eye that gave it the name. Audubon probably worked from specimens collected by Russian or British voyagers returning through London, since he himself never reached the Pacific.

294_Pectoral_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 294 Pectoral Sandpiper
294_Pectoral_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 294 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pectoral Sandpiper.
294_Pectoral_Sandpiper.jpg.story: Male Pectoral Sandpipers inflate a chestful of air sacs and boom a hollow hoot across the Arctic tundra, a display the females travel through on the way to their chosen mate. Audubon knew the species only as a migrant along eastern flyways.

295_Manks_Shearwater.jpg.title: 295 Manks Shearwater
295_Manks_Shearwater.jpg.description: Plate 295 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Manks Shearwater.
295_Manks_Shearwater.jpg.story: Manx Shearwaters nest in earthen burrows on remote Atlantic islands, returning only under cover of night to avoid marauding gulls. Ringing has revealed individuals that crossed the equator and lived past fifty, figures Audubon could not have guessed but hinted at in his descriptions of their ocean wandering.

296_Barnacle_Goose.jpg.title: 296 Barnacle Goose
296_Barnacle_Goose.jpg.description: Plate 296 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Barnacle Goose.
296_Barnacle_Goose.jpg.story: Medieval Europeans believed Barnacle Geese hatched from goose barnacles on driftwood, a handy myth that let churchgoers eat them on fast days as if they were fish. The species breeds in Greenland and Svalbard and reaches North America only rarely.

297_Harlequin_Duck.jpg.title: 297 Harlequin Duck
297_Harlequin_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 297 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Harlequin Duck.
297_Harlequin_Duck.jpg.story: Harlequin Ducks dive in whitewater rapids and at surf-lashed Atlantic rocks, reaching the bottom to prize snails and crustaceans from the stone. The drake's bold slate, white, and chestnut pattern led Audubon to call it the Lord, a name still used by sea-duck hunters on both coasts.

298_Red-necked_Grebe.jpg.title: 298 Red-necked Grebe
298_Red-necked_Grebe.jpg.description: Plate 298 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-necked Grebe.
298_Red-necked_Grebe.jpg.story: Red-necked Grebes build floating nests of soggy vegetation anchored to emergent stems, and the downy chicks ride the parents' backs across the pond. Audubon's text volumes were written jointly with the Scottish naturalist William MacGillivray, who supplied the technical anatomy for every species.

299_Dusky_Petrel.jpg.title: 299 Dusky Petrel
299_Dusky_Petrel.jpg.description: Plate 299 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Dusky Petrel.
299_Dusky_Petrel.jpg.story: The Audubon's Shearwater was named in his honor and spends most of its life on tropical and subtropical seas, nesting in rocky crevices on Caribbean islands. Audubon met them from the deck of the revenue cutter Marion on his 1832 Florida expedition, a voyage that fleshed out much of the folio's seabird coverage.

29_Towhe_Bunting.jpg.title: 29 Towhe Bunting
29_Towhe_Bunting.jpg.description: Plate 29 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Towhe Bunting.
29_Towhe_Bunting.jpg.story: The Eastern Towhee scratches through leaf litter with a distinctive two-footed backward hop, kicking up insects from forest floors. Audubon knew the bird as the Towhe Bunting, an onomatopoeic name echoing its ringing call. Plate 29 was among the earliest engraved by Robert Havell Jr., who took over printing in 1827 after William Lizars's colorists went on strike in Edinburgh.

2_Yellow-billed_Cuckoo.jpg.title: 2 Yellow-billed Cuckoo
2_Yellow-billed_Cuckoo.jpg.description: Plate 2 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-billed Cuckoo.
2_Yellow-billed_Cuckoo.jpg.story: The Yellow-billed Cuckoo slips secretively through leafy canopies hunting tent caterpillars others refuse to touch, its hollow 'kowp-kowp' call earning it the folk name 'rain crow.' Plate 2 was one of ten original plates engraved in Edinburgh by William Lizars before production moved permanently to Havell's London shop.

300_Golden_Plover.jpg.title: 300 Golden Plover
300_Golden_Plover.jpg.description: Plate 300 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Golden Plover.
300_Golden_Plover.jpg.story: The American Golden-Plover undertakes one of the longest migrations of any shorebird, flying from Arctic tundra to the Argentine pampas and back, often nonstop over open ocean. Audubon witnessed immense spring flocks moving through the prairies, some so dense that market hunters filled barrels in a single morning. Plate 300 marks a milestone in the folio's slow serial release to subscribers.

301_Canvas_backed_Duck.jpg.title: 301 Canvas backed Duck
301_Canvas_backed_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 301 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Canvas backed Duck.
301_Canvas_backed_Duck.jpg.story: The Canvasback dives deep for wild celery tubers on the Chesapeake, a diet that made it the most prized table duck of nineteenth-century America. Audubon painted several ducks against sweeping bay backgrounds rather than the plain vignettes of his songbirds, showing off Havell's aquatint skies.

302_Dusky_Duck.jpg.title: 302 Dusky Duck
302_Dusky_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 302 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Dusky Duck.
302_Dusky_Duck.jpg.story: The American Black Duck, Audubon's Dusky Duck, is a wary dabbler of salt marshes and beaver ponds, its sooty plumage flashing violet speculum in flight. The species has since hybridized extensively with Mallards expanding east, blurring the clean lines Audubon drew.

303_Bartram_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 303 Bartram Sandpiper
303_Bartram_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 303 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bartram Sandpiper.
303_Bartram_Sandpiper.jpg.story: The Upland Sandpiper is a shorebird that shuns the shore, breeding instead in tallgrass prairie where it perches on fenceposts and whistles eerie wolf-whistle songs at dusk. Audubon named it for William Bartram, the Philadelphia botanist whose 1791 Travels first inspired him toward American natural history.

304_Turn-stone.jpg.title: 304 Turn-stone
304_Turn-stone.jpg.description: Plate 304 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Turn-stone.
304_Turn-stone.jpg.story: The Ruddy Turnstone lives up to its name, flipping pebbles, shells, and seaweed with a short wedge bill to expose the crustaceans beneath. Audubon's turnstones are posed on a rocky shoreline rendered in Havell's delicate aquatint stipple, a tonal technique that suited wet stone and feather barring better than line engraving.

305_Purple_Gallinule.jpg.title: 305 Purple Gallinule
305_Purple_Gallinule.jpg.description: Plate 305 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Purple Gallinule.
305_Purple_Gallinule.jpg.story: The Purple Gallinule strides across lily pads on absurdly long yellow toes, iridescent in purple and turquoise like a walking jewel. Audubon observed them in Louisiana bayous, where they clamber up reeds to feed on seeds and frogs. Havell's colorists used shell gold on some impressions to catch the shimmering forehead shield.

306_Great_Northern_Diver_or_Loon.jpg.title: 306 Great Northern Diver or Loon
306_Great_Northern_Diver_or_Loon.jpg.description: Plate 306 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Northern Diver or Loon.

source.canonical_title: File:306 Great Northern Diver or Loon.jpg

306_Great_Northern_Diver_or_Loon.jpg.story: The Common Loon's tremolo wail carries miles across northern lakes, a sound Audubon called 'one of the most mournful.' Loons can dive below 200 feet on a single breath, their solid bones sacrificing flight efficiency for underwater pursuit. Plate 306 is one of the folio's largest compositions, sprawling across the full double-elephant sheet.

307_Blue_Crane_or_Heron.jpg.title: 307 Blue Crane or Heron
307_Blue_Crane_or_Heron.jpg.description: Plate 307 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue Crane or Heron.
307_Blue_Crane_or_Heron.jpg.story: The Little Blue Heron wades methodically through shallow marshes, sometimes freezing mid-step for minutes before striking. Juveniles are pure white and molt gradually through a piebald phase into slaty adult plumage. Audubon classed herons among cranes in the loose taxonomy of his day.

308_Tell-tale_Godwit_or_Snipe.jpg.title: 308 Tell-tale Godwit or Snipe
308_Tell-tale_Godwit_or_Snipe.jpg.description: Plate 308 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tell-tale Godwit or Snipe.
308_Tell-tale_Godwit_or_Snipe.jpg.story: The Greater Yellowlegs earned Audubon's name 'Tell-tale' from its shrill three-note alarm call, which betrays the approach of any hunter to every other bird on the mudflat. The yellow-legged wader bobs its head constantly while foraging. Market gunners cursed it as the most unhelpful sentinel of the salt marsh.

309_Great_Tern.jpg.title: 309 Great Tern
309_Great_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 309 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Tern.
309_Great_Tern.jpg.story: The Common Tern, slim and forked-tailed, plunges headfirst for silversides with a sharp crack of wings. Colonies defend nests by divebombing intruders and striking scalps with needle bills. Plate 309 showcases the white-on-white challenge that tested Havell's finest aquatint tones.

30_Vigor's_Warbler.jpg.title: 30 Vigor's Warbler
30_Vigor's_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 30 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Vigor's Warbler.
30_Vigor's_Warbler.jpg.story: Vigors's Warbler was an error — Audubon painted a juvenile Pine Warbler and mistook it for a new species, naming it for the Irish zoologist Nicholas Aylward Vigors. Several 'new' warblers in Birds of America turned out to be misidentified molts or aberrant individuals, a hazard of describing birds from single specimens.

310_Spotted_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 310 Spotted Sandpiper
310_Spotted_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 310 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Spotted Sandpiper.
310_Spotted_Sandpiper.jpg.story: The Spotted Sandpiper teeters constantly along stream banks, bobbing its tail with every step in a motion no one has fully explained. Females arrive first on breeding grounds, defend territories, and often mate with multiple males who then incubate the eggs — a reversed arrangement unusual among North American birds.

311_American_White_Pelican.jpg.title: 311 American White Pelican
311_American_White_Pelican.jpg.description: Plate 311 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American White Pelican.
311_American_White_Pelican.jpg.story: The American White Pelican cooperates in shallow lines, herding fish into the shallows with synchronized wing-beats before scooping them up in expandable pouches. Unlike its brown cousin, it never plunge-dives. Audubon's enormous pelican required the full nine-foot wingspan of the double-elephant sheet to render at life size.

312_Long-tailed_Duck.jpg.title: 312 Long-tailed Duck
312_Long-tailed_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 312 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Long-tailed Duck.
312_Long-tailed_Duck.jpg.story: The Long-tailed Duck, once called Oldsquaw, dives deeper than any other duck — regularly below 200 feet — pursuing crustaceans on the ocean floor. Males bray a yodeling call across Arctic bays. Audubon's pair are set against open sea, a staging Havell mastered with layered aquatint.

313_Blue-Winged_Teal.jpg.title: 313 Blue-Winged Teal
313_Blue-Winged_Teal.jpg.description: Plate 313 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue-Winged Teal.
313_Blue-Winged_Teal.jpg.story: The Blue-winged Teal is the first duck to leave northern marshes in autumn, some reaching Peru by October. The sky-blue wing patch flashes only in flight, hidden at rest. Audubon's teal pair includes the male in full breeding plumage with its crescent white face mark.

314_Black-headed_Gull.jpg.title: 314 Black-headed Gull
314_Black-headed_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 314 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-headed Gull.
314_Black-headed_Gull.jpg.story: The Bonaparte's Gull — Audubon's Black-headed Gull — is a delicate tern-like gull that breeds in spruce trees, the only North American gull to regularly nest in conifers. Its dark hood is actually chocolate brown in good light. Audubon dedicated several American birds to Charles Lucien Bonaparte, the ornithologist nephew of Napoleon.

315_Red-breasted_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 315 Red-breasted Sandpiper
315_Red-breasted_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 315 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-breasted Sandpiper.
315_Red-breasted_Sandpiper.jpg.story: The Red Knot flies pole to pole each year, timing its spring stopover on Delaware Bay to the horseshoe crab spawn that fuels the final leg north. The rufa subspecies has declined sharply as crab eggs have grown scarcer. Audubon's plate shows both breeding and winter plumages, a practical concession for sprawling shorebird identification.

316_Black-bellied_Darter.jpg.title: 316 Black-bellied Darter
316_Black-bellied_Darter.jpg.description: Plate 316 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-bellied Darter.
316_Black-bellied_Darter.jpg.story: The Anhinga swims half-submerged with only its serpentine neck above water, earning the nickname 'snakebird.' Lacking waterproof feathers, it spreads its wings to dry on cypress branches after each dive. Audubon painted his darters in a Louisiana swamp crowded with Spanish moss, one of Havell's most atmospheric backgrounds.

317_Black,_or_Surf_Duck.jpg.title: 317 Black, or Surf Duck
317_Black,_or_Surf_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 317 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black, or Surf Duck.
317_Black,_or_Surf_Duck.jpg.story: The Surf Scoter rides breaking waves along the Atlantic and Pacific coasts, diving through surf for mussels. The male's black body and patchwork white-and-orange bill earned it the fisherman's name 'skunkhead.' Audubon's scoter pair floats on a frothing sea rendered in Havell's finest wave-work.

318_American_Avocet.jpg.title: 318 American Avocet
318_American_Avocet.jpg.description: Plate 318 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Avocet.
318_American_Avocet.jpg.story: The American Avocet sweeps its upcurved bill through shallow alkaline water, snapping up invertebrates by feel. During breeding the head and neck flush cinnamon, fading to gray by winter. Audubon's elegant, long-legged pair are among the folio's most graphic compositions.

319_Lesser_Tern.jpg.title: 319 Lesser Tern
319_Lesser_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 319 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Lesser Tern [Least Tern Sternula antillarum ].
319_Lesser_Tern.jpg.story: The Least Tern is North America's smallest tern, nesting on bare sand and pebble beaches. Breeding colonies on the Mississippi have largely moved to barge-dredged sandbars after natural sandbars disappeared behind dams. Audubon knew them darting along the Gulf, their hover-and-plunge smaller and quicker than any gull.

31_White-headed_Eagle.jpg.title: 31 White-headed Eagle
31_White-headed_Eagle.jpg.description: Plate 31 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-headed Eagle.
31_White-headed_Eagle.jpg.story: The Bald Eagle — Audubon's White-headed Eagle — pirates fish from ospreys as readily as it catches its own. Plate 31 is among the most reproduced images of Birds of America, printed at the double-elephant scale Audubon insisted upon so no bird needed shrinking. The 'double elephant' folio measured roughly 26 by 39 inches.

320_Little_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 320 Little Sandpiper
320_Little_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 320 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Little Sandpiper.
320_Little_Sandpiper.jpg.story: The Least Sandpiper is the smallest shorebird on earth, barely weighing an ounce, with yellow-green legs that distinguish it from similar peeps. Flocks twist in tight synchrony across mudflats. Audubon's trio is posed at the shoreline where Havell's colorists layered sepia washes over the stippled aquatint.

321_Roseate_Spoonbill.jpg.title: 321 Roseate Spoonbill
321_Roseate_Spoonbill.jpg.description: Plate 321 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Roseate Spoonbill.
321_Roseate_Spoonbill.jpg.story: The Roseate Spoonbill sweeps its paddle-shaped bill side to side through brackish shallows, snapping shut on shrimp and small fish by touch. Plume hunters nearly exterminated Florida's spoonbills by 1900 for the feather trade. Audubon's bird is among the folio's most striking plates, the pink body contrasting a stormy Gulf sky.

322_Red-headed_Duck.jpg.title: 322 Red-headed Duck
322_Red-headed_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 322 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-headed Duck.
322_Red-headed_Duck.jpg.story: The Redhead is a diving duck that often lays its eggs in the nests of Canvasbacks, letting the larger cousin raise them. Males sport a coppery red head that glows in low sun. Audubon painted both sexes on open water, a composition type Havell handled with layered tonal etching.

323_Black_Skimmer_or_Shearwater.jpg.title: 323 Black Skimmer or Shearwater
323_Black_Skimmer_or_Shearwater.jpg.description: Plate 323 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black Skimmer or Shearwater.
323_Black_Skimmer_or_Shearwater.jpg.story: The Black Skimmer slices the water's surface with a blade-thin lower mandible longer than the upper — unique among birds — snapping shut on any fish it strikes. Skimmers forage most actively at dusk and dawn when the water goes glassy. Audubon caught the low, scissoring flight just above a coastal bay.

324_Bonapartian_Gull.jpg.title: 324 Bonapartian Gull
324_Bonapartian_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 324 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bonapartian Gull.
324_Bonapartian_Gull.jpg.story: Bonaparte's Gull nests in black spruce trees across the boreal forest, often high off the ground — unusual behavior for a gull. The small, buoyant flier was named for Charles Lucien Bonaparte, who helped Audubon navigate Philadelphia's hostile scientific establishment.

325_Buffel-headed_Duck.jpg.title: 325 Buffel-headed Duck
325_Buffel-headed_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 325 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Buffel-headed Duck.
325_Buffel-headed_Duck.jpg.story: The Bufflehead is a compact diving duck that nests almost exclusively in old Northern Flicker cavities — a reliance so tight that its range tracks the woodpecker's across the boreal forest. The male's oversized puffy head shows an iridescent green-and-purple sheen that Havell's colorists rendered with overlaid washes of ink on the aquatint base.

326_Gannet.jpg.title: 326 Gannet
326_Gannet.jpg.description: Plate 326 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Gannet.
326_Gannet.jpg.story: The Northern Gannet plunges from 100 feet at 60 miles per hour, folding back its wings an instant before impact and slicing into shoals of herring. Air sacs beneath the skin cushion the blow. Audubon visited a Labrador gannet colony in 1833, part of the long expeditions that fueled the later plates.

327_Shoveller_Duck.jpg.title: 327 Shoveller Duck
327_Shoveller_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 327 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Shoveller Duck.
327_Shoveller_Duck.jpg.story: The Northern Shoveler's broad, spatulate bill is lined with comb-like lamellae that strain plankton and seeds from the water surface. Small groups sometimes swim in tight circles to create a central vortex that pulls food upward. Audubon's pair are shown dabbling heads-down at a pond edge.

328_Long-legged_Avocet.jpg.title: 328 Long-legged Avocet
328_Long-legged_Avocet.jpg.description: Plate 328 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Long-legged Avocet.
328_Long-legged_Avocet.jpg.story: The Black-necked Stilt wades on legs proportionally longer than any bird except the flamingo, picking insects from water surfaces with a fine, straight bill. Audubon grouped avocets and stilts together in this plate — they share a family despite different bills.

329_Yellow-breasted_Rail.jpg.title: 329 Yellow-breasted Rail
329_Yellow-breasted_Rail.jpg.description: Plate 329 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-breasted Rail.
329_Yellow-breasted_Rail.jpg.story: The Yellow Rail is one of North America's most secretive birds, hiding in wet sedge meadows and calling only at night with a metallic tick-tick-tick like stones striking. Most birders hear it without ever seeing one. Audubon's specimen was one of the few collected in his lifetime.

32_Black-billed_Cuckoo.jpg.title: 32 Black-billed Cuckoo
32_Black-billed_Cuckoo.jpg.description: Plate 32 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-billed Cuckoo.
32_Black-billed_Cuckoo.jpg.story: The Black-billed Cuckoo, like its yellow-billed cousin, gorges on hairy caterpillars during outbreak years, sometimes migrating hundreds of miles to follow them. It occasionally lays an egg in another species' nest — a rare hint of Old World cuckoo parasitism.

330_Ring_Plover.jpg.title: 330 Ring Plover
330_Ring_Plover.jpg.description: Plate 330 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ring Plover [Semipalmated Plover] Charadrius semipalmatus .
330_Ring_Plover.jpg.story: The Semipalmated Plover is the small, brown-backed plover of mudflats, its single dark breast band distinguishing it from the paler Piping Plover. 'Semipalmated' refers to the partial webbing between its toes. The species breeds across the Arctic and migrates to South America.

331_Goosander.jpg.title: 331 Goosander
331_Goosander.jpg.description: Plate 331 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Goosander.
331_Goosander.jpg.story: The Common Merganser, Audubon's Goosander, has a serrated sawbill for gripping fish and is sometimes called the 'sawbill' by river fishermen. Females nest in tree cavities; ducklings leap from heights of up to fifty feet on their first day. Audubon painted a paired drake and hen against a river landscape.

332_Pied_Duck.jpg.title: 332 Pied Duck
332_Pied_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 332 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pied Duck.
332_Pied_Duck.jpg.story: The Labrador Duck, Audubon's Pied Duck, is one of the few extinct North American birds depicted in Birds of America, vanishing around 1878 despite never having been especially common. Only about fifty specimens survive in museums. Audubon painted it from live and market birds he never saw on its breeding grounds.

333_Green_Heron.jpg.title: 333 Green Heron
333_Green_Heron.jpg.description: Plate 333 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Green Heron.
333_Green_Heron.jpg.story: The Green Heron is one of the few tool-using birds, dropping bread crusts, twigs, or insects onto the water to lure curious fish into striking range. Its neck looks absurdly short at rest and extends to full length in a heartbeat strike. Audubon's heron is posed in a marsh thick with lotus.

334_Black-bellied_Plover.jpg.title: 334 Black-bellied Plover
334_Black-bellied_Plover.jpg.description: Plate 334 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-bellied Plover.
334_Black-bellied_Plover.jpg.story: The Black-bellied Plover is the largest and hardiest plover, wintering farther north than any shorebird on both coasts. In breeding plumage its coal-black belly contrasts starkly with silvered back and white crown. Audubon shows both the striking breeding male and duller winter adult.

335_Red-breasted_Snipe.jpg.title: 335 Red-breasted Snipe
335_Red-breasted_Snipe.jpg.description: Plate 335 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-breasted Snipe.
335_Red-breasted_Snipe.jpg.story: The Short-billed Dowitcher feeds with a rapid sewing-machine motion, driving its long bill straight down through mudflats to find worms and small mollusks by touch. Audubon's 'Red-breasted Snipe' was indistinguishable in his era from the Long-billed Dowitcher, a cryptic species pair not formally split until 1950 on subtle call and molt differences.

336_Yellow-Crowned_Heron.jpg.title: 336 Yellow-Crowned Heron
336_Yellow-Crowned_Heron.jpg.description: Plate 336 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-Crowned Heron.
336_Yellow-Crowned_Heron.jpg.story: The Yellow-crowned Night-Heron specializes in crabs and crayfish, crushing hard shells with a stout bill unlike the slim dagger of most herons. It hunts day and night along southern shores. Audubon painted it against cypress knees in a swamp landscape typical of the folio's later plates.

337_American_Bittern.jpg.title: 337 American Bittern
337_American_Bittern.jpg.description: Plate 337 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Bittern.
337_American_Bittern.jpg.story: The American Bittern freezes with its bill skyward, vertical streaking blending with surrounding reeds — a camouflage so effective that birders often stare right through them. Its booming 'oonk-a-lunk' call, produced by gulping air, carries across marshes at dusk like a distant pump.

338_Bemaculated_Duck.jpg.title: 338 Bemaculated Duck
338_Bemaculated_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 338 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bemaculated Duck.
338_Bemaculated_Duck.jpg.story: Audubon's Bemaculated Duck is widely regarded today as a hybrid, possibly a Mallard crossed with another dabbler, mistakenly described as a new species. Several apparent discoveries in Birds of America turned out to be aberrant individuals, a common peril of working from freshly shot specimens.

339_Little_Auk.jpg.title: 339 Little Auk
339_Little_Auk.jpg.description: Plate 339 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Little Auk.
339_Little_Auk.jpg.story: The Dovekie is a starling-sized seabird that nests in dense colonies on Arctic scree slopes, sometimes numbering in the millions. It winters in open North Atlantic waters where storms occasionally blow weakened birds far inland. Audubon painted his Little Auks floating on choppy seas.

33_American_Goldfinch.jpg.title: 33 American Goldfinch
33_American_Goldfinch.jpg.description: Plate 33 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Goldfinch.
33_American_Goldfinch.jpg.story: The American Goldfinch is one of the strictest vegetarians among songbirds, feeding almost entirely on seeds and timing its late-summer nesting to the peak of thistle down. Plate 33 is an early, balanced composition from before Audubon's most theatrical narrative plates of the 1830s.

340_Least_Stormy-Petrel.jpg.title: 340 Least Stormy-Petrel
340_Least_Stormy-Petrel.jpg.description: Plate 340 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Least Stormy-Petrel.
340_Least_Stormy-Petrel.jpg.story: Storm-petrels patter across wave crests with dangling feet, picking plankton from the surface in the open ocean. They nest only on remote islands in rock crevices and visit the colony only at night to avoid predators. Audubon's 'Least' Stormy-Petrel corresponds to the Wilson's or the European Storm-Petrel.

341_Great_Auk.jpg.title: 341 Great Auk
341_Great_Auk.jpg.description: Plate 341 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Auk.
341_Great_Auk.jpg.story: The Great Auk was the original 'penguin' — a flightless, upright-walking diver of the North Atlantic — driven to extinction by 1844 when the last known breeding pair was strangled on an Icelandic skerry. Audubon painted it from museum specimens; he never saw a living bird. The plate is now one of the most poignant images in American ornithology.

342_Golden-Eye_Duck.jpg.title: 342 Golden-Eye Duck
342_Golden-Eye_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 342 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Golden-Eye Duck.
342_Golden-Eye_Duck.jpg.story: The Common Goldeneye whistles in flight — a clear, ringing sound from the wings that gunners used to call it the 'whistler.' It nests in tree cavities, often reusing pileated woodpecker holes. Audubon's pair floats on open water typical of Havell's mature bay compositions.

343_Ruddy_Duck.jpg.title: 343 Ruddy Duck
343_Ruddy_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 343 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ruddy Duck.
343_Ruddy_Duck.jpg.story: The Ruddy Duck male displays by slapping his sky-blue bill against his chestnut breast fast enough to beat bubbles into a froth. The stiff tail cocks vertically at the slightest alarm. Audubon's bold, small-bodied duck is painted against a plain water vignette.

344_Long-legged_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 344 Long-legged Sandpiper
344_Long-legged_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 344 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Long-legged Sandpiper.
344_Long-legged_Sandpiper.jpg.story: Audubon's Long-legged Sandpiper is now thought to be the Stilt Sandpiper, which feeds belly-deep with a head-down motion unusual among the peeps, probing mud with a long slightly downcurved bill. It breeds on Arctic tundra and winters as far south as Argentina, one of the long-haul migrants of the North American shorebird community.

345_American_Widgeon.jpg.title: 345 American Widgeon
345_American_Widgeon.jpg.description: Plate 345 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Widgeon.
345_American_Widgeon.jpg.story: The American Wigeon steals aquatic plants pulled up by diving ducks and coots, a kleptoparasitic habit that's earned it the nickname 'baldpate' for the white crown that gives away the thief. Audubon's green-masked drake is among the more ornate duck plates.

346_Black-Throated_Diver.jpg.title: 346 Black-Throated Diver
346_Black-Throated_Diver.jpg.description: Plate 346 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-Throated Diver.
346_Black-Throated_Diver.jpg.story: The Arctic Loon or Pacific Loon — likely the bird Audubon painted — breeds on tundra ponds and winters along northern coasts, diving for fish in frigid water. The velvet black throat patch shows purple in sun. Audubon drew from specimens sent by Arctic expedition naturalists.

347_Smew_or_White_Nun.jpg.title: 347 Smew or White Nun
347_Smew_or_White_Nun.jpg.description: Plate 347 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Smew or White Nun.
347_Smew_or_White_Nun.jpg.story: The Smew is a Eurasian sawbill that very rarely strays to North America; Audubon's inclusion reflects his ambition to paint every bird that might be called American. The male's cracked-ice pattern is unmistakable. Havell's engraving captures the sharp boundaries that no photo of the day could.

348_Gadwall_Duck.jpg.title: 348 Gadwall Duck
348_Gadwall_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 348 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Gadwall Duck.
348_Gadwall_Duck.jpg.story: The Gadwall is a subtly gorgeous duck — vermiculated gray with a black stern, the female nearly identical to a Mallard hen. Gadwalls sometimes steal food from diving coots, snatching pondweed as the coots surface. Audubon painted both sexes in a quiet marsh vignette.

349_Least_Water-hen.jpg.title: 349 Least Water-hen
349_Least_Water-hen.jpg.description: Plate 349 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Least Water-hen.
349_Least_Water-hen.jpg.story: The Black Rail is the smallest rail in North America, the size of a sparrow and nearly impossible to see in salt-marsh grass. Its 'kickee-doo' call comes mostly at night from April through June. Audubon's drawing is one of the earliest accurate renderings of the species.

34_Worm_eating_Warbler.jpg.title: 34 Worm eating Warbler
34_Worm_eating_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 34 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Worm eating Warbler.
34_Worm_eating_Warbler.jpg.story: The Worm-eating Warbler doesn't eat earthworms — it probes dead leaf clusters hanging in the understory for caterpillars, a foraging niche shared with no other North American warbler. Its buzzy trill resembles a Chipping Sparrow's, drifting from the slopes of steep hardwood ravines where it nests on the ground beneath overhanging roots.

350_Rocky_Mountain_Plover.jpg.title: 350 Rocky Mountain Plover
350_Rocky_Mountain_Plover.jpg.description: Plate 350 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Rocky Mountain Plover.
350_Rocky_Mountain_Plover.jpg.story: The Mountain Plover nests on short-grass prairie and bare, baked ground, not in mountains at all — the name was bestowed after a specimen was collected near the Rockies. Audubon painted it from a skin sent by Thomas Nuttall, whose western expeditions supplied many later plates.

351_Great_Cinereous_Owl.jpg.title: 351 Great Cinereous Owl
351_Great_Cinereous_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 351 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Cinereous Owl.
351_Great_Cinereous_Owl.jpg.story: The Great Gray Owl looks massive but weighs less than a Great Horned Owl — most of its bulk is fluffed feathers insulating it through boreal winters. Its facial disc funnels sound so precisely that it can strike voles beneath two feet of snow. Audubon's stately bird reflects the folio's shift toward larger, more dramatic single-bird compositions in its later volumes.

352_Black-Winged_Hawk.jpg.title: 352 Black-Winged Hawk
352_Black-Winged_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 352 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-Winged Hawk.
352_Black-Winged_Hawk.jpg.story: The White-tailed Kite hovers above grassland with slow, easy wingbeats, dropping on voles like a pale falcon. Once nearly extirpated from California by shooting, it has recovered and expanded east. Audubon called it the Black-winged Hawk after the bold shoulder patches.

353_I._Chesnut-backed_Titmouse_-_2._Blac.title: 353 I. Chesnut-backed Titmouse - 2. Black-capt Titmouse - 3. Chesnut-crowned Titmouse
353_I._Chesnut-backed_Titmouse_-_2._Blac.description: Plate 353 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Chesnut-backed Titmouse - . Black-capt Titmouse - . Chesnut-crowned Titmouse.

source.canonical_title: File:353 I. Chesnut-backed Titmouse - 2. Black-capt Titmouse - 3. Chesnut-crowned Titmouse.jpg

353_I._Chesnut-backed_Titmouse_-_2._Blac.story: Audubon grouped three chickadees and tits on one plate: the Chestnut-backed Chickadee of Pacific rainforests, the familiar Black-capped Chickadee, and a third chestnut-crowned form. Such composite plates appear throughout the later volumes as Audubon gathered species from Nuttall and Townsend's western expeditions.

354_I._Louisiana_Tanager_-_2._Scarlet_Ta.title: 354 I. Louisiana Tanager - 2. Scarlet Tanager
354_I._Louisiana_Tanager_-_2._Scarlet_Ta.description: Plate 354 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Louisiana Tanager - . Scarlet Tanager.

source.canonical_title: File:354 I. Louisiana Tanager - 2. Scarlet Tanager.jpg

354_I._Louisiana_Tanager_-_2._Scarlet_Ta.story: The Louisiana Tanager — now the Western Tanager — winters in Mexico and nests in high conifer forests with a flame-orange face unique among North American tanagers. Audubon paired it with its eastern cousin the Scarlet Tanager on a single plate, a side-by-side trick used for ocean-to-ocean species comparisons.

355_MacGillivray's_Finch.jpg.title: 355 MacGillivray's Finch
355_MacGillivray's_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 355 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting MacGillivray's Finch.
355_MacGillivray's_Finch.jpg.story: Audubon named this sparrow — now classified as a Seaside Sparrow variant — for his friend William MacGillivray, the Scottish ornithologist who edited much of the accompanying Ornithological Biography text. MacGillivray never traveled to America but polished Audubon's prose into publishable English.

356_Marsh_Hawk.jpg.title: 356 Marsh Hawk
356_Marsh_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 356 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Marsh Hawk.
356_Marsh_Hawk.jpg.story: The Northern Harrier, Audubon's Marsh Hawk, courses low over grasslands with its wings held in a shallow V, relying on an owl-like facial disc to locate voles by sound. Males are gray, females and juveniles brown, and the species is one of the few raptors with obvious sex differences.

357_American_Magpie.jpg.title: 357 American Magpie
357_American_Magpie.jpg.description: Plate 357 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Magpie.
357_American_Magpie.jpg.story: The Black-billed Magpie builds elaborate domed stick nests with roofs and multiple entrances, sometimes returning to extend old nests year after year. Lewis and Clark brought the first western specimens east in 1804. Audubon's pair flaunts the long iridescent tail across nearly the full plate.

358_Pine_Grosbeak.jpg.title: 358 Pine Grosbeak
358_Pine_Grosbeak.jpg.description: Plate 358 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pine Grosbeak.
358_Pine_Grosbeak.jpg.story: The Pine Grosbeak is a lumbering, tame finch of boreal conifer forests, famous for letting observers walk within arm's reach. Irruptions push flocks south into the northern states every few winters when cone crops fail. Audubon's red male and yellow-olive female share the plate.

359_I._Arkansaw_Flycatcher_-_2._Swallow-.title: 359 I. Arkansaw Flycatcher - 2. Swallow-Tailed Flycatcher - 3. Says Flycatcher
359_I._Arkansaw_Flycatcher_-_2._Swallow-.description: Plate 359 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Arkansaw Flycatcher - . Swallow-Tailed Flycatcher - . Says Flycatcher.

source.canonical_title: File:359 I. Arkansaw Flycatcher - 2. Swallow-Tailed Flycatcher - 3. Says Flycatcher.jpg

359_I._Arkansaw_Flycatcher_-_2._Swallow-.story: Three western flycatchers share the plate: the Western Kingbird (Audubon's Arkansaw Flycatcher), the spectacular Scissor-tailed Flycatcher, and Say's Phoebe. All three were named for naturalists of the Long Expedition to the Rockies in 1819-20 — a journey whose collections fed much of the folio's later continental reach.

35_Children's_Warbler.jpg.title: 35 Children's Warbler
35_Children's_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 35 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Children's Warbler.
35_Children's_Warbler.jpg.story: Children's Warbler is almost certainly a female or juvenile Yellow Warbler, misidentified by Audubon and named for his London publisher John George Children, keeper of zoology at the British Museum. Several 'new' species described from single specimens in Birds of America were later shown to be misinterpreted plumages, a common risk in early ornithology.

360_I._Winter_Wren_-_2._Rock_Wren.jpg.title: 360 I. Winter Wren - 2. Rock Wren
360_I._Winter_Wren_-_2._Rock_Wren.jpg.description: Plate 360 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Winter Wren - . Rock Wren.

source.canonical_title: File:360 I. Winter Wren - 2. Rock Wren.jpg

360_I._Winter_Wren_-_2._Rock_Wren.jpg.story: The Winter Wren packs an astonishing ten seconds of silvery trills into one song, more notes per second than nearly any songbird. The Rock Wren builds a stone pathway of pebbles leading to its crevice nest. Audubon assembled this pair from eastern field observation and western Townsend specimens.

361_Long-tailed_or_Dusky_Grous.jpg.title: 361 Long-tailed or Dusky Grous
361_Long-tailed_or_Dusky_Grous.jpg.description: Plate 361 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Long-tailed or Dusky Grous.
361_Long-tailed_or_Dusky_Grous.jpg.story: The Dusky Grouse struts along coniferous ridges where males inflate cervical air sacs to produce deep, slow booming that echoes between Douglas-firs. The species was one of many Audubon described from skins brought east by Townsend after his 1834 Oregon expedition.

362_I._Yellow_billed_Magpie_-_2._Steller.title: 362 I. Yellow billed Magpie - 2. Stellers Jay - 3. Ultramarine Jay - 4. Clark's Crow
362_I._Yellow_billed_Magpie_-_2._Steller.description: Plate 362 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Yellow billed Magpie - . Stellers Jay - . Ultramarine Jay - . Clark's Crow.

source.canonical_title: File:362 I. Yellow billed Magpie - 2. Stellers Jay - 3. Ultramarine Jay - 4. Clark's Crow.jpg

362_I._Yellow_billed_Magpie_-_2._Steller.story: Four corvids of the American West share this plate: the California-endemic Yellow-billed Magpie, the dark-crested Steller's Jay, a 'Ultramarine' Jay, and Clark's Nutcracker, which caches tens of thousands of pine seeds each autumn. Audubon worked entirely from Nuttall and Townsend's western specimens to compile this New World corvid survey.

363_Bohemian_Chatterer.jpg.title: 363 Bohemian Chatterer
363_Bohemian_Chatterer.jpg.description: Plate 363 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bohemian Chatterer.
363_Bohemian_Chatterer.jpg.story: The Bohemian Waxwing roams nomadically across boreal forests in winter, descending on berry-laden mountain-ash and crabapples in flocks that sometimes hit windows while drunk on fermented fruit. Audubon's 'Chatterer' captures the waxy red tips on the secondaries — actual concentrations of the pigment astaxanthin.

364_White-winged_Crossbill.jpg.title: 364 White-winged Crossbill
364_White-winged_Crossbill.jpg.description: Plate 364 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-winged Crossbill.
364_White-winged_Crossbill.jpg.story: The White-winged Crossbill's peculiar mandibles pry apart spruce cones to extract seeds, and the bill crosses one way in half the population and the other way in the rest. Flocks wander thousands of miles following cone crops. Audubon placed males and females on a spruce branch in his characteristic pose.

365_Lapland_Long-spur.jpg.title: 365 Lapland Long-spur
365_Lapland_Long-spur.jpg.description: Plate 365 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Lapland Long-spur.
365_Lapland_Long-spur.jpg.story: The Lapland Longspur is a circumpolar Arctic breeder that winters in stubble fields and roadside shoulders across the Great Plains in swirling brown flocks. Its name comes from the long hind claw that gives grip on frozen tundra. Audubon likely worked from Nuttall's subarctic specimens.

366_Iceland_or_Jer_Falcon.jpg.title: 366 Iceland or Jer Falcon
366_Iceland_or_Jer_Falcon.jpg.description: Plate 366 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Iceland or Jer Falcon.
366_Iceland_or_Jer_Falcon.jpg.story: The Gyrfalcon is the largest falcon on earth, a master of Arctic cliffs and snowbound seabird colonies. Medieval falconers reserved the white form exclusively for kings. Audubon painted the Gyrfalcon grandly; Havell's double-elephant sheet was just large enough for a life-sized image of its four-foot wingspan.

367_Band-tailed_Pigeon.jpg.title: 367 Band-tailed Pigeon
367_Band-tailed_Pigeon.jpg.description: Plate 367 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Band-tailed Pigeon.
367_Band-tailed_Pigeon.jpg.story: The Band-tailed Pigeon is the heaviest native pigeon and the closest surviving relative of the extinct Passenger Pigeon, which once formed flocks Audubon watched darken the sky for days. Band-tails travel in smaller bands through western oak and madrone woodland.

368_Rock_Grous.jpg.title: 368 Rock Grous
368_Rock_Grous.jpg.description: Plate 368 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Rock Grous.
368_Rock_Grous.jpg.story: The Rock Ptarmigan turns pure white in winter, each toe feathered for insulation and snowshoe lift. It molts three times a year to match Arctic seasons — a trick shared by few birds on earth. Audubon worked from specimens sent by Hudson's Bay Company collectors.

369_I._Mountain_Mocking_bird_-_2._Varied.title: 369 I. Mountain Mocking bird - 2. Varied Thrush
369_I._Mountain_Mocking_bird_-_2._Varied.description: Plate 369 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Mountain Mocking bird - . Varied Thrush.

source.canonical_title: File:369 I. Mountain Mocking bird - 2. Varied Thrush.jpg

369_I._Mountain_Mocking_bird_-_2._Varied.story: The Sage Thrasher (Audubon's Mountain Mocking-bird) is the smallest North American thrasher, bounding low across sagebrush steppe with a surprisingly elaborate song. The Varied Thrush is a ghostly orange-and-gray relative of the robin, haunting dripping Pacific rainforests with a single sustained buzzing note. Both arrived at Audubon's studio as Townsend expedition skins from Oregon.

36_Stanley_Hawk.jpg.title: 36 Stanley Hawk
36_Stanley_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 36 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Stanley Hawk.
36_Stanley_Hawk.jpg.story: The Stanley Hawk is today's Cooper's Hawk, a long-tailed forest accipiter that ambushes songbirds in explosive chases through dense cover. Audubon named it for Edward Smith-Stanley, the 13th Earl of Derby, whose patronage and private menagerie supported much nineteenth-century British natural history. The Earl's support was vital in securing London subscribers to the folio.

370_American_Water_Ouzel.jpg.title: 370 American Water Ouzel
370_American_Water_Ouzel.jpg.description: Plate 370 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Water Ouzel.
370_American_Water_Ouzel.jpg.story: The American Dipper walks underwater on mountain stream beds, gripping stones with its claws and using its wings as paddles to pursue aquatic insect larvae. Oil glands secrete enough waterproofing to repel ice water. Audubon painted a pair on a fast-running Rockies creek.

371_Cock_of_the_Plains.jpg.title: 371 Cock of the Plains
371_Cock_of_the_Plains.jpg.description: Plate 371 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Cock of the Plains.
371_Cock_of_the_Plains.jpg.story: The Greater Sage-Grouse's lek displays involve yellow air sacs bursting from the breast with a popping 'plop' audible a mile off. The species depends entirely on sagebrush, which fuels it year-round — a monoculture diet that has made it vulnerable to range fragmentation.

372_Common_Buzzard.jpg.title: 372 Common Buzzard
372_Common_Buzzard.jpg.description: Plate 372 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Common Buzzard.
372_Common_Buzzard.jpg.story: The Common Buzzard is Europe's rough equivalent of the Red-tailed Hawk, soaring on broad wings over woodland edges, and Audubon's specimen was likely an accidental North Atlantic inclusion or a misidentified Red-shouldered or juvenile Red-tailed. Birds of America occasionally reached beyond American natives in its ambition to be comprehensive across the continent.

373_I._Evening_Grosbeak_-_2._Spotted_Gro.title: 373 I. Evening Grosbeak - 2. Spotted Grosbeak
373_I._Evening_Grosbeak_-_2._Spotted_Gro.description: Plate 373 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Evening Grosbeak - . Spotted Grosbeak.

source.canonical_title: File:373 I. Evening Grosbeak - 2. Spotted Grosbeak.jpg

373_I._Evening_Grosbeak_-_2._Spotted_Gro.story: The Evening Grosbeak descends on winter feeders like a swarm of stout yellow-and-black finches, cracking sunflower seeds with a bill strong enough to crush cherry pits. 'Evening' stuck from a misconception that they sang only at dusk. The Spotted Grosbeak is the Black-headed Grosbeak of the West.

374_Sharp-shinned_Hawk.jpg.title: 374 Sharp-shinned Hawk
374_Sharp-shinned_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 374 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Sharp-shinned Hawk.
374_Sharp-shinned_Hawk.jpg.story: The Sharp-shinned Hawk is a small, agile accipiter that ambushes songbirds at feeders in explosive, wing-slashing pursuits through shrubbery. Females outweigh males by half — one of the largest sex differences in any bird. Audubon painted the pair in aerial action on plate 374.

375_Lesser_Red-Poll.jpg.title: 375 Lesser Red-Poll
375_Lesser_Red-Poll.jpg.description: Plate 375 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Lesser Red-Poll.
375_Lesser_Red-Poll.jpg.story: The Common Redpoll survives Arctic winters by shoving seeds into an esophageal pouch to digest slowly through subzero nights. Irruption years bring flocks flooding into the northern states whenever birch catkin crops fail. Audubon's plate displays both the rosy-breasted male and duller female.

376_Trumpeter_Swan.jpg.title: 376 Trumpeter Swan
376_Trumpeter_Swan.jpg.description: Plate 376 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Trumpeter Swan.
376_Trumpeter_Swan.jpg.story: The Trumpeter Swan is North America's largest waterfowl, with an eight-foot wingspan and a brassy call from the long, looped windpipe of its sternum. It was nearly extinct by 1900, its skins sold as 'swansdown' for powder puffs. Audubon's enormous plate fills the double-elephant sheet.

377_Scolopaceus_Courlan.jpg.title: 377 Scolopaceus Courlan
377_Scolopaceus_Courlan.jpg.description: Plate 377 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Scolopaceus Courlan.
377_Scolopaceus_Courlan.jpg.story: The Limpkin wades through Florida swamps hunting apple snails, which it extracts from the shell with a slightly curved bill. Its weird, wailing cry — often called the sound of the swamp at night — has been dubbed into countless jungle movies. The species is the sole member of its family.

378_Hawk_Owl.jpg.title: 378 Hawk Owl
378_Hawk_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 378 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hawk Owl.
378_Hawk_Owl.jpg.story: The Northern Hawk Owl is the most diurnal owl in North America, hunting in full daylight from conifer tops with a long-tailed, falcon-like silhouette. It can spot prey from half a mile away. Audubon's specimen likely came from Hudson's Bay Company agents in the Canadian subarctic.

379_Ruff-necked_Humming-bird.jpg.title: 379 Ruff-necked Humming-bird
379_Ruff-necked_Humming-bird.jpg.description: Plate 379 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ruff-necked Humming-bird.
379_Ruff-necked_Humming-bird.jpg.story: The Rufous Hummingbird makes one of the longest migrations relative to body size of any bird — a two-inch flier traveling four thousand miles from Alaska to Mexico annually.
