Males dive-display with a metallic whine from the tail feathers. Audubon painted multiple individuals on flowering branches.

37_Golden-winged_Woodpecker.jpg.title: 37 Golden-winged Woodpecker
37_Golden-winged_Woodpecker.jpg.description: Plate 37 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Golden-winged Woodpecker.
37_Golden-winged_Woodpecker.jpg.story: The Northern Flicker is a ground-feeding woodpecker, hammering anthills apart and extracting ants with a barbed tongue that extends three inches beyond the bill tip. Golden-winged refers to the eastern 'yellow-shafted' form, whose underwings flash saffron in flight. Audubon painted both male and female, the male distinguished by a neat black moustache stripe.

380_Tengmalm's_Owl.jpg.title: 380 Tengmalm's Owl
380_Tengmalm's_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 380 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tengmalm's Owl.
380_Tengmalm's_Owl.jpg.story: The Boreal Owl, called Tengmalm's Owl in Europe, is a small, strictly nocturnal predator of northern spruce forests that nests in old pileated-woodpecker cavities. Males lure females to potential nest holes and sing rapid whistled trills through subarctic spring nights.

381_Snow_Goose.jpg.title: 381 Snow Goose
381_Snow_Goose.jpg.description: Plate 381 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Snow Goose.
381_Snow_Goose.jpg.story: Snow Geese migrate in swirling V-shaped flocks sometimes exceeding 100,000 birds, their cries blending into a continuous roar audible miles off. Audubon painted both the white and the 'blue' color morphs, once thought separate species. Plate 381 fills the sheet with a skein of birds against gray wetlands.

382_Sharp-tailed_Grous.jpg.title: 382 Sharp-tailed Grous
382_Sharp-tailed_Grous.jpg.description: Plate 382 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Sharp-tailed Grous.
382_Sharp-tailed_Grous.jpg.story: The Sharp-tailed Grouse dances on communal leks where males stamp in tight circles, rattle stiff tail feathers, and inflate purple throat sacs in synchronized competitive display through the spring dawn. The species depends on mosaic prairie — brush, grass, and burned patches together — a habitat fragmented by modern cropping.

383_Long-eared_Owl.jpg.title: 383 Long-eared Owl
383_Long-eared_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 383 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Long-eared Owl.
383_Long-eared_Owl.jpg.story: The Long-eared Owl roosts by day in dense conifers, elongating itself against the trunk with ear tufts raised until it nearly vanishes into the bark. At night it quarters open grasslands and marshes for voles, hunting silently on long wings. Audubon's elegant upright portrait captures the species' slim, vertical daytime posture.

384_Black-Throated_Bunting.jpg.title: 384 Black-Throated Bunting
384_Black-Throated_Bunting.jpg.description: Plate 384 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black-Throated Bunting.
384_Black-Throated_Bunting.jpg.story: The Dickcissel, Audubon's Black-throated Bunting, sings its own name from fencerows across the tallgrass prairie before gathering into enormous winter flocks in the Venezuelan llanos that locals shoot as crop pests. The species' east-of-Rockies range has shifted west over the last century.

385_I._Bank_Swallow_-_2._Violet-green_Sw.title: 385 I. Bank Swallow - 2. Violet-green Swallow
385_I._Bank_Swallow_-_2._Violet-green_Sw.description: Plate 385 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Bank Swallow - . Violet-green Swallow.

source.canonical_title: File:385 I. Bank Swallow - 2. Violet-green Swallow.jpg

385_I._Bank_Swallow_-_2._Violet-green_Sw.story: Bank Swallows excavate honeycomb colonies in riverbank cliffs, each pair digging a three-foot tunnel to a nest chamber. The Violet-green Swallow glitters emerald and purple above western ponderosa pine forests. Audubon painted both on a single plate as continental cousins.

386_White_Heron.jpg.title: 386 White Heron
386_White_Heron.jpg.description: Plate 386 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White Heron.
386_White_Heron.jpg.story: The Great Egret was nearly exterminated by plume hunters in the 1890s to supply hatmakers with breeding-season aigrettes. Audubon's grand white bird was painted long before the slaughter, during a period when Florida rookeries still spanned acres. The species' recovery helped spur the founding of the Audubon Society in 1896.

387_Glossy_Ibis.jpg.title: 387 Glossy Ibis
387_Glossy_Ibis.jpg.description: Plate 387 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Glossy Ibis.
387_Glossy_Ibis.jpg.story: The Glossy Ibis looks black from a distance but gleams bronze, purple, and green in direct sun. It probes mudflats for crayfish and aquatic insects with a long downcurved bill. The species spread into North America from Africa and Europe, likely crossing via West Indian islands in the nineteenth century.

388_I._Nuttall's_Starling_-_2._Yellow-he.title: 388 I. Nuttall's Starling - 2. Yellow-headed Troopial - 3. Bullock's Oriole
388_I._Nuttall's_Starling_-_2._Yellow-he.description: Plate 388 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Nuttall's Starling - . Yellow-headed Troopial - . Bullock's Oriole.

source.canonical_title: File:388 I. Nuttall's Starling - 2. Yellow-headed Troopial - 3. Bullock's Oriole.jpg

388_I._Nuttall's_Starling_-_2._Yellow-he.story: Three western blackbirds share the plate: Nuttall's Starling (the Western Meadowlark), the booming Yellow-headed Blackbird of prairie marshes, and Bullock's Oriole of cottonwood galleries. Audubon honored both Thomas Nuttall and William Bullock, whose western collecting filled the folio's late plates.

389_Red-Cockaded_Woodpecker.jpg.title: 389 Red-Cockaded Woodpecker
389_Red-Cockaded_Woodpecker.jpg.description: Plate 389 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-Cockaded Woodpecker.
389_Red-Cockaded_Woodpecker.jpg.story: The Red-cockaded Woodpecker excavates nest cavities only in living, old-growth longleaf pines infected with red heart rot — a razor-narrow requirement that has made it a federally endangered species as the southeastern pine forest was logged to remnant stands. Family groups cooperate to raise young, with older offspring helping parents tend the nest.

38_Kentucky_Warbler.jpg.title: 38 Kentucky Warbler
38_Kentucky_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 38 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Kentucky Warbler.
38_Kentucky_Warbler.jpg.story: The Kentucky Warbler walks along the shaded forest floor like a miniature thrush, flicking leaves to expose caterpillars. Its loud 'churry churry churry' rings from rich bottomland woods. The male's black sideburns and yellow spectacles give him a distinctive face Audubon rendered with care.

390_I._Lark_Finch_-_2._Prairie_Finch_-_3.title: 390 I. Lark Finch - 2. Prairie Finch - 3. Brown Song Sparrow
390_I._Lark_Finch_-_2._Prairie_Finch_-_3.description: Plate 390 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Lark Finch - . Prairie Finch - . Brown Song Sparrow.

source.canonical_title: File:390 I. Lark Finch - 2. Prairie Finch - 3. Brown Song Sparrow.jpg

390_I._Lark_Finch_-_2._Prairie_Finch_-_3.story: Three open-country sparrows of the West share the plate: the Lark Sparrow with its harlequin face, the Lark Bunting (Colorado's state bird), and a regional Song Sparrow. Audubon's composite gathers multiple species into efficient single plates, helping the later volumes wrap up the continental project.

391_Brant_Goose.jpg.title: 391 Brant Goose
391_Brant_Goose.jpg.description: Plate 391 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Brant Goose.
391_Brant_Goose.jpg.story: The Brant is a small, stocky sea goose that winters on eelgrass beds along both coasts and suffered catastrophic declines in the 1930s when an eelgrass blight wiped out most Atlantic beds. Audubon's plate shows the dark-bellied Atlantic form feeding in a tidal flat.

392_Louisiana_Hawk.jpg.title: 392 Louisiana Hawk
392_Louisiana_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 392 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Louisiana Hawk.
392_Louisiana_Hawk.jpg.story: Harris's Hawk — Audubon's Louisiana Hawk — hunts cooperatively in family groups, flushing jackrabbits and taking turns at capture. Such pack-hunting is unique among North American raptors. Audubon named the species for his friend Edward Harris, who accompanied him on the 1843 Missouri River expedition.

393_I._Townsend's_Warbler_-_2._Arctic_Bl.title: 393 I. Townsend's Warbler - 2. Arctic Blue-bird - 3. Western Blue-bird
393_I._Townsend's_Warbler_-_2._Arctic_Bl.description: Plate 393 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Townsend's Warbler - . Arctic Blue-bird - . Western Blue-bird.

source.canonical_title: File:393 I. Townsend's Warbler - 2. Arctic Blue-bird - 3. Western Blue-bird.jpg

393_I._Townsend's_Warbler_-_2._Arctic_Bl.story: Townsend's Warbler nests in the tops of Pacific Northwest old growth Douglas-firs, where its buzzy song rarely descends to observers. The Mountain Bluebird (Arctic Blue-bird) hovers while hunting insects over sagebrush, and the Western Bluebird nests in abandoned woodpecker holes. All three came to Audubon through John Kirk Townsend's Oregon expedition.

394_I._Chestnut-coloured_Finch_-_2._Blac.title: 394 I. Chestnut-coloured Finch - 2. Black-headed Siskin - 3. Black crown Bunting - 4. Arctic Ground Finch
394_I._Chestnut-coloured_Finch_-_2._Blac.description: Plate 394 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Chestnut-coloured Finch - . Black-headed Siskin - . Black crown Bunting - . Arctic Ground Finch.

source.canonical_title: File:394 I. Chestnut-coloured Finch - 2. Black-headed Siskin - 3. Black crown Bunting - 4. Arctic Ground Finch.jpg

394_I._Chestnut-coloured_Finch_-_2._Blac.story: Four sparrows assembled from western collections crowd this late plate: a Chestnut-collared Longspur, a siskin, a Lapland Longspur, and a ground finch. Audubon often grouped small drab birds together to compress the plate count as the folio neared its planned 435 total.

395_I._Audubon's_Warbler_-_2._Hermit_War.title: 395 I. Audubon's Warbler - 2. Hermit Warbler - 3. Black-throated gray Warbler
395_I._Audubon's_Warbler_-_2._Hermit_War.description: Plate 395 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Audubon's Warbler ( Setophaga auduboni ) - . Hermit Warbler ( Setophaga occidentalis ) - . Black-throated Gray Warbler ( Setophaga nigrescens ).

source.canonical_title: File:395 I. Audubon's Warbler - 2. Hermit Warbler - 3. Black-throated gray Warbler.jpg

395_I._Audubon's_Warbler_-_2._Hermit_War.story: The western-lumped Yellow-rumped Warbler was originally split as Audubon's Warbler, retaining a yellow throat patch the eastern Myrtle lacks. The Hermit Warbler nests in tall Pacific firs, and the Black-throated Gray flickers through western oaks. Audubon included his self-named species among his very last published plates.

396_Burgomaster_Gull.jpg.title: 396 Burgomaster Gull
396_Burgomaster_Gull.jpg.description: Plate 396 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Burgomaster Gull.
396_Burgomaster_Gull.jpg.story: The Glaucous Gull, Audubon's Burgomaster, is a massive, pale Arctic gull that pirates food from puffins, eiders, and even other gulls at breeding cliffs, and cleans carcasses on polar shores. 'Burgomaster' is Dutch for 'mayor,' a sailor's epithet reflecting its imposing, domineering presence on the northern seas whalers sailed.

397_Scarlet_Ibis.jpg.title: 397 Scarlet Ibis
397_Scarlet_Ibis.jpg.description: Plate 397 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Scarlet Ibis.
397_Scarlet_Ibis.jpg.story: The Scarlet Ibis gets its vivid red plumage from crab and shrimp carotenoids in the diet — captive birds fade to pink without the right food. It occurs naturally in Venezuela and the Guianas and reaches the United States only as a rare stray. Audubon's flame-colored plate is one of the folio's visual showpieces.

398_I._Lazuli_Finch_-_2._Clay-coloured_F.title: 398 I. Lazuli Finch - 2. Clay-coloured Finch - 3. Oregon Snow Finch
398_I._Lazuli_Finch_-_2._Clay-coloured_F.description: Plate 398 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Lazuli Finch - . Clay-coloured Finch - . Oregon Snow Finch.

source.canonical_title: File:398 I. Lazuli Finch - 2. Clay-coloured Finch - 3. Oregon Snow Finch.jpg

398_I._Lazuli_Finch_-_2._Clay-coloured_F.story: Three prairie and western buntings appear here: the brilliant turquoise Lazuli Bunting, the nondescript Clay-colored Sparrow of northern shrublands, and a dark-eyed Junco form. Like many late plates, this one pulled together species from Nuttall and Townsend's continental specimens — birds Audubon himself never saw in life.

399_I._Black-throated_green_Warbler_-_2..title: 399 I. Black-throated green Warbler - 2. Blackburnian - 3. Mourning Warbler
399_I._Black-throated_green_Warbler_-_2..description: Plate 399 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Black-throated green Warbler - . Blackburnian - . Mourning Warbler.

source.canonical_title: File:399 I. Black-throated green Warbler - 2. Blackburnian - 3. Mourning Warbler.jpg

399_I._Black-throated_green_Warbler_-_2..story: A trio of spring migrants: the Black-throated Green's buzzy zee-zee-zee-zoo-zee drifts from hemlocks, the Blackburnian's orange throat flares like ember in the canopy, and the skulking Mourning Warbler slinks through brambles. Plate 399 belongs to the late London run engraved by Robert Havell Jr., who took over the aquatints after William Lizars withdrew in 1827.

39_Crested_Titmouse.jpg.title: 39 Crested Titmouse
39_Crested_Titmouse.jpg.description: Plate 39 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Crested Titmouse.
39_Crested_Titmouse.jpg.story: Now called the Tufted Titmouse, this pert gray sprite hoards sunflower seeds and wedges acorns into bark to hammer them open. It lines its nest with fur plucked straight from living squirrels and opossums. Plate 39 was among the earliest aquatints engraved in London by Robert Havell Jr., whose workshop took over the project that year.

3_Prothonotary_Warbler_restored.jpg.title: 3 Prothonotary Warbler restored
3_Prothonotary_Warbler_restored.jpg.description: Plate 3 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Prothonotary Warbler.
3_Prothonotary_Warbler_restored.jpg.story: The only eastern warbler that nests in tree cavities, the Prothonotary glows saffron-gold against blackwater swamps where it hunts caterpillars over still water. Its name honors the yellow-robed papal clerks of the Roman Curia. This restored scan comes from the double-elephant folio, each sheet measuring roughly 39 by 26 inches to accommodate life-size figures.

400_I._Arkansaw_Siskin_-_2._Mealy_Red-po.title: 400 I. Arkansaw Siskin - 2. Mealy Red-poll - 3. Louisiana Tanager - 4.Townsend's Finch - 5. Buff-breasted Finch
400_I._Arkansaw_Siskin_-_2._Mealy_Red-po.description: Plate 400 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Arkansaw Siskin - . Mealy Red-poll - . Louisiana Tanager - .Townsend's Finch - . Buff-breasted Finch.

source.canonical_title: File:400 I. Arkansaw Siskin - 2. Mealy Red-poll - 3. Louisiana Tanager - 4.Townsend's Finch - 5. Buff-breasted Finch.jpg

400_I._Arkansaw_Siskin_-_2._Mealy_Red-po.story: A composite of western finches Audubon never saw alive, including what we now call the Western Tanager and Lesser Goldfinch. He worked from skins supplied by John Kirk Townsend and Thomas Nuttall after their 1834 overland expedition. Plate 400 marked a milestone: Havell's shop had now pulled four hundred aquatints for the subscription.

401_Red-breasted_Merganser.jpg.title: 401 Red-breasted Merganser
401_Red-breasted_Merganser.jpg.description: Plate 401 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-breasted Merganser.
401_Red-breasted_Merganser.jpg.story: A sleek sawbill with serrated edges on its bill for gripping slippery fish, the Red-breasted Merganser clocks the fastest level flight ever measured in a duck, roughly 80 mph. The males sport a ragged double crest that gives them a windblown look. Havell's aquatint softens the drake's metallic green head into a smoky bottle-glass sheen.

402_I._Black-throated_Guillemot_-_2._Nob.title: 402 I. Black-throated Guillemot - 2. Nobbed-billed Auk - 3. Curled-crested Auk - 4. Horned-billed Guillemot
402_I._Black-throated_Guillemot_-_2._Nob.description: Plate 402 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Black-throated Guillemot - . Nobbed-billed Auk - . Curled-crested Auk - . Horned-billed Guillemot.

source.canonical_title: File:402 I. Black-throated Guillemot - 2. Nobbed-billed Auk - 3. Curled-crested Auk - 4. Horned-billed Guillemot.jpg

402_I._Black-throated_Guillemot_-_2._Nob.story: Four North Pacific alcids crowd this plate: auklets and murrelets that whirr past sea cliffs on stubby wings. Audubon never went west; these skins came from the Columbia River via Townsend and Nuttall. Havell engraved the rocky backdrop from sketches, a common practice when the artist worked only from preserved specimens.

403_Golden-eye_Duck.jpg.title: 403 Golden-eye Duck
403_Golden-eye_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 403 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Golden-eye Duck.
403_Golden-eye_Duck.jpg.story: The Common Goldeneye's whistling wingbeats earned it the nickname whistler among market hunters. Drakes throw their heads backward in a theatrical courtship display, almost touching their rumps. Audubon painted this plate late in the project, when Havell's aquatint technique had grown confident enough to render a shimmering golden iris in just two bites of acid.

404_Eared_Grebe.jpg.title: 404 Eared Grebe
404_Eared_Grebe.jpg.description: Plate 404 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Eared Grebe.
404_Eared_Grebe.jpg.story: The Eared Grebe stages the largest flightless gathering of any North American bird, hundreds of thousands molting on Mono Lake and the Great Salt Lake each fall. It doubles its body weight on brine shrimp before resuming migration. Audubon placed this western species in his final volume, filling gaps using skins collected by expedition naturalists.

405_Semipalmated_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 405 Semipalmated Sandpiper
405_Semipalmated_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 405 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Semipalmated Sandpiper.
405_Semipalmated_Sandpiper.jpg.story: A thumb-sized shorebird that migrates eight thousand miles between Arctic tundra and Suriname mudflats, fueled by mass-feasting on horseshoe crab eggs at Delaware Bay. Its feet bear partial webbing, hence the name. The plate reflects Havell's mature technique of biting copper plates in successive aquatint stages to build tonal depth in the wet sand.

406_Trumpeter_Swan.jpg.title: 406 Trumpeter Swan
406_Trumpeter_Swan.jpg.description: Plate 406 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Trumpeter Swan.
406_Trumpeter_Swan.jpg.story: North America's heaviest native waterfowl, with an eight-foot wingspan and a bugling call produced by an elongated windpipe coiled inside the breastbone. Audubon reportedly used a Trumpeter Swan quill to paint the fine feather details on several plates. Market hunting for skins and swansdown pushed the species to near-extinction by 1900.

407_Dusky_Albatros.jpg.title: 407 Dusky Albatros
407_Dusky_Albatros.jpg.description: Plate 407 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Dusky Albatros.
407_Dusky_Albatros.jpg.story: The Sooty Albatross, a pelagic wanderer of the southern oceans, rarely approaches North American shores; Audubon's figure is based on a specimen of questionable provenance. The plate is among the most geographically anomalous in the entire folio. Havell engraved it anyway, filling out the subscription's promised 435-plate count.

408_American_Scoter_Duck.jpg.title: 408 American Scoter Duck
408_American_Scoter_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 408 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Scoter Duck.
408_American_Scoter_Duck.jpg.story: The Black Scoter, a stocky sea duck whose drake is entirely coal-black save for a swollen yellow knob at the bill's base. Courting males produce a mournful, whistling piu call that carries over winter bays. Audubon's Havell-engraved aquatint captures the slick oiliness of the drake's plumage using layered tones of lampblack ink.

409_I._Havell's_Tern_-_2._Trudeau's_Tern.title: 409 I. Havell's Tern - 2. Trudeau's Tern
409_I._Havell's_Tern_-_2._Trudeau's_Tern.description: Plate 409 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Havell's Tern - . Trudeau's Tern.

source.canonical_title: File:409 I. Havell's Tern - 2. Trudeau's Tern.jpg

409_I._Havell's_Tern_-_2._Trudeau's_Tern.story: Audubon named the first bird for his London engraver Robert Havell Jr., a gesture of thanks for the decade Havell spent transferring 435 watercolors onto copper. Both species were later reassigned: Havell's Tern proved to be an immature Common Tern. Trudeau's Tern honors New Orleans physician James de Berty Trudeau.

40_American_Redstart.jpg.title: 40 American Redstart
40_American_Redstart.jpg.description: Plate 40 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Redstart.

410_Marsh_Tern.jpg.title: 410 Marsh Tern
410_Marsh_Tern.jpg.description: Plate 410 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Marsh Tern.
410_Marsh_Tern.jpg.story: Known today as the Gull-billed Tern, this marsh-hunter is unusual among terns for plucking insects, lizards, and even rodents off the ground rather than diving for fish. Its stout black bill gives it a gull-like profile. The plate demonstrates Havell's skill at aquatint grading, from chalky white underparts to the solid black cap.

411_Common_American_Swan.jpg.title: 411 Common American Swan
411_Common_American_Swan.jpg.description: Plate 411 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Common American Swan.
411_Common_American_Swan.jpg.story: Now the Tundra Swan, a high-Arctic breeder that migrates in long, honking chevrons to Chesapeake Bay. Audubon depicted it with a stylized water lily, one of the botanical flourishes his daughter-in-law Maria Martin often contributed to the later plates. The species was formerly called Whistling Swan for its high, yelping flight call.

412_I._Violet-green_Cormorant_-_2._Towns.title: 412 I. Violet-green Cormorant - 2. Townsend's Cormorant
412_I._Violet-green_Cormorant_-_2._Towns.description: Plate 412 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Violet-green Cormorant - . Townsend's Cormorant.

source.canonical_title: File:412 I. Violet-green Cormorant - 2. Townsend's Cormorant.jpg

412_I._Violet-green_Cormorant_-_2._Towns.story: Two Pacific coast cormorants, the Pelagic and Brandt's, rendered from skins Audubon never saw in life. Both nest on sheer sea cliffs, gluing seaweed to the rock with guano. John Kirk Townsend, honored in the species name, collected along the Columbia River in 1834-35 and shipped specimens east to Audubon for inclusion in the final plates.

413_California_Partridge.jpg.title: 413 California Partridge
413_California_Partridge.jpg.description: Plate 413 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting California Partridge.
413_California_Partridge.jpg.story: The California Quail, recognizable by its forward-curling topknot of six fused feathers. Coveys scratch in the chaparral in tight family groups, posting a sentinel on a low branch while the rest feed. The species was unknown east of the Rockies until California specimens began reaching Philadelphia and London in the 1830s.

414_I._Golden-winged_Warbler_-_2._Cape_M.title: 414 I. Golden-winged Warbler - 2. Cape May Warbler
414_I._Golden-winged_Warbler_-_2._Cape_M.description: Plate 414 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Golden-winged Warbler - . Cape May Warbler.

source.canonical_title: File:414 I. Golden-winged Warbler - 2. Cape May Warbler.jpg

414_I._Golden-winged_Warbler_-_2._Cape_M.story: A zebra-striped Golden-wing probes dead leaf clusters for caterpillars, while the tiger-chested Cape May sips nectar with its uniquely tubular tongue. Alexander Wilson named the Cape May for the New Jersey location where he first collected one. Decades later the species was not seen again at Cape May for over a century.

415_I._Brown_Creeper_-_2._Californian_Nu.title: 415 I. Brown Creeper - 2. Californian Nuthatch
415_I._Brown_Creeper_-_2._Californian_Nu.description: Plate 415 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Brown Creeper - . Californian Nuthatch.

source.canonical_title: File:415 I. Brown Creeper - 2. Californian Nuthatch.jpg

415_I._Brown_Creeper_-_2._Californian_Nu.story: The Brown Creeper spirals up tree trunks in tight inch-worm hitches, then drops to the base of the next tree to start again; the Pygmy Nuthatch of western pinewoods huddles in cavities by the dozen on cold nights. Havell's tight hatching conveys the creeper's bark-camouflage patterning with extraordinary restraint.

416_I._Hairy_Woodpecker_-_2._Red-bellied.title: 416 I. Hairy Woodpecker - 2. Red-bellied Woodpecker - 3. Red-shafted Woodpecker - 4. Lewis' Woodpecker - 5. Red-breasted Woodpecker
416_I._Hairy_Woodpecker_-_2._Red-bellied.description: Plate 416 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Hairy Woodpecker - . Red-bellied Woodpecker - . Red-shafted Woodpecker - . Lewis' Woodpecker - . Red-breasted Woodpecker.

source.canonical_title: File:416 I. Hairy Woodpecker - 2. Red-bellied Woodpecker - 3. Red-shafted Woodpecker - 4. Lewis' Woodpecker - 5. Red-breasted Woodpecker.jpg

416_I._Hairy_Woodpecker_-_2._Red-bellied.story: Five woodpeckers packed into one composite, including Lewis's Woodpecker, named for Meriwether Lewis, who collected the type specimen on the 1805 expedition. The species flycatches like a crow and stores acorns in bark crevices. Composite plates like this helped Audubon efficiently fill subscription quotas in the final volumes.

417_I._Maria's_Woodpecker_-_2._Three-toe.title: 417 I. Maria's Woodpecker - 2. Three-toed Woodpecker - 3. Phillips' Woodpecker - 4. Canadian Woodpecker - 5. Harris's Woodpecker - 6. Audubon's Woodpecker
417_I._Maria's_Woodpecker_-_2._Three-toe.description: Plate 417 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Maria's Woodpecker - . Three-toed Woodpecker - . Phillips' Woodpecker - . Canadian Woodpecker - . Harris's Woodpecker - . Audubon's Woodpecker.

source.canonical_title: File:417 I. Maria's Woodpecker - 2. Three-toed Woodpecker - 3. Phillips' Woodpecker - 4. Canadian Woodpecker - 5. Harris's Woodpecker - 6. Audubon's Woodpecker.jpg

417_I._Maria's_Woodpecker_-_2._Three-toe.story: Six woodpeckers, several of which modern taxonomy folded into the Hairy and Downy species. Audubon named Harris's Woodpecker for Edward Harris, the Philadelphia Quaker who bankrolled his 1843 Missouri River expedition. The plate is a taxonomic snapshot of the pre-Darwinian splitter era, when every regional variant got its own Latin name.

418_I._American_Ptarmigan_-_2._White-tai.title: 418 I. American Ptarmigan - 2. White-tailed Grous
418_I._American_Ptarmigan_-_2._White-tai.description: Plate 418 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. American Ptarmigan - . White-tailed Grous.

source.canonical_title: File:418 I. American Ptarmigan - 2. White-tailed Grous.jpg

418_I._American_Ptarmigan_-_2._White-tai.story: The Rock Ptarmigan molts three times a year to match the Arctic cycle: snow-white in winter, mottled brown in summer, gray in autumn. The White-tailed Ptarmigan is the only bird that lives year-round above alpine treeline in North America. Audubon worked from northern skins supplied by Hudson's Bay Company correspondents.

419_I._Little_Tawny_Thrush_-_2._Ptiliogo.title: 419 I. Little Tawny Thrush - 2. Ptiliogony's Townsendi - 3. Canada Jay
419_I._Little_Tawny_Thrush_-_2._Ptiliogo.description: Plate 419 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Little Tawny Thrush - . Ptiliogony's Townsendi - . Canada Jay.

source.canonical_title: File:419 I. Little Tawny Thrush - 2. Ptiliogony's Townsendi - 3. Canada Jay.jpg

419_I._Little_Tawny_Thrush_-_2._Ptiliogo.story: The Veery, Townsend's Solitaire, and Canada Jay (now Gray Jay) share this composite. The Canada Jay caches thousands of food morsels in spruce crowns, gluing them with saliva; the Veery spirals its flutelike song downward like a tumbling bell. Audubon folded late-arriving western skins into his northeastern work for symmetry.

41_Ruffed_Grouse.jpg.title: 41 Ruffed Grouse
41_Ruffed_Grouse.jpg.description: Plate 41 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ruffed Grouse.
41_Ruffed_Grouse.jpg.story: The Ruffed Grouse drums with its wings, not its throat, producing a hollow thumping that rises to a blurred roar; the pulse can carry a quarter-mile through hardwood forest. Audubon shows a drumming male atop a fallen log, the classic display posture. Robert Havell Jr. etched the blurred wings with drypoint to suggest motion.

420_Prairie_Starling.jpg.title: 420 Prairie Starling
420_Prairie_Starling.jpg.description: Plate 420 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Prairie Starling.
420_Prairie_Starling.jpg.story: Now called the Yellow-headed Blackbird, a cattail marsh specialist whose males sound like a rusty hinge forced open. Flocks wheel in such density over prairie potholes that farmers complained of eclipsed sunlight. Audubon first encountered the species on his 1843 Missouri River trip and added it to the final octavo editions.

421_Brown_Pelican.jpg.title: 421 Brown Pelican
421_Brown_Pelican.jpg.description: Plate 421 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Brown Pelican.
421_Brown_Pelican.jpg.story: One of only two pelican species that plunge-dive, folding its nine-foot wingspan and smashing headfirst into the surf to stun fish. The throat pouch holds three gallons of water. The Brown Pelican's population later collapsed in the mid-twentieth century from DDT-thinned eggshells before rebounding spectacularly after the 1972 pesticide ban.

422_Rough-legged_Falcon.jpg.title: 422 Rough-legged Falcon
422_Rough-legged_Falcon.jpg.description: Plate 422 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Rough-legged Falcon.
422_Rough-legged_Falcon.jpg.story: The Rough-legged Hawk (not a falcon, despite Audubon's name) breeds on Arctic cliffs and winters across open American fields, hovering into headwinds to scan for voles. Feathered legs insulate it to the toes. This plate came late in the subscription, when Audubon was correcting earlier identification mistakes.

423_I._Plumed--Partridge_-_2._Thick--leg.title: 423 I. Plumed--Partridge - 2. Thick--legged Partridge
423_I._Plumed--Partridge_-_2._Thick--leg.description: Plate 423 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Plumed--Partridge - . Thick--legged Partridge.
423_I._Plumed--Partridge_-_2._Thick--leg.story: The Mountain Quail wears the longest topknot in the New World quail family, two straight black plumes like arrows; the Chukar was actually introduced from Eurasia decades after Audubon painted it. Havell's double-dash typography on the plate reflects inconsistent punctuation in Audubon's original captions, preserved through the lithographic transfers.

424_I._Lazuli_Finch_-_2._Crimson-necked_.title: 424 I. Lazuli Finch - 2. Crimson-necked Bull-Finch - 3. Gray-crowned Linnet - 4. Cow-pen Bird - 5. Evening Grosbeak - 6. Brown Longspur
424_I._Lazuli_Finch_-_2._Crimson-necked_.description: Plate 424 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Lazuli Finch - . Crimson-necked Bull-Finch - . Gray-crowned Linnet - . Cow-pen Bird - . Evening Grosbeak - . Brown Longspur.

source.canonical_title: File:424 I. Lazuli Finch - 2. Crimson-necked Bull-Finch - 3. Gray-crowned Linnet - 4. Cow-pen Bird - 5. Evening Grosbeak - 6. Brown Longspur.jpg

424_I._Lazuli_Finch_-_2._Crimson-necked_.story: Six small finches and finch-kin, including the Evening Grosbeak, whose bill is stout enough to crack cherry pits. Audubon first recorded the species only in 1823 near the Canadian border. The Cow-pen Bird (now Brown-headed Cowbird) is the continent's most notorious brood parasite, laying eggs in some 220 host species.

425_Columbian_Humming_Bird.jpg.title: 425 Columbian Humming Bird
425_Columbian_Humming_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 425 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Columbian Humming Bird.
425_Columbian_Humming_Bird.jpg.story: The Anna's Hummingbird, a west-coast resident whose courting male climbs 130 feet and dives at 60 mph, producing an explosive chirp with its tail feathers at the bottom. Its heart beats over 1,200 times a minute. Havell captured the iridescent magenta gorget with gold leaf tones laid over aquatint for a convincing metallic flash.

426_Californian_Vulture.jpg.title: 426 Californian Vulture
426_Californian_Vulture.jpg.description: Plate 426 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Californian Vulture.
426_Californian_Vulture.jpg.story: The California Condor, largest land bird in North America with a 9.5-foot wingspan, ranged as far east as New York during the Pleistocene. By Audubon's era it was already retreating to the Pacific cordillera. The species crashed to just 22 individuals in the 1980s before captive breeding brought it slowly back from functional extinction.

427_I._White-legged_Oyster-catcher_-_2._.title: 427 I. White-legged Oyster-catcher - 2. Slender-billed Oyster-catcher
427_I._White-legged_Oyster-catcher_-_2._.description: Plate 427 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. White-legged Oyster-catcher - . Slender-billed Oyster-catcher.

source.canonical_title: File:427 I. White-legged Oyster-catcher - 2. Slender-billed Oyster-catcher.jpg

427_I._White-legged_Oyster-catcher_-_2._.story: Oystercatchers use their flattened chisel-bills to slip between the valves of bivalves and sever the adductor muscle before the mollusk can clamp shut. Each bird learns the technique from its parents over a long apprenticeship. The two forms shown were later reclassified as the American Oystercatcher and the Black Oystercatcher of the Pacific coast.

428_Townsend's_Sandpiper.jpg.title: 428 Townsend's Sandpiper
428_Townsend's_Sandpiper.jpg.description: Plate 428 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Townsend's Sandpiper.
428_Townsend's_Sandpiper.jpg.story: A mystery plate depicting a specimen collected by John Kirk Townsend in 1836 that modern ornithologists now believe was a juvenile Surfbird or Rock Sandpiper. Audubon's willingness to name a species from a single skin reflects the hurried pace of the last plates. Havell pulled the aquatint as the subscription was closing.

429_Western_Duck.jpg.title: 429 Western Duck
429_Western_Duck.jpg.description: Plate 429 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Western Duck.
429_Western_Duck.jpg.story: Now the Steller's Eider, a small Arctic sea duck whose drakes wear chestnut underparts and a green nape spot. It dives for amphipods under pack ice. Audubon never saw one alive and worked from Alaskan skins that had traveled through Russian trading posts to reach European collections.

42_Orchard_Oriole.jpg.title: 42 Orchard Oriole
42_Orchard_Oriole.jpg.description: Plate 42 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Orchard Oriole.
42_Orchard_Oriole.jpg.story: The Orchard Oriole, smallest American oriole, weaves a hanging basket-nest of grass blades in apple trees and pecans. The male is brick-burgundy rather than the flame-orange of its Baltimore cousin. Audubon painted this pair in 1822 in Louisiana, during the years of his greatest field productivity.

430_Slender-billed_Guillemot.jpg.title: 430 Slender-billed Guillemot
430_Slender-billed_Guillemot.jpg.description: Plate 430 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Slender-billed Guillemot.
430_Slender-billed_Guillemot.jpg.story: The Marbled Murrelet, an enigmatic Pacific alcid that flies inland each dawn to lay a single egg on a mossy branch of an old-growth conifer, sometimes fifty miles from the sea. The nesting habit was not confirmed by ornithologists until 1974. Audubon figured it without any clue where it actually bred.

431_American_Flamingo.jpg.title: 431 American Flamingo
431_American_Flamingo.jpg.description: Plate 431 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting American Flamingo.
431_American_Flamingo.jpg.story: Audubon's flamingo is drawn bent nearly double to fit the double-elephant folio's upright format, the compositional solution he used for tall birds throughout the project. The birds filter brine shrimp through fine lamellae, and the pink pigment comes straight from their food. Free-flying flocks in Florida were effectively extirpated by plume hunters a generation after this plate was published.

432_I._Burrowing_Owl_-_2._Large-headed_B.title: 432 I. Burrowing Owl - 2. Large-headed Burrowing Owl - 3. Little night Owl - 4. Columbian Owl - 5. Short-eared Owl
432_I._Burrowing_Owl_-_2._Large-headed_B.description: Plate 432 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Burrowing Owl - . Large-headed Burrowing Owl - . Little night Owl - . Columbian Owl - . Short-eared Owl.

source.canonical_title: File:432 I. Burrowing Owl - 2. Large-headed Burrowing Owl - 3. Little night Owl - 4. Columbian Owl - 5. Short-eared Owl.jpg

432_I._Burrowing_Owl_-_2._Large-headed_B.story: Five small owls share this composite. The Burrowing Owl nests down prairie-dog holes and lines the entrance with cattle dung to attract dung beetles it then eats. The Short-eared Owl quarters open grasslands like a tilting moth. Audubon's western specimens came via Townsend; the eastern ones he collected himself.

433_I._Bullock's_Oriole_-_2._Baltimore_O.title: 433 I. Bullock's Oriole - 2. Baltimore Oriole - 3. Mexican Goldfinch - 4. Varied Thrush - 5. Common Water Thrush
433_I._Bullock's_Oriole_-_2._Baltimore_O.description: Plate 433 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Bullock's Oriole - . Baltimore Oriole - . Mexican Goldfinch - . Varied Thrush - . Common Water Thrush.

source.canonical_title: File:433 I. Bullock's Oriole - 2. Baltimore Oriole - 3. Mexican Goldfinch - 4. Varied Thrush - 5. Common Water Thrush.jpg

433_I._Bullock's_Oriole_-_2._Baltimore_O.story: Bullock's Oriole and the Baltimore Oriole were lumped as a single species called the Northern Oriole in 1973, then split again in 1995 after genetic studies. The Varied Thrush haunts deep Pacific rainforest with an eerie minor-key whistle. Audubon crowded five species onto this plate to meet the subscription deadline.

434_I._Little_Tyrant_Fly-catcher_-_2._Sm.title: 434 I. Little Tyrant Fly-catcher - 2. Small-headed Fly-catcher - 3. Blue Mountain Warbler - 4. Bartran's Vireo - 5. Short-legged Pewee - 6. Rocky Mountain Fly-catcher
434_I._Little_Tyrant_Fly-catcher_-_2._Sm.description: Plate 434 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting I. Little Tyrant Fly-catcher - . Small-headed Fly-catcher - . Blue Mountain Warbler - . Bartran's Vireo - . Short-legged Pewee - . Rocky Mountain Fly-catcher.

source.canonical_title: File:434 I. Little Tyrant Fly-catcher - 2. Small-headed Fly-catcher - 3. Blue Mountain Warbler - 4. Bartran's Vireo - 5. Short-legged Pewee - 6. Rocky Mountain Fly-catcher.jpg

434_I._Little_Tyrant_Fly-catcher_-_2._Sm.story: Several of these names have no modern equivalent, and the Small-headed Flycatcher and Blue Mountain Warbler are often cited as possible extinct species or misidentifications. Audubon clashed bitterly with Alexander Wilson's camp over whether he had invented some of them. The truth may never be settled: no specimens survive.

435_I._Columbian_Water_Ouzel_-_2._Arctic.title: 435 I. Columbian Water Ouzel - 2. Arctic Water Ouzel
435_I._Columbian_Water_Ouzel_-_2._Arctic.description: Plate 435 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting 435 I. Columbian Water Ouzel - 2. Arctic Water Ouzel.jpg

source.canonical_title: File:435 I. Columbian Water Ouzel - 2. Arctic Water Ouzel.jpg

435_I._Columbian_Water_Ouzel_-_2._Arctic.story: The final plate of Birds of America, issued in June 1838, depicts the American Dipper, a songbird that walks underwater along mountain-stream bottoms hunting caddisfly larvae. Audubon chose it deliberately as a closer, eleven years after the first plate appeared. The complete subscription required 435 prints delivered in 87 parts of 5 plates each.

43_Cedar_Bird.jpg.title: 43 Cedar Bird
43_Cedar_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 43 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Cedar Bird.
43_Cedar_Bird.jpg.story: The Cedar Waxwing, a silky gregarious fruit-eater whose red wax wingtip-droplets are the solidified pigments of berries it has eaten. Waxwings sometimes get drunk on fermented winter fruit. Audubon's pair perches on a branch laden with red cedar berries, one of the species's favored staples in the eastern woodlands.

44_Summer_Red_Bird.jpg.title: 44 Summer Red Bird
44_Summer_Red_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 44 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Summer Red Bird.
44_Summer_Red_Bird.jpg.story: The Summer Tanager, the only entirely red bird in North America, specializes in bees and wasps; it catches them on the wing, bashes them against a branch to remove the stinger, then swallows them whole. Audubon places the male and female together in a figured pose typical of his early Mississippi Valley fieldwork.

45_Traill's_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 45 Traill's Flycatcher
45_Traill's_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 45 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Traill's Flycatcher.
45_Traill's_Flycatcher.jpg.story: Named by Audubon for his Liverpool friend Thomas Stewart Traill, who helped arrange the English subscriptions that funded Birds of America. The bird was later split into two look-alike species, the Alder and Willow Flycatchers, distinguishable only by voice. Audubon's original specimen probably belonged to the Alder side of that split.

46_Barred_Owl.jpg.title: 46 Barred Owl
46_Barred_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 46 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Barred Owl.
46_Barred_Owl.jpg.story: The Barred Owl, the only eastern owl with dark eyes rather than yellow, hoots its famous who-cooks-for-you in eight resonant notes. It has recently expanded westward and now threatens the endangered Spotted Owl through interbreeding. Audubon's plate shows the distinctive horizontal barring across the throat that gave the species its name.

47_Ruby-throated_Humming_Bird.jpg.title: 47 Ruby-throated Humming Bird
47_Ruby-throated_Humming_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 47 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ruby-throated Humming Bird.
47_Ruby-throated_Humming_Bird.jpg.story: The only hummingbird that breeds east of the Great Plains, it crosses the Gulf of Mexico nonstop on spring migration, a 500-mile flight powered by fat reserves it nearly doubles in weight before departing. Audubon's spirited plate shows ten birds visiting a trumpet creeper vine, a riot of action unusual in the folio.

48_Azure_Warbler.jpg.title: 48 Azure Warbler
48_Azure_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 48 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Azure Warbler.
48_Azure_Warbler.jpg.story: The Cerulean Warbler, a canopy-dwelling sky-blue sprite now in steep decline as both eastern hardwood forests and Andean wintering grounds shrink. Audubon's figure captures its powder-blue back. The species has lost more than 70 percent of its population since 1966, the steepest drop of any North American warbler.

49_Blue-green_Warbler.jpg.title: 49 Blue-green Warbler
49_Blue-green_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 49 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue-green Warbler.
49_Blue-green_Warbler.jpg.story: The Blue-green Warbler may have been a mislabeled or hybrid specimen: no bird matching Audubon's description has been conclusively linked to any modern species, and it joins a short list of his ghost taxa. Some ornithologists suspect an immature Cerulean Warbler. The mystery has persisted almost two centuries.

4_Purple_Finch.jpg.title: 4 Purple Finch
4_Purple_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 4 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Purple Finch.
4_Purple_Finch.jpg.story: Not actually purple but raspberry-dipped, as Roger Tory Peterson put it. The Purple Finch was eastern North America's common feeder bird until the 1940s, when House Finches introduced from California outcompeted it. Plate 4 was among the first engraved in William Lizars's Edinburgh shop before production moved to Havell's in London.

50_Black_&_Yellow_Warbler.jpg.title: 50 Black & Yellow Warbler
50_Black_&_Yellow_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 50 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black & Yellow Warbler.
50_Black_&_Yellow_Warbler.jpg.story: The Magnolia Warbler, named by Alexander Wilson after shooting one in a magnolia tree in Mississippi, though the bird actually breeds in young spruce stands far to the north. The name stuck anyway. Audubon shows the male's bold yellow underparts streaked with black, against a green sprig likely drawn by his assistant Joseph Mason.

51_Red-tailed_Hawk.jpg.title: 51 Red-tailed Hawk
51_Red-tailed_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 51 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-tailed Hawk.
51_Red-tailed_Hawk.jpg.story: The most common hawk in North America and the continent's unofficial soundtrack: its piercing keeeer cry is dubbed over virtually every eagle in Hollywood. Audubon's pair is shown stooping on a rabbit, a dynamic composition that pushed Havell to experiment with layered aquatint to render the brick-red tail translucent against sky.

52_Chuck-will's_Widow.jpg.title: 52 Chuck-will's Widow
52_Chuck-will's_Widow.jpg.description: Plate 52 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Chuck-will's Widow.
52_Chuck-will's_Widow.jpg.story: The largest nightjar in North America, it swallows small songbirds whole; stomach contents have yielded entire warblers and hummingbirds. Its name imitates the three-note nocturnal call. Audubon shows it flushed from the leaf litter, mouth agape, one of several nightjars he painted with the species's unique sit-and-wait camouflage.

53_Painted_Finch.jpg.title: 53 Painted Finch
53_Painted_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 53 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Painted Finch.
53_Painted_Finch.jpg.story: The Painted Bunting, the most chromatically extravagant songbird north of the Rio Grande, the male a stained-glass riot of indigo, lime, and scarlet. Audubon's text records that Creoles in New Orleans caged it as the nonpareil. Heavy trapping drove Gulf Coast populations down; the bird is now protected but still declining.

54_Rice_Bird.jpg.title: 54 Rice Bird
54_Rice_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 54 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Rice Bird.
54_Rice_Bird.jpg.story: The Bobolink, which Audubon called the Rice Bird for its habit of gorging on flooded southern rice fields during fall migration. Males sing a bubbling electronic song that Emily Dickinson compared to the mob. The species migrates 12,500 miles round-trip from northern hay meadows to Argentine pampas, one of the longest routes of any songbird.

55_Cuvier's_Regulus.jpg.title: 55 Cuvier's Regulus
55_Cuvier's_Regulus.jpg.description: Plate 55 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Cuvier's Regulus.
55_Cuvier's_Regulus.jpg.story: A kinglet-like bird that Audubon claimed to have collected in Pennsylvania and named for the French anatomist Georges Cuvier, but which has never been documented since. It joins the folio's small catalog of possible hoaxes or misidentifications. Whether Audubon made an honest error or inflated his count has been debated ever since.

56_Red-shouldered_Hawk.jpg.title: 56 Red-shouldered Hawk
56_Red-shouldered_Hawk.jpg.date_created: 1827-1838 (publication; from painting made in Louisiana c. 1826)
56_Red-shouldered_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 56 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-shouldered Hawk.
56_Red-shouldered_Hawk.jpg.story: A strikingly patterned woodland hawk whose keeee-ahh call is routinely imitated by Blue Jays to clear feeders. The Red-shouldered hunts by still-watching from a low perch, dropping on frogs and snakes. Audubon's pair is set in a mixed deciduous forest, his favored habitat during the Ohio Valley years of the 1820s.

57_Loggerhead_Shrike.jpg.title: 57 Loggerhead Shrike
57_Loggerhead_Shrike.jpg.description: Plate 57 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Loggerhead Shrike.
57_Loggerhead_Shrike.jpg.story: A songbird that hunts like a hawk, impaling grasshoppers, lizards, and even sparrows on thorns or barbed-wire barbs to anchor them for tearing. The cached surplus also signals quality to prospective mates. Audubon's plate catches the shrike's black bandit mask and hooked bill, hallmarks of this predatory passerine.

58_Hermit_Thrush.jpg.title: 58 Hermit Thrush
58_Hermit_Thrush.jpg.description: Plate 58 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Hermit Thrush.
58_Hermit_Thrush.jpg.story: The Hermit Thrush's spiraling flutelike song, delivered in two pitches at once via a double-chambered syrinx, inspired Whitman's elegy for Lincoln in When Lilacs Last in the Dooryard Bloom'd. Unlike its relatives it winters in the southern United States rather than the tropics. Audubon's subdued figure matches the bird's retiring habits.

59_Chestnut-sided_Warbler.jpg.title: 59 Chestnut-sided Warbler
59_Chestnut-sided_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 59 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Chestnut-sided Warbler.
59_Chestnut-sided_Warbler.jpg.story: A warbler that exploded in numbers during the nineteenth century as eastern forests were cleared for second-growth edge habitat. Audubon called it extremely rare in his day; a century later it was common. Its song is often rendered pleased-pleased-pleased-to-MEET-cha. The reversal of its fortunes is a rare story of gain from disturbance.

5_Bonaparte's_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 5 Bonaparte's Flycatcher
5_Bonaparte's_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 5 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bonaparte 's Flycatcher.
5_Bonaparte's_Flycatcher.jpg.story: Named by Audubon for the naturalist Charles Lucien Bonaparte, Napoleon's nephew and a champion of Audubon's work in Philadelphia scientific circles. The bird itself has never been reliably identified since. Like several ghost species in the folio, it may represent a plumage variant or a single lost specimen.

60_Carbonated_Warbler.jpg.title: 60 Carbonated Warbler
60_Carbonated_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 60 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Carbonated Warbler.
60_Carbonated_Warbler.jpg.story: Another of Audubon's unconfirmed species, known only from a single plate and a pair of specimens he said he shot in Kentucky in 1811. No ornithologist has ever seen one since. The Carbonated Warbler is a standing puzzle: a possibly extinct species, a hybrid, or a misidentified aberrant individual.

61_Great_Horned_Owl.jpg.title: 61 Great Horned Owl
61_Great_Horned_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 61 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Horned Owl.
61_Great_Horned_Owl.jpg.story: The tiger of the air, able to kill prey three times its weight including skunks, which it eats without apparent distress. Its ears are asymmetrically placed on the skull to triangulate prey under snow. Audubon shows a pair with prey at a nest cavity, one of his more dramatic predator compositions.

62_Passenger_Pigeon.jpg.title: 62 Passenger Pigeon
62_Passenger_Pigeon.jpg.description: Plate 62 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Passenger Pigeon.
62_Passenger_Pigeon.jpg.story: The billion-bird flocks that Audubon watched darken the Ohio sky for three days in 1813 vanished within a century; Martha, the last individual, died in the Cincinnati Zoo in 1914. His plate shows a courting pair on a branch, tender and unassuming. It is now among the folio's most haunting images.

63_White-eyed_Flycatcher_or_Vireo.jpg.title: 63 White-eyed Flycatcher or Vireo
63_White-eyed_Flycatcher_or_Vireo.jpg.description: Plate 63 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-eyed Flycatcher or Vireo.
63_White-eyed_Flycatcher_or_Vireo.jpg.story: The White-eyed Vireo, a scrubby thicket bird whose snappy song ends in the characteristic pick-up-the-beer-check. Its pale iris gleams from the shadows. The species was once regularly called a flycatcher because Audubon's era grouped insectivorous small birds by behavior rather than anatomy.

64_Swamp_Sparrow.jpg.title: 64 Swamp Sparrow
64_Swamp_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 64 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Swamp Sparrow.
64_Swamp_Sparrow.jpg.story: A rich rust-and-gray sparrow that wades belly-deep in cattail marshes, sometimes sticking its head underwater to seize aquatic invertebrates, a behavior shared with no other North American sparrow. Audubon's figure is among the more accurate of his marsh-bird portraits, reflecting time spent paddling Louisiana bayous in the early 1820s.

65_Rathbone_Warbler.jpg.title: 65 Rathbone Warbler
65_Rathbone_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 65 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Rathbone Warbler.
65_Rathbone_Warbler.jpg.story: Named for the Rathbone family of Liverpool, merchants whose hospitality in 1826 launched Audubon's London success. The species itself turned out to be an immature Yellow Warbler, one of the folio's early taxonomic errors. The Rathbones remained lifelong patrons even after the naming misfired.

66_Ivory-billed_Woodpecker.jpg.title: 66 Ivory-billed Woodpecker
66_Ivory-billed_Woodpecker.jpg.description: Plate 66 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Ivory-billed Woodpecker.

source.credit: University of Pittsburgh, Audubon's Birds of America, Darlington Digital Library , Image

66_Ivory-billed_Woodpecker.jpg.story: The largest North American woodpecker, twenty inches from beak to tail, last confirmed in Louisiana's Singer Tract in 1944 and likely extinct despite tantalizing recent reports. Its three-note double-rap on a buttressed cypress could be heard a mile away. Audubon's pair, shown prying bark grubs, is now a memorial as much as a record.

67_Red-winged_Starling_or_Marsh_Blackbir.title: 67 Red-winged Starling or Marsh Blackbird
67_Red-winged_Starling_or_Marsh_Blackbir.description: Plate 67 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Red-winged Starling or Marsh Blackbird.

source.canonical_title: File:67 Red-winged Starling or Marsh Blackbird.jpg

67_Red-winged_Starling_or_Marsh_Blackbir.story: The Red-winged Blackbird, possibly the most abundant bird in North America, its conk-la-REE song the signature of every cattail marsh. Males fluff their scarlet epaulets in territorial display; females are streaky brown and often mistaken for sparrows. Audubon's plate was drawn during his Mississippi Delta excursions.

68_Republican_or_Cliff_Swallow.jpg.title: 68 Republican or Cliff Swallow
68_Republican_or_Cliff_Swallow.jpg.description: Plate 68 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Republican or Cliff Swallow.
68_Republican_or_Cliff_Swallow.jpg.story: The Cliff Swallow builds gourd-shaped mud nests by the thousands beneath bridges and on rock overhangs; each pellet of mud represents one round-trip from shoreline to construction site. The swallows of San Juan Capistrano are the most famous colony. Audubon's plate shows a colony affixed to a vertical cliff face.

69_Bay-breasted_Warbler.jpg.title: 69 Bay-breasted Warbler
69_Bay-breasted_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 69 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Bay-breasted Warbler.
69_Bay-breasted_Warbler.jpg.story: A chestnut-flanked boreal specialist that depends heavily on spruce budworm outbreaks; its numbers rise and fall with the infestation cycle. The species winters in Panama and northern South America. Audubon figured it during the spruce-fir productivity of the 1820s northeast, when old-growth conifer forest was still extensive.

6_Wild_Turkey.jpg.title: 6 Wild Turkey
6_Wild_Turkey.jpg.description: Plate 6 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wild Turkey.
6_Wild_Turkey.jpg.story: Audubon's most celebrated plate and the unofficial emblem of Birds of America: a life-size strutting tom at 4.5 feet tall fills the entire double-elephant sheet. The species had been extirpated from much of its range by the early 1900s before twentieth-century reintroductions restored it to every state except Alaska. He designed his seal around this figure.

70_Henslow's_Bunting.jpg.title: 70 Henslow's Bunting
70_Henslow's_Bunting.jpg.description: Plate 70 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Henslow's Bunting.
70_Henslow's_Bunting.jpg.story: Henslow's Sparrow, named by Audubon for John Stevens Henslow, the Cambridge botanist who had mentored a young Charles Darwin. Its song is an emphatic but barely audible tse-lick. The grassland sparrow is now among the fastest-declining songbirds in North America as native prairies yield to row crops.

71_Winter_Hawk.jpg.title: 71 Winter Hawk
71_Winter_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 71 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Winter Hawk.
71_Winter_Hawk.jpg.story: The Winter Hawk was eventually recognized as an immature Red-shouldered Hawk, an error typical of a period when juvenile plumages were often mistaken for separate species. Audubon's scientific publisher corrected many such duplications in later editions. The plate survives as a record of early nineteenth-century taxonomic confusion.

72_Swallow-tailed_Hawk.jpg.title: 72 Swallow-tailed Hawk
72_Swallow-tailed_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 72 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Swallow-tailed Hawk.
72_Swallow-tailed_Hawk.jpg.story: The Swallow-tailed Kite drinks on the wing by skimming its bill through pond surfaces and eats live snakes in mid-flight without landing. Once common north to the Great Lakes, it retreated to Florida and Gulf swamps after 1900. Audubon's plate shows its forked tail at full spread, a pure elegance of line.

73_Wood_Thrush.jpg.title: 73 Wood Thrush
73_Wood_Thrush.jpg.description: Plate 73 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Wood Thrush.
73_Wood_Thrush.jpg.story: The Wood Thrush's ee-oh-lay song, produced simultaneously at two pitches by its paired syrinx, is the sound of the eastern hardwood forest. The species has lost nearly half its population since 1966 as its Central American rainforest wintering grounds shrink. Audubon's plate is set among the flowering dogwood of its breeding habitat.

74_Indigo_Bird.jpg.title: 74 Indigo Bird
74_Indigo_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 74 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Indigo Bird.
74_Indigo_Bird.jpg.story: Male Indigo Buntings look black by any direct measure; their blue is structural, produced by feather microstructure scattering light. The species navigates by starlight during nocturnal migration, a discovery made by Stephen Emlen in the 1960s. Audubon's plate catches the cobalt shimmer that evaporates in the hand.

75_Le_Petit_Caporal.jpg.title: 75 Le Petit Caporal
75_Le_Petit_Caporal.jpg.description: Plate 75 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Le Petit Caporal.
75_Le_Petit_Caporal.jpg.story: Audubon's Little Corporal was eventually determined to be an immature Merlin, named as an homage to Napoleon Bonaparte's nickname. The falcon takes songbirds in aerial chases and is one of few raptors that does not build its own nest, using abandoned crow and magpie nests instead.

76_Virginian_Partridge.jpg.title: 76 Virginian Partridge
76_Virginian_Partridge.jpg.description: Plate 76 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Virginian Partridge.
76_Virginian_Partridge.jpg.story: The Northern Bobwhite, whose clear bob-WHITE whistle once rang from every hedgerow east of the Rockies. Fall coveys roost in a tight defensive circle, heads outward. Audubon's plate shows a covey flushing in alarm, rendered with explosive compositional energy as a red-shouldered hawk descends from above.

77_Belted_Kingfisher.jpg.title: 77 Belted Kingfisher
77_Belted_Kingfisher.jpg.description: Plate 77 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Belted Kingfisher.
77_Belted_Kingfisher.jpg.story: One of few North American birds in which the female is more colorful than the male, wearing an extra chestnut belt across her belly. Kingfishers excavate three-foot tunnels into stream banks with their bills and feet. Audubon's plate captures a male plunging for a minnow, a moment of explosive verticality.

78_Great_Carolina_Wren.jpg.title: 78 Great Carolina Wren
78_Great_Carolina_Wren.jpg.description: Plate 78 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Great Carolina Wren.
78_Great_Carolina_Wren.jpg.story: A loud rust-brown wren whose teakettle-teakettle-teakettle song can carry a quarter-mile. It builds a domed nest and stuffs the inner chamber with shed snake skin, which may deter predators. Audubon's range figure reflects the species's steady northward expansion, now reaching southern Canada.

79_Tyrant_Fly-catcher.jpg.title: 79 Tyrant Fly-catcher
79_Tyrant_Fly-catcher.jpg.description: Plate 79 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Tyrant Fly-catcher.
79_Tyrant_Fly-catcher.jpg.story: The Eastern Kingbird, a flycatcher so pugnacious it routinely attacks hawks and crows twice its size, riding on their backs and pecking their heads. Its flight is rapid and stiff-winged. Audubon's plate shows a pair in characteristic upright alertness on a flowering branch, the black-and-white crispness perfect for aquatint.

7_Purple_Grakle_or_Common_Crow_Blackbird.title: 7 Purple Grakle or Common Crow Blackbird
7_Purple_Grakle_or_Common_Crow_Blackbird.description: Plate 7 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Purple Grakle or Common Crow Blackbird.

source.canonical_title: File:7 Purple Grakle or Common Crow Blackbird.jpg

80_Prairie_Titlark.jpg.title: 80 Prairie Titlark
80_Prairie_Titlark.jpg.description: Plate 80 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Prairie Titlark.
80_Prairie_Titlark.jpg.story: The Sprague's Pipit, a northern prairie specialist whose song is delivered from heights of up to a thousand feet in sustained flights that can last an hour, one of the longest known for any bird. Audubon learned of it only in 1843 during his final expedition to the Upper Missouri, too late for inclusion in early plates.

81_Fish_Hawk_or_Osprey.jpg.title: 81 Fish Hawk or Osprey
81_Fish_Hawk_or_Osprey.jpg.description: Plate 81 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Fish Hawk or Osprey.
81_Fish_Hawk_or_Osprey.jpg.story: The Osprey's outer toe pivots backward to give it two forward and two backward toes, a specialized grip for seizing fish. Its eggshells thinned catastrophically under DDT pressure in the mid-twentieth century; populations have since rebounded almost everywhere. Audubon's plate renders the catch dangling head-forward for aerodynamic transport.

82_Whip-poor-will.jpg.title: 82 Whip-poor-will
82_Whip-poor-will.jpg.description: Plate 82 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Whip-poor-will.
82_Whip-poor-will.jpg.story: A nocturnal nightjar whose three-note call once dominated summer evenings across eastern woodlands but now sounds in a fraction of its former range. Its eyes are large enough to gather starlight for hawking moths from the forest floor. Audubon's plate renders the cryptic bark-like plumage that vanishes on a leaf-strewn ground.

83_House_Wren.jpg.title: 83 House Wren
83_House_Wren.jpg.description: Plate 83 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting House Wren.
83_House_Wren.jpg.story: A tiny bubbling-voiced cavity-nester that will pierce the eggs of competing bluebirds and chickadees to clear the neighborhood. It sometimes builds dummy nests in multiple cavities to confuse predators. Audubon's plate shows a pair at a nest site tucked into what he called an old hat hung against a barn wall.

84_Blue-Grey_Fly-catcher.jpg.title: 84 Blue-Grey Fly-catcher
84_Blue-Grey_Fly-catcher.jpg.description: Plate 84 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Blue-Grey Fly-catcher.
84_Blue-Grey_Fly-catcher.jpg.story: The Blue-gray Gnatcatcher, a slim tail-flicking sprite that builds a lichen-covered nest small enough to fit in a teacup. Its nest is bound with spider silk so it expands as the chicks grow. Audubon painted this pair in their typical restless posture, a difficult subject for a static medium.

85_Yellow_Throated_Warbler.jpg.title: 85 Yellow Throated Warbler
85_Yellow_Throated_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 85 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow Throated Warbler.
85_Yellow_Throated_Warbler.jpg.story: A Spanish-moss-loving warbler of southern bottomlands that creeps along pine branches like a nuthatch, probing bark crevices. Its clear descending song is an early voice of southern spring. Audubon's plate captures the clean yellow bib and black facial mask against gray upperparts, a trim geometric portrait.

86_Black_Warrior.jpg.title: 86 Black Warrior
86_Black_Warrior.jpg.description: Plate 86 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting "Black Warrior Falco harlani " (= Harlan's Hawk Buteo jamaicensis harlani ).
86_Black_Warrior.jpg.story: Audubon named this bird for the Black Warrior River of Alabama and dedicated it to Benjamin Phillips, an English correspondent. It was later recognized as a dark form of the Red-tailed Hawk now called Harlan's Hawk. Harlan's remains the most aggressive and elusive of the Red-tail's many plumage variants.

87_Florida_Jay.jpg.title: 87 Florida Jay
87_Florida_Jay.jpg.description: Plate 87 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Florida Jay.
87_Florida_Jay.jpg.story: The Florida Scrub-Jay, the only bird species endemic to Florida, restricted to a few patches of oak-sand-scrub habitat that fire suppression has steadily eroded. The species is a cooperative breeder: grown offspring help their parents raise the next year's brood. Audubon's plate was painted during his 1832 trip to East Florida.

88_Autumnal_Warbler.jpg.title: 88 Autumnal Warbler
88_Autumnal_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 88 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Autumnal Warbler.
88_Autumnal_Warbler.jpg.story: The Autumnal Warbler was discovered to be merely the fall plumage of the Bay-breasted Warbler, an understandable confusion since fall warbler identification is notorious even today. Audubon painted it as a separate species during the Ohio Valley years before the seasonal variation was worked out.

89_Nashville_Warbler.jpg.title: 89 Nashville Warbler
89_Nashville_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 89 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Nashville Warbler.
89_Nashville_Warbler.jpg.story: Alexander Wilson collected the first specimen near Nashville in 1811 during his pioneering American Ornithology work, and the name stuck even though the bird is only a migrant through Tennessee. It breeds in two disjunct populations, one in New England and one in the Pacific Northwest, separated by two thousand miles.

8_White_throated_Sparrow.jpg.title: 8 White throated Sparrow
8_White_throated_Sparrow.jpg.description: Plate 8 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White throated Sparrow.
8_White_throated_Sparrow.jpg.story: The White-throated Sparrow's whistled Old-Sam-Peabody-Peabody-Peabody is one of the most learned sounds of the northern woods. The species comes in two color morphs, white-striped and tan-striped, and almost always mates with the opposite morph. Plate 8 is among the earliest Havell prints after the handover from Lizars.

90_Black_&_White_Creeper.jpg.title: 90 Black & White Creeper
90_Black_&_White_Creeper.jpg.description: Plate 90 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Black & White Creeper.
90_Black_&_White_Creeper.jpg.story: The Black-and-white Warbler creeps along trunks and branches like a nuthatch and probes bark crevices, a niche no other wood-warbler occupies. Its wiry thin song is easily missed. Audubon figured it in the nuthatch-like posture that led to its creeper name, a taxonomic holdover now abandoned.

91_Broad-winged_Hawk.jpg.title: 91 Broad-winged Hawk
91_Broad-winged_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 91 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Broad-winged Hawk.
91_Broad-winged_Hawk.jpg.story: A small forest hawk that migrates in spectacular kettles of thousands at Hawk Mountain and Veracruz each September, riding thermals to conserve energy on its trip to the Amazon. In summer it hunts frogs and toads from shaded forest perches. Audubon's plate catches its compact shape and broad black-and-white banded tail.

92_Pigeon_Hawk.jpg.title: 92 Pigeon Hawk
92_Pigeon_Hawk.jpg.description: Plate 92 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Pigeon Hawk.
92_Pigeon_Hawk.jpg.story: The Merlin, a small falcon that hunts songbirds in bursting low-level chases, often dismembering its prey on a favorite plucking post. Its numbers have rebounded strongly from twentieth-century declines and it now nests in city parks across the Great Plains. Audubon's plate shows the blue-gray male with fine streaking.

93_Sea-side_Finch.jpg.title: 93 Sea-side Finch
93_Sea-side_Finch.jpg.description: Plate 93 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Sea-side Finch.
93_Sea-side_Finch.jpg.story: The Seaside Sparrow, a saltmarsh specialist with huge feet for perching on reeds above tidal water. The Dusky subspecies of the Florida coast went extinct in 1987 after its Merritt Island habitat was flooded for NASA mosquito control and then burned. Audubon's plate captures the species before industrial land-use changed its world.

94_Grass_Finch_or_Bay-winged_Bunting.jpg.title: 94 Grass Finch or Bay-winged Bunting
94_Grass_Finch_or_Bay-winged_Bunting.jpg.description: Plate 94 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Grass Finch or Bay-winged Bunting.

source.canonical_title: File:94 Grass Finch or Bay-winged Bunting.jpg

94_Grass_Finch_or_Bay-winged_Bunting.jpg.story: The Vesper Sparrow, named by the ornithologist John Burroughs for its evening-singing habits after Audubon's era. It scratches in gravel for seeds and is the only sparrow to bathe regularly in dust rather than water. Audubon's figure captures the distinctive white outer tail feathers that flash in flight.

95_Yellow-poll_Warbler.jpg.title: 95 Yellow-poll Warbler
95_Yellow-poll_Warbler.jpg.description: Plate 95 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Yellow-poll Warbler.
95_Yellow-poll_Warbler.jpg.story: The Yellow Warbler, the most widely distributed warbler in the Americas, nesting from tundra edge to South American mangroves. When Brown-headed Cowbirds lay eggs in its nest, the warbler sometimes buries them by building a new nest floor on top, producing six-story nest-cakes. Audubon painted this yellow jewel amid flowering branches.

96_Columbia_Jay.jpg.title: 96 Columbia Jay
96_Columbia_Jay.jpg.description: Plate 96 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Columbia Jay.
96_Columbia_Jay.jpg.story: Audubon's Columbia Jay is actually a Mexican species, the Black-throated Magpie-Jay, with an extraordinary two-foot tail. He worked from a skin of uncertain provenance and placed it vaguely in the American West. Its inclusion in Birds of America is a reminder that specimen geography was often guesswork in the 1830s.

97_Little_Screech_Owl.jpg.title: 97 Little Screech Owl
97_Little_Screech_Owl.jpg.description: Plate 97 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Little Screech Owl.
97_Little_Screech_Owl.jpg.story: The Eastern Screech-Owl comes in two color morphs, gray and rufous, within the same brood; the ratio tilts toward rufous in the warmer south. Its trembling whinny carries through suburban woodlots, where it nests comfortably in backyard cavities. Audubon's plate shows both morphs for comparison.

98_White-bellied_Swallow.jpg.title: 98 White-bellied Swallow
98_White-bellied_Swallow.jpg.description: Plate 98 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting White-bellied Swallow.
98_White-bellied_Swallow.jpg.story: The Tree Swallow, the only swallow that can digest berries, a capacity that lets it winter as far north as Long Island on bayberry when insects disappear. It nests in tree cavities and readily adopts nest boxes. Audubon's plate captures the iridescent blue-green back that flashes bottle-glass in sunlight.

99_Cow-pen_Bird.jpg.title: 99 Cow-pen Bird
99_Cow-pen_Bird.jpg.description: Plate 99 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Cow-pen Bird.
99_Cow-pen_Bird.jpg.story: The Brown-headed Cowbird, America's most prolific brood parasite, laid eggs in some 220 host species and leaves the foster parents to raise its young at their own chicks' expense. It once followed bison herds across the prairie; now it follows cattle, expanding dramatically with land clearing since Audubon's time.

9_Selby's_Flycatcher.jpg.title: 9 Selby's Flycatcher
9_Selby's_Flycatcher.jpg.description: Plate 9 of Birds of America by John James Audubon depicting Selby's Flycatcher.
9_Selby's_Flycatcher.jpg.story: Named by Audubon for Prideaux John Selby, the Northumberland ornithologist whose Illustrations of British Ornithology he had studied during his English years. The species was later recognized as the Hooded Warbler in an unusual plumage or a related vireo. Selby, like the Rathbones, remained a steadfast patron of the folio.
